experiments.title: How removing a form disclaimer from a content offer landing page impacts sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the form disclaimer for 2026 Calendar landing page visitors will increase sign-up rates by eliminating unnecessary friction and reducing distractions, thereby preventing users from being drawn away from the form and allowing them to focus solely on completing their request.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2026 - 02/03/2026
Each year, IMB runs a Calendar acquisition offer that consistently performs well. The campaign follows a standard funnel: users land on a page to request the calendar, then are redirected to a donation page to complete their gift.
This year, we noticed a new addition to the landing page—a small form disclaimer that was not present last year. Although subtle, the change raised concerns about whether it might be creating friction and negatively impacting conversion.
To evaluate its effect, we ran an A/B test over a two-week period, splitting traffic evenly between the version with the disclaimer and the version without it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that removing the form disclaimer from the landing page for the 2026 Calendar acquisition offer led to a 10.1% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. This change was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating a clear impact on user behavior. Moving forward, it's important to remember that reducing unnecessary friction and distractions can improve conversion rates. By simplifying the user experience and allowing visitors to focus solely on completing their request, we can potentially see higher engagement and sign-up rates. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing different elements on the landing page to further optimize the conversion process. This could include testing different forms of messaging, images, layouts, or additional features to see what resonates best with users. By consistently running experiments and analyzing results, we can continue to improve the effectiveness of our campaigns and drive better outcomes for our nonprofit.

experiments.title: How a Dear Reader on Blog Pages Impacts Email and Donor Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader presenting a content offer for users on the Time of Grace blog and written devotion pages will increase email and donor acquisition, as it offers Time of Grace content to users who are already interested in and enjoy Time of Grace resources.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2024 - 08/26/2024
Time of Grace receives significant traffic to its blogs and written devotion pages. Our hypothesis was that this traffic left a huge opportunity to capture emails and donors by presenting traffic that we already know is interested in Time of Grace content, with an opportunity to receive a free content offer. At the end of this funnel, they would also then be presented with an ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: Key Learnings from this Experiment: The experiment showed that presenting a content offer to users on Time of Grace blog and written devotion pages resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition. This indicates that users who are already engaged with Time of Grace content are more likely to provide their email in exchange for additional resources. The experiment also demonstrated a substantial increase in transactions, showcasing the potential to convert these engaged users into donors, resulting in $802 in revenue from this additional passive stream of audience cultivation. This indicates that providing valuable content offerings can not only increase email acquisition but also boost conversion rates. Further, both the increase in email acquisition and transactions were statistically significant, underscoring the validity and reliability of the findings. Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the importance of providing valuable content offerings to an already engaged audience to boost email acquisition and drive transactions. By leveraging these insights and implementing them in future experimentation, Time of Grace Ministry can further enhance its outreach and impact on its audience.

experiments.title: Testing Form Friction: Will Requiring a Phone Number on a Content Offer Landing Page Hurt Sign-Ups from Facebook Traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that requiring a mobile phone number on the sign-up form for a Facebook video series acquisition offer will result in a small drop in conversions but more valuable contacts because motivated users will tolerate the added friction in exchange for high-value content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2025 - 11/11/2025
Focus on the Family has been working to grow their SMS program and wanted to test the impact of requiring a mobile phone number on a content sign-up form.
We decided to test this on a free video series titled Contagious Joy, which was being promoted on Facebook with acquisition ads. Visitors who clicked the ad would land on a sign-up page with a form asking for First Name, Last Name, and Email Address.
In the Treatment version of the page, a fourth required field—Mobile Phone Number—was added. All other elements of the page remained the same.
The test was set up as a split URL experiment, with 50% of traffic directed to the control version and 50% to the treatment.
This test aimed to answer a key question: How much friction can we introduce in the name of better data collection—without meaningfully hurting conversion rates?
We did expect a potential drop in sign-ups due to the added field but hypothesized that the value of acquiring phone numbers would outweigh the slight loss in volume.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results we saw were surprising. We had anticipated a slight drop in email conversions, but didn’t expect it to be as significant as it was. Requiring a phone number led to a 26.4% decrease in email sign-ups, with a 99.9% level of confidence—clear evidence that this added field created too much friction for users coming from Facebook ads.
We also saw an almost statistically significant 40% drop in transactions on the post-sign-up donation page (92.2% LOC), suggesting the impact extended beyond the initial form. While average gift amount and recurring donor conversion didn’t change, the decrease in overall donor volume is still meaningful.
For this acquisition audience, the added requirement of a phone number appeared to outweigh the perceived value of the free video series. This test shows that asking for too much, too soon—especially from new visitors—can hurt both lead generation and potential revenue.
Going forward, it may be more effective to collect phone numbers later in the journey such as on the donation page, once trust has been built or value has been delivered. Or test adding the Phone Number field on the sign-up page but do not make it required to try and reduce friction.

experiments.title: How a more detailed and benefit-focused landing page headline impacts the sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline for Talking Sense e-book landing page visitors will achieve an increase in the sign up rate because it clearly communicates both the value of the ebook and the immediacy of access, making it more appealing and actionable to the visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2025 - 02/21/2025
In an effort to increase sign-up rates on MPR’s Talking Sense e-book landing page, we conducted a headline test designed to more effectively communicate the value of the offer to visitors. Our goal was to determine whether a more explicit, benefit-driven headline would lead to higher engagement and conversions.
Control: Free E-book: Have Hard Political Conversations, Better
Treatment: Get instant access to this free e-book “Talking Sense” today to have better political conversations
*This test ran on both Talking Sense funnels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline for the Talking Sense e-book landing page resulted in a 5.9% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This increase was statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.5%, indicating that the treatment version outperformed the control. Based on these results, we recommend rolling out the improved headline to all traffic.

These findings reinforce that MPR’s audience engages more when the benefits are clearly articulated.

Furthermore, the treatment also led to a 12.9% increase in donations at a 67.1% LOC. While this metric did not meet our standard of 95% validity, it does suggest that the treatment motivated more visitors to make donations over the control.

Building on this success, our next tests will explore how refining the CTA and button clarity can further enhance conversions.

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins

rows.name: Remove Form Frame + Condensed Opt-In language/Preselect

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Today in the Word acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 07/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Today in the Word’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
Control: Stay grounded in your faith through God’s Word! Get our Daily Devotional and Bible-centered resources from Today in the Word! Sign Me Up!
Treatment: “I want to stay grounded in faith through God’s Word! Send me the Daily Devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the opt-in copy and removing the form frame on the Today in the Word acquisition landing page led to a significant decrease in opt-ins for all traffic, with a 46.8% decrease at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence. This indicates that the treatment did not resonate well with visitors and may have caused confusion or hesitation in opting in for Bible-centric content and resources from Moody Radio.
Furthermore, the experiment also showed a considerable decrease in One-Time Donations, Transactions, and Revenue, with decreases ranging from 758% to 764% at statistical confidence levels of 96% to 100%. This suggests that the changes made to the opt-in process not only impacted the opt-ins but also other key metrics related to donations and transactions.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of simplifying messaging and form layouts on user behavior and conversion rates. While streamlining the opt-in process may work in some cases, it is crucial to test and validate these changes thoroughly before implementation. Additionally, it is essential to prioritize user experience and ensure that any modifications to the acquisition funnels are user-friendly and clear to visitors to avoid any potential drop in engagement or conversions.

experiments.title: How an adding a relevant image on a petition landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad will increase the email acquisition rate because the image provides continuity for people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2025 - 05/29/2025
Mountain States Legal Foundation protects property rights and economic liberty, defends the right to keep and bear arms, champions free speech and association, and upholds the principle of equal protection.
We ran this test on an instant donation page from a petition. Audience: People from social media ads.
We wanted to know if more people will donate because people see an image on the ad and then the same image on the landing page. We thought this would provide continuity between the channels.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the same relevant image to a petition landing page that people saw on the social media ad did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions and recurring donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of image continuity between different channels. While the hypothesis was based on the idea that continuity could enhance the user experience and engagement, the results indicate that this may not always be the case.

Moving forward, it is crucial to test hypotheses thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. This can help avoid potential negative impacts on donation rates or other key metrics. Additionally, it is important to analyze the results objectively and be prepared to pivot strategies based on data-driven insights, even if they contradict initial assumptions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of experimentation and data analysis in optimizing donation pages and marketing strategies. It is essential to continuously learn from both successful and unsuccessful experiments to improve future campaigns and drive better outcomes for the nonprofit organization.

 	To further optimize the sign-up process, future experiments could explore different variations of the landing page design, such as testing the placement of the sign-up form, the design elements, or the messaging to find the ideal balance between user experience and business outcomes.
 	Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is the importance of balancing user experience improvements with the impact on key business metrics, and using data-driven insights to inform decision-making in future experimentation.

By leveraging the insights gained from this experiment, the organization can continue to enhance conversions, increase revenue, and ultimately provide a better experience for their audience.

experiments.title: How does highlighting the comment box impact comments left?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a highlighted comment box for visitors on an author profile will achieve an increase comments because it increases the visibility of the prompt and section.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2025 - 07/19/2025
On CaringBridge user profiles, visitors are prompted to leave a comment with a supportive data point. We hypothesized that by increasing the visibility of this section, with a blue highlight over the comment box, will increase the number of comments.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the comment box on user profiles led to a 3.6% increase in comments left by visitors. This result was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.6%. This indicates that increasing the visibility of the comment prompt can positively impact user engagement and interaction on the platform.

In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to enhance the visibility of interactive features on the platform. This could include experimenting with different colors, placement, or wording of prompts to further optimize user engagement. Additionally, it would be valuable to test the impact of highlighted comment boxes on other metrics such as return visits or user satisfaction to fully understand the overall impact on user experience. By consistently testing and analyzing different strategies for improving user interaction, CaringBridge can continue to enhance its platform and better support its users during challenging times.

experiments.title: How anonymity on a landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the option to send a note anonymously for Note of Encouragement landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it reduces the perceived pressure and lowers the barrier for participation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2025 - 06/30/2025
In an effort to increase the conversion rate on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested allowing users to mark their message as anonymous, allowing them to send their message without their name attached.
Our goal was that this treatment would allow visitors to feel more comfortable and confident submitting their encouraging notes, therefore leading to an increase in emails acquired on the page.
The treatment ran for about 2 weeks with half the traffic seeing the anonymous note check box.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the option to send a note anonymously on the Note of Encouragement landing page did not lead to an increase in email acquisition as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 19.3% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group, with a high statistical level of confidence at 98.1%.

Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changes to the user experience on conversion rates. While the idea of reducing perceived pressure and lowering barriers for participation seemed promising, the results show that anonymity may not always lead to increased engagement. These results indicate that the treatment added too much additional friction and not enough value to the visitors.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to thoroughly test and evaluate any proposed changes before fully implementing them. Additionally, conducting user testing and gathering feedback prior to launching an experiment can help uncover potential pitfalls and ensure that the changes align with the preferences and behaviors of the target audience.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to continually test and iterate on strategies to optimize conversion rates and user engagement.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 04/24/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A “Christian Lawyer” style design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run on the German petition pages (launching inline with their Stop Digital Euro Campaign) with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments. This result is parallel to what we saw when we ran this test on Hazteoir petition pages.

Results:
Control: 14,099 samples | 535 opt-in clicks | 6,707 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1: 14,274 samples | 1,272 opt-in clicks | 6,667 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2: 14,155 samples | 1,680 opt-in clicks | 6,808 email acquisitions
137.7%
213.7% increase
100% Level of Confidence (LoC)
Control conversion rate: ~47.6%
 	Treatment 1 conversion rate: ~46.7%
 	Treatment 2 conversion rate: ~48.1%
Recommendation:
Treatment 2

experiments.title: How adding an image of a well known individual impacts sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of Dr. Carson to the landing page for Read Across America visitors will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because it enhances the credibility of the pledge through his recognizable and trusted reputation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2025
In an effort to boost email acquisition on ACI’s Read Across America landing page, we opted to test adding an image to the landing page.
We typically include images of Dr. Carson on our landing pages. However, we haven’t ever tested this.
Control: No image
Treatment: Image of Dr. Ben Carson
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of Dr. Ben Carson to the landing page for Read Across America visitors did not result in an increase in sign-up rates. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease of 22.8% in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. This outcome was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.8%.
Moving forward, it is important to carefully consider the impact of visual elements such as images on landing pages. While the hypothesis assumed that Dr. Carson's recognizable and trusted reputation would enhance the credibility of the pledge and increase sign-up rates, the results demonstrated otherwise. It is crucial to test assumptions and hypotheses through experimentation to truly understand what resonates with the target audience.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing different strategies and elements on landing pages to optimize email acquisition. This could involve testing different images, content layouts, calls to action, or even testing the absence of images altogether. By consistently testing and analyzing results, we can gain valuable insights into what drives engagement and conversion rates, leading to more effective marketing strategies in the future.

experiments.title: How embedding an email sign up form onto the registration page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding the email sign up form to the course registration page will increase the email acquisition because it reduces the friction at the decision point.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2025 - 05/23/2025
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible.
We ran this test on the DTS Revelation Free Online Course registration page. The audience: People from social media ads.
We want to know if more people will sign up for the course if the form is embedded on the register page. The original version a person had to click a button to go to the sign up form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the email sign-up form directly to the course registration page significantly increased email acquisition for Dallas Theological Seminary. The treatment group experienced a 307.4% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the change had a substantial impact.

In future experimentation, Dallas Theological Seminary should consider simplifying the registration process by reducing the steps required for users to sign up. This can help reduce friction at the decision point and potentially lead to more conversions. Additionally, A/B testing different variations of the registration process, such as the placement of the form or the wording of the call-to-action, can provide valuable insights into what resonates best with the target audience.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of optimizing the user experience to make it as seamless as possible for individuals to engage with the organization's offerings, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates and increased participation in their educational programs.

This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.

experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week, and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments.

 	Control: 10,980 samples, 481 opt-in clicks, 5,599 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1 (Long): 11,262 samples, 1,094 opt-in clicks, 5,718 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): 11,195 samples, 1,387 opt-in clicks, 5,762 email acquisitions

The Treatment 1 (Long) increased opt-in clicks by 121.7% at a 100% Level of Confidence (LoC), while Treatment 2 (Christian) showed an even stronger increase of 182.8% at a 100% LoC. Both treatments outperformed the control, where the opt-in clicks were much lower.

Despite these significant increases in opt-in clicks, there was no impact on email acquisitions. This is fine, as the goal of the test was to increase consent to the terms (via checking the box) rather than directly driving email sign-ups. The conversion rates for email acquisitions were as follows:

 	Control: ~4.38%
 	Treatment 1 (Long): ~4.13%
 	Treatment 2 (Christian): ~4.17%

This indicates that while the treatments successfully increased opt-in clicks, they did not lead to a notable change in email sign-ups. Given the strong results from Treatment 2 (Christian), we recommend implementing this treatment across all petition pages. This approach had the highest increase in opt-in clicks and aligns with the goal of improving user engagement with the petition.

experiments.title: How does adding an exclusive offers slide-up to a landing page impact email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting users with additional video series options in a slide-up, they will be more likely to find content that resonates with them, which will lead to a higher email acquisition rate. By offering more choices, we expect to reduce friction in the decision-making process and increase the likelihood that users will opt in with their email addresses.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2025 - 03/24/2025
We hypothesized by providing visitors with alternative video series options, visitors would more likely to find content that resonates with them, thereby increasing the likelihood of signing up. The test was not about determining which series resonated more with people but rather whether offering additional options would lead to more email sign-ups.
To test this, we implemented an A/B test in VWO, splitting traffic evenly (50/50) between the Control and Treatment. The Control version of the landing page remained unchanged, while the Treatment featured a slide-up at the bottom of the page displaying three alternative video series options. We tested this on “Proof You Need,” their most successful video series we are running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran for 18 days, during which the control acquired 1,249 emails and the treatment acquired 1,155 emails. This resulted in a 6.8% decrease in email acquisition for the Treatment at a 95.6% level of confidence. While we initially hypothesized that offering more options would increase conversions, the results suggest that the slide-up may have unintentionally introduced decision friction. Users who arrived on the landing page were likely already primed to sign up based on the ad they clicked, but the presence of additional choices may have disrupted that intent, leading to hesitation rather than action. Instead of reinforcing their decision, the added options could have created uncertainty, ultimately reducing the overall conversion rate.

Based on these results, we recommend maintaining the current landing page design without the slide-up to avoid introducing additional friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How Meta creative with a landscape or a soldier impacted email acquisition and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that If we use a more emotionally engaging image that features a soldier making eye contact and smiling (Version B), for the Meta audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donations because the human-centered imagery creates a stronger emotional connection, conveys resilience and personal sacrifice, and draws the viewer into the narrative more effectively than a distant, abstract group scene..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 04/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of meta ad creative for one of their content offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. We wanted to test:
Image A: A group of soldiers hiking in silhouette, viewed from behind, ascending a hill.
VS.
Image B: A smiling, bandaged soldier holding a helmet with a bullet hole — direct eye contact with the viewer.
We wanted to test to see what the impact would be on email acquisition, donations, and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results Overview
Version A (Landscape Image)
Reach: 51,400Email Acquisitions: 516Cost per Email: $8.61Donations: 27Cost per Donation: $164.61Female Donors: $96.39 cost per donationMale Donors: $196.29 cost per donation
Version B (Soldier Image)
Reach: 51,900Email Acquisitions: 441Cost per Email: $10.08Donations: 28Cost per Donation: $158.79Female Donors: $162.07 cost per donationMale Donors: $155.99 cost per donation
Key Learnings:
Version A generated more email signups (516 vs. 441) and did so at a lower cost per acquisition. The clearer "FREE EBOOK" call to action and more spacious design likely made the value proposition more immediately apparent.Version B slightly outperformed in donation count (28 vs. 27) and had a slightly lower cost per donor. The emotional appeal and personal connection from the soldier’s face may have played a role in nudging more donors across the finish line.Creative tone influences outcome based on objective. Version A was more efficient for lead generation. Version B may have had marginally stronger performance for donor conversion, supporting the idea that emotional resonance matters more in the lower funnel.Creative resonance may vary by gender. Version A was significantly more effective at converting female donors, with a 40% lower cost per donation compared to Version B. Version B performed notably better with male donors, with a ~20% lower cost per donation than Version A...This suggests that creative resonance may vary by gender, with the soldier image potentially appealing more to men and the landscape image connecting more with women in this context.
Interpretation of Results:
Clarity beats emotion at the top of the funnel. The straightforward visual layout and “FREE EBOOK” message in Version A likely made it easier for users to understand what they were getting, which helped with email conversion.Emotion might matter more post-conversion. The slight lift in donations from Version B suggests the image's human connection and storytelling potential could increase the perceived significance of the offer, nudging users toward giving.Cost difference is minimal at the donation level. Though not statistically significant, the lower cost per donor in Version B is worth noting as a potential trend to explore further.
Next Testing Steps:
Combine the strengths of both creatives. Test a version that uses the emotionally compelling soldier image but adds back the “FREE EBOOK” call to action in bold, high-contrast text.Test additional human-centered imagery. Try other close-up image that evokes emotional engagement (e.g., generals, veterans, sacrifice) to validate the emotional-resonance hypothesis.Test gender-targeted creative variations. Run separate ad sets targeting men vs. women, using the image that performed better for each: Use Version A (landscape) for female audiences. Use Version B (soldier) for male audiences. Measure whether tailoring creative by gender improves overall cost efficiency.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity around where an email is being used on a landing page form affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity around where an email is being used for quiz takers will achieve an increase in conversion rate because users will feel more informed and confident about how their personal information is being used, reducing hesitation and fostering trust in the process, which leads to higher engagement and willingness to complete the download.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2024 - 10/01/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a quiz on Marxism through paid social and email prospecting. The control landing page didn’t address on the form where the email address the user submitted would be used, so Heritage wanted to test adding an enrollment option button to the form that allows users to opt in to communication from Heritage and HAFA.
They originally wanted to run this experiment to be able to capture emails who opted in to HAFA communications as well as Heritage.
This test ran for two weeks to 50% of the traffic to this landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the clarity around where an email is being used for survey takers did not achieve an increase in conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version, which included additional language about agreeing to terms and conditions and privacy policies, saw a significant 33.1% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control version with a 100% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of adding extra language or steps to the conversion process. While transparency and clarity are important, it is crucial to balance this with the potential for increasing friction and reducing conversion rates. It may be beneficial to test different variations of messaging or form options to find the right balance between providing information and maintaining a smooth user experience.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of continuously testing and optimizing messaging and form elements to ensure the highest conversion rates possible.

experiments.title: How an “Disagree” default answer for survey questions affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to “Disagree” in dropdown lists for survey answers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 04/15/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In a previous experiment, they wanted to know if using a dropdown for the answers instead of radio buttons would impact or increase email conversion rate, resulting in a decrease in email acquisitions. In an additional follow up experiment, they wanted to test whether assigning the default answer in the dropdown lists to “Agree” as opposed to a blank value or “Undecided” would result in this increase, but this experiment too led to a decrease.
To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Disagree” would encourage the survey taker, who likely would agree with the questions asked, to take action by answering survey questions and in turn increase email acquisitions.
The control experience showed the original radio button answers and the treatment experience showed the dropdown list answers with “Disagree” or “No” as the default.
The experiment ran for two weeks and each variant saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that defaulting to "Disagree" in dropdown lists for survey answers led to a significant 77.3% decrease in email acquisitions. This result suggests that the assumption that setting a negative default option would encourage action was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it's essential to carefully consider the impact of default options in survey questions. It is clear from this experiment that defaulting to a negative answer might discourage participants from engaging or taking action, resulting in a decrease in desired outcomes such as email acquisitions. Therefore, when designing future experiments, it would be beneficial to test different default options or even consider changing the dropdown lists back to radio buttons to see which approach yields the best results.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and hypotheses rigorously before implementing changes. It is crucial to analyze the potential impact of any alterations to the survey design thoroughly and be prepared to pivot based on the actual outcomes observed.

experiments.title: How an “Agree” default answer for survey questions affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to “Agree” in dropdown lists for survey answers will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/27/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running a survey in paid Facebook ads and through email prospecting. In a previous experiment, they wanted to know if using a dropdown for the answers instead of radio buttons would impact or increase email conversion rate, resulting in a decrease in email acquisitions. In an additional follow up experiment, they wanted to test whether assigning the default answer in the dropdown lists to “Undecided” as opposed to a blank value would result in this increase, but this experiment too led to a decrease of 32%.
To further test the dropdown list idea, they wanted to know if setting the default dropdown list answer to “Agree” would encourage the survey taker, who likely would agree with the questions asked, to submit the survey and increase email acquisitions.
The control experience showed the original radio button answers and the treatment experience showed the dropdown list answers with “Agree” or “Yes” as the default.
The experiment ran for one week and each variant saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that setting the default dropdown list answer to "Agree" did not achieve an increase in email conversion rate. In fact, the treatment resulted in a significant decrease of 87.6% in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control.

This indicates that simply defaulting to a positive response like "Agree" may not necessarily encourage survey takers to submit the survey or increase email acquisitions. It is essential to carefully consider all aspects of the survey design and wording, as well as understanding the preferences and behaviors of the target audience.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different variables and elements of the survey , including testing different wording for the default dropdown list answer, adjusting the layout or design of the survey, or even exploring other formats such as open text responses.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of thorough testing and the need to adapt strategies based on data-driven insights rather than assumptions.

These results are specific to desktop only, when we looked at mobile we saw a similar situation with a 14% decrease in emails acquired at a 90% LoC
Based on these findings, it is clear that the radical redesign did not achieve the intended outcome of increasing email conversations. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of significant design changes on user behavior and conversion rates. It may be beneficial to conduct user testing or smaller-scale experiments before implementing radical redesigns across all offers.

experiments.title: TW – What happens when we present an email sign up opportunity higher on the page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the email CTA bigger and easier to fill out for the homepage will achieve more emails acquired because it’s more visible .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/25/2025
Today in the Word is a ministry that offers daily devotionals delivered right to your email.
When looking at the Today in the Word site, we noticed that the only sign up for receive emails was in the footer. There is a CTA right below the hero image, but it was very small and easily to miss. With the main goal of this site being to get email address, we wondered what would happen if we made that CTA bigger and easier to fill out right on the page.
This test only showed on the homepage for new visitors
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. **Visibility is crucial**: By making the email CTA bigger and easier to fill out on the homepage, there was a significant increase in email acquisitions. This indicates that visibility plays a key role in encouraging visitors to take action.
2. **Simplify the process**: Making it easier for users to sign up for emails directly on the homepage likely reduced friction and made it more convenient for them to engage with the content.
3. **Data-driven decisions**: The significant increase in email acquisitions and the high level of statistical confidence in the results emphasize the importance of data-driven decision-making in optimizing website performance.
Moving forward, these learnings can be applied in future experimentation by:
1. **Prioritizing visibility**: Ensuring that important CTAs or conversion points are prominently displayed on the website to attract the attention of visitors and encourage action.
2. **Continuing to simplify**: Streamlining the user experience by simplifying processes such as email sign-ups can lead to higher engagement and conversion rates.
3. **Testing and iterating**: Building on the success of this experiment, it's important to continue testing variations to further optimize the website for conversions and user engagement. Regular experiments and A/B testing can help in refining strategies based on user behavior and preferences.

experiments.title: How form location on a survey landing page impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the form below the survey for MPR Listener Survey landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because participants will focus solely on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront, creating a more seamless and user-friendly experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/20/2025
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on MPR’s Listener Survey landing page, we opted to test into relocating the form to the bottom of the page.
Our hypothesis was that this change would reduce the friction happening at the top of the page therefore increasing the number of people who would complete the survey.
Control: form above the survey
Treatment: form below the survey
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the form below the survey on the MPR's Listener Survey landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition. The treatment version, where the form was placed at the bottom of the page, saw a 39.2% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic with a 99.1% statistical level of confidence, indicating that this change was highly effective in improving the user experience and increasing conversions and should be rolled out to all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of page layout on user behavior. Placing the form below the survey reduced the friction at the top of the page, allowing participants to focus on completing the survey first without feeling pressured to provide personal information upfront. This created a more seamless and user-friendly experience ultimately leading to a higher conversion rate.

Moving forward, we should continue to test different page layout elements, such as form placement, language, and design, to optimize the user experience and improve conversion rates. It is also important to analyze the results of each experiment carefully and draw actionable insights to inform future optimization strategies.

experiments.title: How a reputable quote impacts email conversion rate on a petition funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of Dr. Carson’s quote for Pro-Life Pledge landing page visiors will achieve an increase in email conversions because the quote establishes trust and credibility for the visitor leading to higher engagement on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2025
In an effort to boost the email conversion rate on ACI’s Pro-Life Petition landing page, we opted to test into adding a quote of Dr. Carson’s to the copy.
The quote read: “”I am committed to protecting innocent lives, especially when they are most vulnerable. The sanctity of life is something that we must wholeheartedly promote and enforce.” -Dr. Ben Carson”
Our hypothesis was that this addition would immediately establish trust and credibility for the visitor as Dr. Carson is a highly respected individual especially in the pro-life space.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of Dr. Ben Carson's quote to the Pro-Life Petition landing page did not lead to an increase in email conversions as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group showed a 5.0% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical significance of 98.9%. This suggests that the quote did not have the intended effect of establishing trust and credibility for visitors, leading to higher engagement on the page.

While these results did not validate in the direction that we anticipated, they do offer a learning into the motivations behind ACI's audience.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the landing page. While leveraging quotes from respected individuals can be a powerful tool, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging resonates with the target audience and aligns with the overall goals of the campaign. Additionally, it is important to continually test and iterate on different elements of the landing page to optimize conversion rates. This experiment highlights the importance of relying on data-driven insights to inform decision-making and the need to be open to adjusting strategies based on the results observed.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security statement at the bottom of the form for 2025 Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because it alleviates nerves about giving up personal information through our form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2025 - 02/24/2025
In an effort to boost our sign-up rate on IMB’s 2025 Calendar Landing Page, we decided to test into a security statement on the landing page.
The security statement read: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your calendar.”
Our hypothesis was that this statement would ease any nervousness of giving up personal information through our landing page form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. The addition of a security statement at the bottom of the form on the 2025 Calendar landing page led to a significant decrease in email acquisition rates.

2. The decrease in email acquisition suggests that the security statement may have inadvertently raised concerns or doubts among visitors, leading them to reconsider providing their personal information.

Future Experimentation:

1. When testing security statements or trust signals on landing pages, it is crucial to carefully consider the phrasing and placement of such statements to avoid creating unintended negative effects.

2. In future experiments, it may be beneficial to test different variations of security statements or phrasing to determine what resonates best with visitors and effectively boosts email acquisition rates.

3. It is essential to continuously monitor and analyze the impact of any changes made to landing pages to ensure that they are having the intended positive effect on visitor behavior.

4. Collecting feedback from visitors through surveys or interviews can provide valuable insights into their perceptions of security statements and help in optimizing their effectiveness in future experiments.

experiments.title: How including how many people downloaded impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a subheadline of how many have already downloaded the offer for Avila’s landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because people are influenced to download because they see others have done it too.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2025 - 02/05/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether adding the number of people who have downloaded this offer to the landing page headline would impact email acquisition and donations.
We wanted to see if including “Downloaded by 18,377 others!” in the subheadline would engage others. This is known as social proof marketing or the bandwagon effect, where people are influenced to take action because they see others doing it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the number of people who have downloaded the offer to the landing page headline had a negative impact on email acquisition for Avila Foundation. The treatment, which included the subheadline "Downloaded by 18,377 others!" resulted in a 24.5% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the effects of social proof marketing strategies such as the bandwagon effect. While showing the number of people who have engaged with a particular offer can sometimes increase engagement, in this case, it had the opposite effect. It is crucial to test different variations of social proof messaging and other marketing strategies to understand their impact fully.
People may not have signed up for the offer if the "Downloaded by 18,377 others" didn’t feel relevant to them or it wasn’t clear why they should care. Additionally, if something appears too common or widely available, it can sometimes lose its appeal.
It's important to note that the average gift size in the treatment was $52.50" whereas the control was $13.75. It would be interesting to dig in to see if this was based upon one large gift or if it was a trend.
It is essential to continue using A/B testing and data analysis to make informed decisions about website optimizations and marketing strategies. This experiment demonstrates the importance of testing hypotheses and not assuming that a particular approach will always yield positive results.

experiments.title: Does updating the cover for a content offer to be clearer affect email acquisition?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that updating the ebook cover on the landing page to a more designed and easy-to-read version would lead to higher email acquisition rates because the improved visual clarity and appeal would better engage users and encourage them to complete the form.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the SETI Institute, a nonprofit organization dedicated to scientific research and education in the search for life beyond Earth. The focus of this test was on the landing page, where the ebook cover image was updated from a photo (control) to a more designed and easy-to-read version (test). The goal was to evaluate whether the updated cover would improve email acquisition by enhancing the user experience on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Main Takeaway
The test version of the landing page, featuring the updated ebook cover, acquired 19% more emails than the control, with a confidence level of 99.2%. This result demonstrates that the more visually appealing cover design positively impacted user engagement and lead generation.
Observations:

 	The redesigned cover likely improved the user’s first impression of the landing page, making the offer more compelling and increasing trust in the content.
 	The consistent visual alignment between the ad and the landing page may have reinforced the user’s decision to engage with the form.

Next Steps

 	Adopt the New Cover Design: Implement the updated cover design on all landing pages to improve email acquisition rates across campaigns.
 	A/B Test Additional Landing Page Elements: Evaluate other design and content changes, such as CTA language, form placement, or page layout, to identify further optimization opportunities.
 	Monitor Long-Term Impact: Assess the quality of leads acquired through the updated landing page to ensure sustained performance improvements downstream.

experiments.title: How an aligned, value-driven image of an ebook on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using imagery that shows the content of an ebook for users visiting an ebook landing page will achieve an increase in email sign-ups because the imagery will showcase the value and ebook content more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/31/2024
During the Christmas season, Mission Aviation Fellowship (MAF) was running a devotional ebook acquisition offer. When running an offer like this, the landing page will typically feature a 3D mockup image of the ebook cover to help users on the page understand what they are receiving.
For this devotional offer, the ads on Facebook used a “scattered pages” design to showcase some of the unique offerings inside the ebook (devotions, Bible verses, crossword puzzles, etc.). We wondered if using this imagery on the devotional ebook landing page would help to enhance the value proposition and increase conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test for 29 days, with 50% of landing page traffic seeing the image of the 3D ebook, and the other 50% of traffic seeing the “scattered pages” design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing imagery that shows the content of an ebook on a landing page can significantly increase email sign-ups. In this case, the treatment version, which featured an image showcasing some of the unique offerings inside the ebook, led to a 35.5% increase in emails acquired compared to the control version that only showed the 3D ebook cover. This lift was at a 99.2% LOC, which means that the result is considered statistically valid.

In future experiments, it is important to continue testing imagery on the landing page that effectively showcases the value and content of the content offer. This can help capture the attention of users and communicate the benefits of the ebook more clearly, ultimately leading to higher conversion rates.

Further testing should also be done to determine the impact of aligning the imagery on the landing page with any imagery that users are seeing at the front of the funnel through ads, emails, MMS, etc.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown timer to an “update your membership information” landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 10-minute countdown timer for membership update page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because there is an increased sense of urgency to complete the form when given a set amount of time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign targeting donors who had not yet given a gift in 2024 to update their membership information, including name, email address, phone number, and address on a landing page. They were then redirected to a donation page where they could give a gift in order to renew their Heritage membership.
In an effort to increase email acquisition rate on the landing page, Heritage wanted to test whether adding a 10-minute countdown timer would increase the viewer’s sense of urgency and encourage them to fill out the form more often.
The control page did not have a countdown timer, and the treatment page had a 10-minute countdown timer that started when the viewer started the page session and ended when it reached 0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a 10-minute countdown timer to the membership update page did not achieve the desired increase in email acquisitions. In fact, the treatment page saw a significant and valid 11.5% decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control page. This indicates that the sense of urgency created by the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on the viewers, leading to a lower conversion rate. Viewers may have felt put off or pressured by the timer, or there may have been a disconnect in understanding why there was a limited timeframe to update their membership information.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of elements like countdown timers on user behavior. While urgency can sometimes be effective in driving action, it is crucial to test such elements to ensure they are positively influencing the desired outcome. Additionally, it is essential to analyze the results of experiments thoroughly before implementing changes across all marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How using direct, biblically-aligned language in a Facebook ebook ad impacted sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using direct, biblically-aligned language in the opening of our Facebook ads will increase email sign-ups for the ebook because it makes the offer feel more interesting, accessible, and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 10/28/2024
Luther Seminary was running Facebook ads to promote their free ebook about the book of Numbers. We wanted to test whether a shorter, clearer opening paragraph in the ad would lead to more engagement and email signups. Our theory was that the Control ad might scare users away because it used phrases like “daunting hike” and “endless desert”.
For the treatment language, we focused on getting the reader’s attention by referencing the content of the book of Numbers and using more biblically-aligned language to describe the journey of the people of Israel (“guided through the wilderness” vs the control that said “daunting hike…endless desert”).
Our hypothesis was that using direct, biblically-aligned language in our Facebook ads would increase email sign-ups for the ebook because:
It creates a vivid, authentic connection to scripture, 
Evokes a sense of divine guidance by mentioning God’s guidance, and 
Reduces anxiety by making the offer feel more accessible and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using direct, biblically-aligned language in the opening of our Facebook ads can increase email sign-ups for an biblical study guide ebook. The treatment ad, which focused on referencing the content of the book of Numbers and using clearer, more biblically-aligned language, led to a valid 22.1% in email sign-ups with a 99.2% level of confidence (LOC).
We also saw that the treatment ads drove a 225.5% increase in instant donations with a 97.4% level of confidence!
When thinking about the donor psychology behind these results, these are some possible reasons for why the treatment ads performed better:
Authentic Biblical Imagery: The use of traditional biblical language like "wilderness" and the reference to "walking alongside the people of Israel" resonated strongly with the target audience who are familiar with biblical narratives, appealing to those seeking a genuine, faith-based study experience.Divine Guidance and Protection: Mentioning "God guided them through the wilderness" taps into the core desire for divine guidance and protection, which adds to the spiritual significance and relevance.Accessibility and Curiosity: The simpler, more straightforward language reduces anxiety by making the offer feel more accessible. The phrase "walking alongside" suggests a less intimidating, observational role compared to the language in the control ad.
In future experimentation, Luther Seminary should continue to use direct, biblically-aligned language in their marketing materials to create a vivid, authentic connection to scripture, evoke a sense of divine guidance, and make the offer feel more accessible and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth. Additionally, since instant donations also saw a significant increase with the treatment language, it may be worthwhile to explore how this language can be incorporated into donation campaigns as well.

experiments.title: How using more vigorous copy that focuses on providing value to the reader in Facebook ads affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing value through more vigorous copy for Facebook ad recipients will achieve more email acquisition because it will motivate them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/23/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary used Facebook ads to promote their free online course How to Read the Bible Like a Seminary Professor. The objective of the ads was to acquire new email addresses. The original ad copy focused on the organization first. Would more vigorous ad copy that provided value to the reader result in an increase in motivation and email acquisition?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Providing value through more vigorous ad copy can significantly increase email acquisition rates. The treatment, which included more engaging and valuable ad copy, resulted in a 148.4% increase in emails acquired compared to the control group. This success was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 96.1%.

In future experimentation, it is essential to prioritize providing value to the audience in ad copy. By focusing on how the offering can benefit the reader and addressing their needs and interests, it is more likely to motivate them to take action, such as providing their email address. This approach can be applied to other marketing campaigns to improve results and increase the effectiveness of acquiring new leads or customers.

experiments.title: How removing imagery on a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the images for Avila’s landing page viewer will achieve an increase in email acquisition because less images will reduce friction and draw attention to the sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/25/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
They are currently running an offer, Spiritual Direction and the Discernment of Spirits. We decided to test removing all the images from their landing page to see if this increases conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing images from Avila's landing page led to a 20% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 100.0%. This means the control group, which had images on the landing page, performed better in terms of email sign-ups. This led to the treatment showing a 20% decrease in overall revenue. There was also a decrease in the number of transactions from 30 in the control group to 28 in the treatment group. However, this was not statistically valid, so this cannot be a learning but something to test further.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of visual elements such as images on user engagement and conversion rates. While the hypothesis that removing images would reduce friction and draw attention to the sign-up form seemed plausible, the results indicate the opposite effect.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different types of images, their placement, or the overall design of the landing page to optimize for conversions. Additionally, conducting user surveys or gathering qualitative feedback can provide insights into the preferences and behaviors of the target audience when interacting with the website.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and being open to unexpected results in order to make informed decisions for optimizing conversion rates in the future.

experiments.title: Will Adding the Form Fields to the Bottom of Articles Increase Sign Up Rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the Winsome Ways video series sign up form directly to the article pages for readers of FOTF’s pro-life article content will achieve an increase in email conversions because we removed a step in the sign up process and visitors could see just how quickly they could get something of value from their in-article experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/27/2024 - 09/23/2024
Focus on the Family has a very healthy amount of traffic going to their KMI article pages. Within those pages, they typically include 2 “go deeper” type resources. On the bottom of the page they use a universal CTA system that we used 1 of 6 within this test. By testing the pro-life KMI bottom of article CTA, we wanted to know if adding the video series content offer form for Sharing Pro-Life Views in Winsome Ways would out perform the typical image and click to a standard video series landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that by adding the Winsome Ways video series sign-up form directly to the pro-life article pages, we were able to significantly increase email acquisitions by 95.2% for all traffic. This result was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 99.9%.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore ways to streamline the user experience and reduce steps in the sign-up process. By directly offering valuable content within the article pages, we can capture the interest of visitors more effectively and increase conversions. Additionally, we should consider incorporating similar direct sign-up forms or offers on other relevant article pages to further enhance engagement and drive email acquisitions.

experiments.title: How adding an additional acquisition form field adds friction
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional form field for acquistion offers will achieve a lower conversion rate and a higher cost per email because adding an additional field presents another opportunity to add friction to the page, which is another place for users to abandon the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2024 - 05/24/2024
KCBI just launched a new radio station in Waco. Which lead to several brainstorming sessions trying to figure out how we can separate a DFW or general states listener from a Waco listener from the point of acquisition.
For this test, we wanted to see if adding a zip code form field on an acquisition offer could gain more information about our users, while not sacrificing too much cost on the front end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional form field for acquisition offers led to a significant decrease in email acquisition, with a 43.5% decrease in conversion rate for all traffic. This confirms the hypothesis that adding an extra field can increase friction on the page, causing more users to abandon the process.

Not only did it decrease conversion rate on the page, it increased the cost to acquire a new name by 57%.

experiments.title: How adding a video on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a video to the landing page for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because viewers will be more likely to engage and feel connected to the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/19/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their long-term offers, Dueling Populisms, we wanted to test whether adding a video that we were already running as a YouTube ad to the landing page would make an impact on email acquisition. We hypothesized that adding the video to the page would increase engagement and the viewer would be more likely to sign up for the offer and increase the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a video to the landing page for Hoover's audience actually resulted in a decrease in email acquisitions by 15.9% compared to the control group. This outcome was statistically significant with a 99.5% confidence level, indicating that the difference in email acquisitions between the two groups was unlikely due to random chance.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider that adding a video to the landing page may not always lead to the desired increase in engagement or conversions. It is essential to test different strategies and elements to see what resonates best with the target audience. Additionally, analyzing the impact of each change on user behavior is crucial before implementing it across the board. By continuing to test and iterate based on the results obtained, the Hoover Institution can optimize their campaigns and strategies for better performance in the future.

experiments.title: How adjusting a landing page form and image impacted the email acquisition rate

rows.name: Form Adjustments + New Layout + Image Left

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side for Prayer Calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because there will be less friction on the page and the visitor will perceive the form as less work since all content will be above the fold..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2024 - 08/20/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run an A/B/C split test with traffic equally split between each version.
Treatment 1 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout
Treatment 2 – relocated the landing page form the right hand side making the page look like a two column layout + making the image left aligned
Our hypothesis that the form relocation would make the landing page appear like a short experience for the user and the image adjustment seems small but the image is angled to face outward which we thought may have been adding unnecessary friction to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the form relocation and aligning the image to the left side of the Prayer Calendar landing page resulted in a significant increase in email acquisition rate. The treatment led to a 15.4% increase in email acquisition for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 95.2% meaning that these results are valid and should be rolled out to all traffic.
This result indicates that the hypothesis was correct in assuming that these adjustments would reduce friction on the page and make it appear as a shorter and easier experience for the user. Moving forward, it is important to consider the impact of design elements on user experience and to continue testing and iterating on these factors to optimize conversion rates.
It is important to note that this was technically a multivariate test as we changed both the form location and used a condensed version between the control and treatment 1. This was unintentional, but it is clear that the combination of the form location, condensed fields, and left alignment of the image increased the number of emails acquired which was the final goal.
Future experimentation should focus on further refining the layout and design of the landing page to continue improving the user experience and email acquisition rates. It is also beneficial to involve user feedback and conduct usability testing to gain insights into how visitors interact with the page. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can optimize our conversion funnels and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial to enhance authority impacted course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal, clarity, and authority through a testimonial for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 11/18/2022
In a previous experiment, we had seen that adding a personal quote from the professor on the course registration page increased sign-ups. We wanted to test this same hypothesis on additional courses to see if this is something that was specific to the previous course or specific quote or if this was a global concept that could be rolled out on all courses taught by the seminary’s president. For this course, we added a brief testimonial and a photo of the professor so potential course registrants can get a glimpse of additional clarity about who is teaching the course and how having social proof from a figure of authority would motivate visitors to give away their email address on the page. We split the traffic 50/50 to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered after running the experiment for one month was that the treatment lifted the conversion rate on the acquisition page by 7.4% with 97.2% confidence. Drilling down into specific segments, we did notice a directional decrease in conversion from paid social, which will need to be addressed. However, on its face we believe this treatment layout makes the course more appealing by humanizing the offer. Since we have now seen similar treatments provide lifts on various courses regardless of the course-specific testimonial content, we will add this feature to the existing layout of the course registration pages for those that are taught by the seminary president.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a daily devotional subscription impacts email conversion on the landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe strengthening the appeal and exclusivity of a daily devotional subscription will increase the email conversion on its landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/31/2023 - 08/07/2023
For Bible League Canada’s Words from the Heart daily devotional, we felt the Value Proposition was weaker than it should be. Particularly in the exclusivity and appeal it conveyed. The control page seemed to assume that visitors would already believe that they should be reading their Bibles each day, instead of reminding them why daily Bible reading may be important.
We hypothesized that the landing page’s value proposition could be strengthened by first articulating why a daily devotional is important and then explaining how this short, exclusive devotional would enable subscribers to experience the peace and presence of Christ. To do this, we added a scripture reference that encouraged believers to daily meditate on God’s Word. Then, we emphasized the negative things that can happen when believers don’t take time to follow this verse’s instruction and meditate on Scripture. Finally, we emphasized three separate times in the copy that this was an “exclusive 3-minute daily devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After just one full week, we were able to validate the impact of this strengthened value proposition. This test was a reminder that we shouldn't assume that our audience already understands the full value of an offer. Instead, we should take time to reinforce the need the offer meets as well as the exclusive ways it will benefit users. We will continue to test the value proposition on our other landing pages to ensure that we are providing greater appeal and exclusivity to visitors.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky follow button that “pins” to the top of the page for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it draws more attention to the button visually, but also highlights this as a primary call-to-action.

Timeframe: 07/26/2024 - 08/01/2024
On CaringBridge site profiles, we wanted to re-test a sticky follow button that stayed pinned to the page when a user scrolled down. This had previously been tested in Caring Bridge 1.o, but not yet in Caring Bridge 2.0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky follow button that stays pinned to the top of the page for users on a site profile led to a significant increase in site follows. The treatment version, which included the sticky follow button resulted in a 14.7% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

A takeaway from this experiment is more than just making an element more visually apparent, but what that communicates to the reader. Of all the actions that can be taken on a site profile, we're highlighting this is one that's important.

This strategy can be applied to other features or buttons on the site to capture users' attention and encourage them to take the desired actions. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on design elements, language choices, and user experience to optimize engagement and achieve the desired outcomes.
