experiments.title: How a landing page redesign impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a landing page redesign for content offer viewers from acquisition will achieve the same email conversion rate because the landing page messaging and experience will mostly stay the same.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 08/05/2024
With the launch of a new website, we considered moving landing page platforms but wanted to get the data before deciding to transition in case it impacted results. We built a treatment in the new platform and compared how visitors responded in comparison to the old platform. The treatment kept all of the same copy from the control, but had some differences in formatting which may have impacted results. We ran the two versions side by side and split traffic for two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesign of the landing page for content offer viewers from acquisition led to a significant decrease in email acquisition. The treatment performed 32.3% worse than the control, with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the original landing page outperformed the redesigned version. We believe this is due to a few distinct differences in the user experience on the page, including checkboxes, width of form fields, presentation of headline, formatting of page copy, and color and copy on the CTA button. While on the surface the pages were essentially the same, these slight changes in design made a significant impact on results and reduced email conversion.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to landing pages, even if the messaging and content remain the same. Small differences in formatting and design can have significant effects on user behavior and conversion rates. It is also essential to thoroughly test any new designs before fully implementing them, to avoid negative impacts on conversion rates. Additionally, considering running qualitative research such as user testing and surveys in addition to A/B testing to gather more insights into how users are interacting with the landing page and to understand their preferences and behavior better.

experiments.title: How providing additional value prop on a content offer impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more value prop in the ad copy for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is additional appeal and clarity on the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV utilizes Facebook as a platform to acquire new names and donors for their organization.
One offer that has been a consistently well-performing content offer is their Jesus Map digital download.
The current version of the ad has been running for multiple years and had not been optimized.
We decided to test out expanding the copy so that there is more information regarding the offer and the reasons to request it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Including more value proposition in the ad copy is effective**: The treatment with expanded ad copy led to a significant increase in Complete Registrations, Clicks, and improvements in Cost per Click and Cost per Registration. This indicates that providing more information and clarity on the offer can attract more viewers and increase conversions.

2. **Audience segmentation is crucial**: The experiment revealed significant increases in Complete Registrations across different visitor segments, such as 65+ visitors, Female visitors, Mobile visitors, and Tablet visitors. This highlights the importance of tailoring messaging and targeting specific audience segments to maximize engagement and conversions.

3. **Statistical significance and confidence levels**: The high confidence levels, especially at 100% for Clicks, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration, indicate that the results are reliable and not due to chance. This reinforces the validity and impact of the experiment's findings.

To apply these learnings in future experimentation, we should:

1. **Continue to test and optimize ad copy**: Based on the success of the expanded ad copy in this experiment, we should continue exploring ways to enhance value proposition and clarity in our messaging. This could involve testing different variations of ad copy to identify the most effective messaging strategies.

2. **Segment and target specific audience groups**: Building on the positive outcomes seen across different visitor segments, we should further analyze audience data to understand preferences and behaviors. By tailoring content and targeting specific groups, we can drive higher engagement and conversions.

3. **Maintain statistical rigor in experimentation**: To ensure the reliability of results, we should continue to prioritize statistical significance and confidence levels in future experiments. This will help us make data-driven decisions and confidently implement successful strategies.

Overall, by leveraging the learnings from this experiment, we can optimize our digital marketing efforts, enhance audience engagement, and ultimately drive greater success in acquiring new names and donors for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How using a ‘reverse funnel’ design that places the form above the fold affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving an email acquisition form above the fold will increase sign-up rates by capitalizing on the momentum created by earlier steps in the funnel and giving the user the option to read more if they choose.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
If we’re being honest, we don’t think a prospect should have to read several paragraphs of copy to see the value in exchanging their email address for a content offer. It’s generally accepted that 8 out of 10 people will read your headline/subheadline and nothing else, which means your “above-the-fold” copy is, without a doubt, far and away, the most important copy on the page.
Knowing this and working forward from the assumption that someone had to land on our landing page from somewhere — presumably some form of marketing communication that has already, at least, teased the offer …
We wanted to test what would happen if we seized the existing funnel momentum to drive the conversion by placing the form above the fold (directly beneath the headline and subheadline) and moving the ‘explainer’ copy down the page where a user could scroll to read if they so desired.
The treatment kept the headline and subhead copy unchanged and simply moved the body copy ‘below the fold’ where people could continue reading if they wanted to know more about the offer.
**Important note: although they do not show up in the renders below, both the control and the treatment featured 3D renders of the asset being promoted in the upper righthand space of the page
experiments.keyLearnings:
Moving the form above the fold resulted in a statistically valid 56.3% lift in email acquisition rates.
By giving people the option to 'fast-track' the download process, we were able to see more conversions. Importantly, this test is in no way meant to suggest that 'less copy is better.' We did not remove the copy; we simply moved it down the page so that people could forego additional reading if they were already 'sold.'
What this test does show is that your headline and subhead should be strong enough to carry the conversion on their own and that placing the form above the fold may create a more frictionless experience for enough users to make a significant difference in conversion rates.
It also shows the importance of analyzing each asset on its own and as part of the broader funnel. Avoid repeating the same message in your ad, email, landing page, donation, page, etc. Consider the momentum your other awesome assets have already created and capitalize on that to drive the conversion!
In future experimentation, we will focus on testing the headline, subhead, button copy, and any other above-the-fold elements, as this test shows that 'above-the-fold optimization' likely has an outsized impact on conversion rates

experiments.title: How including specific examples of what a person will learn from an eBook on a landing page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including specific examples of what a person will learn from an eBook on a landing page will increase the conversion rate because it provides more context and they have more information about whether they are interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2024 - 06/17/2024
In an effort to increase the email sign-up rate on an ebook acquisition page, The Heritage Foundation ran an experiment that highlighted specific examples of what a person will learn from this ebook on the landing page.
They believed that it will provide more context and give the user more information to the contents of the ebook which will make them more interested in the offer.
This experiment was split 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including specific examples of what a person will learn from an ebook on a landing page resulted in a 19.1% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic. This decrease was statistically significant at a 98.4% confidence level, indicating that the control group, without the specific examples highlighted, had a higher conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the impact of providing too much information upfront on a landing page. It is possible that overwhelming visitors with specific examples may have dissuaded them from signing up for the ebook. It could be beneficial to test different levels of detail and types of information provided on the landing page to find the right balance that entices visitors to take the desired action.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing hypotheses before implementing changes permanently and the value of continuous experimentation to optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that additional copy and a brighter button color for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it will decrease friction in the form submission process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase the email conversion rate on a landing page for a border crisis eBook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding body copy explaining what the eBook will help the reader learn, as well as changing the form’s button color, would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control page had three paragraphs of copy and a blue form submit button, and the treatment page had five paragraphs of copy including a more in-depth explanation of the value the reader will receive and an orange button.
This split URL test ran for 13 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment with shorter copy and a brighter button color for the eBook offer did not lead to an increase in email conversion rate. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 6.5% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control. With a statistical confidence level of 96.0%, it can be concluded that the control page performed better in terms of email acquisition.

The

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully evaluate the changes being made to the landing page. While the hypothesis suggested that shorter copy and a brighter button color would decrease friction in the form submission process and increase conversions, the results showed otherwise. It is essential to test hypotheses thoroughly and be open to unexpected outcomes.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further investigate the factors that influenced the lower email conversion rate in the treatment. This could involve conducting surveys or collecting feedback from users to understand their preferences and behaviors. Additionally, conducting more A/B tests with different variations can help in identifying the most effective elements for improving email acquisition on the landing page.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to carefully analyze results to inform future experimentation strategies.

experiments.title: How using a ‘pattern interrupt’ in a paid ad affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts will produce higher acquisition rates and lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA—despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 3 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
The simple design did not distract from the value of the offer itself
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to snatch the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test we pitted our usual control —3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field—against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.
The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 1786.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 5556.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.1946.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90
This suggests that by using a creative approach to funnel design, one that subtly blends the ordinary with the unexpected, we can entice prospects to engage by creating an experience beyond just "showing them an ad."
The key to the success of this concept, we believe, is presenting everyday design elements and  language outside of their expected context, using day-to-day, non-promotional urgency, to stoke curiosity and build momentum through to the landing page
What's interesting about this test is that if you stop to think about the treatment logically, it doesn't make any sense. But that does not mean that its "illogical" construction was unintentional. The incongruity of the presentation, we hypothesize, plays a large part in piquing the prospect's interest and pulling them into the funnel.
In future experimentation, we should continue exploring the surprising juxtapositions of elements. It would be interesting to test this concept throughout the entire funnel.
And while we are intrigued and inspired to keep testing this concept, we are also aware of the potential for "fatigue" with the specific application we used in this test. Therefore, while we encourage the "note app" treatment for low-investment to test the concept, and will try it with a few different offers, we believe we will eventually need to test this concept using different tactics and applications.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a landing page headline and copy impacted sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to the headline and the copy for site visitors will achieve a higher sign-up rate because it will better communicate the value they will receive by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
In an effort to increase our sign-up rate on the landing page of this Christmas Cards offer, we wanted to test adding clarity to both the headline and the copy. In Variation 1, we tested the new headline by itself. In Variation 2, we tested the new headline and the new call to action above the sign-up form.
The new headline reads:  Get your free scripture-infused Christmas Cards
The additional copy reads: Simply complete this form to request your free Christmas cards
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to the headline and call to action button on the landing page resulted in a decrease in email acquisition, compared to the control group. Variation 1, only testing the headline, resulted in a 2.4 drop with 19.5% level of confidence. Variation 2, testing the new headline and the statement above the form, resulted in a 31.2 drop in email acquisition with a 99.9% level of confidence.
On the surface, this suggests that the changes made did not resonate with site visitors and may have made the offer less appealing which is why we saw the drop in email acquisition.
It is important to note that this landing page experienced issues with this form after this test was launched. As well as, this experiment only ran for four days which could have heavily impacted our results. Therefore, we plan to retest including clearer language on landing pages, in hopes that we will see an increase in emails acquired.

experiments.title: How donor first party data through AVID Pathways impacted Facebook match rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that AVID Pathways connection  for AVID clients will achieve a higher match rate in Facebook because AVID has access to multiple sources of data. because  AVID has access to multiple sources of data.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
With the introduction of AVID Pathways, we decided to test the Facebook match rate of contacts uploaded via CSV versus that of a continuous AVID Pathways connection.
For the control of this experiment, we exported donors from the organization’s CRM and uploaded the list to Facebook as an audience. The data points provided included first name, last name, email address, phone number, city, state, and zip code.
For the treatment of this experiment, we created a segment of donors in AVID Pathways and then pushed that segment into Facebook as an audience. The AVID Pathways connection to Facebook is a continuous flow of information from the organization’s platforms, such as CRM, email, and donation platforms combined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing AVID Pathways to Facebook as an audience resulted in a significantly higher match rate compared to uploading donor information via CSV. The AVID Pathways connection saw a 45.6% increase in match rate for all segments, with a 100.0% statistical confidence level, indicating a clear win.
For this experiment, we utilized 4 different segments of donors based on the giving cohort:
multichannel donorsoffline only donorsoffline with email donorsonline only donors
For each segment, we saw the following results.
For multichannel donors, donors who have donated through online and offline channels, we saw a 5.5% decrease in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For offline-only donors, we saw an increase of 144.8% in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For offline with email donors, we saw an increase of 34.8% in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For online donors, we saw an 8.3% decrease in match rate at a 100% level of confidence.
While a couple of cohorts did see decreases in match rates, we believe that the AVID Pathways continuous connection will more than compensate for the fewer matches. In addition, we anticipate that AVID Pathways data will continue to grow in knowledge and data points as more systems are connected and data points are expanded.
For this organization, the most surprising cohort result is for offline-only donors. These donors do not have email addresses on file with the organization and, therefore, have fewer data points than the other cohorts. However, even with that missing point, we saw a significant increase in the match rate, which means we will be able to retarget these donors with Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How a question headline on a landing page affects the conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: A question headline on a landing page will increase the conversion rate because it creates curiosity and people will want to sign up to find out the answer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 06/04/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for an eBook on the state of the U.S. Military. We saw an opportunity to raise email conversion rate on the eBook offer’s landing page, so we wondered whether adding a headline that poses a question that the eBook answers would lead to an increase in conversion rate due to creating curiosity in the mind of the reader.
For this experiment, the control’s headline read: “Learn About the Current State of the U.S. Military — And What That Could Mean for Our Nation’s Future”
The treatment’s headline read: “War Is Raging—But How Prepared Is the U.S. Military if They Are Needed?”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split redirect test and ultimately ran for 20 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the control, which had a headline stating the topic directly without posing a question, outperformed the treatment, which featured a question headline. The treatment received a 23.7% decrease in email conversion rate at a valid 95.7% level of confidence compared to the control. This result indicates that in this particular case, the straightforward approach was more effective in capturing the audience's attention and prompting them to sign up for the eBook offer.

Downstream, the treatment saw a 9.5% increase in donor conversion rate, but at a 17% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember that different tactics may work better depending on the context and the audience. While posing a question can create curiosity, it is essential to ensure that the question resonates with the target audience and clearly communicates the value proposition. It may be beneficial to test an even more straightforward and clear headline that states the eBook's title.

experiments.title: How a benefit-focused landing page headline affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a headline on a landing page that focuses on the benefits a supporter receives will increase the conversion rate because they will quickly understand the value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 05/21/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for their Election Fraud eBook content offer. In an attempt to raise email conversion for visitors to the landing page, we wanted to test whether a headline that was more focused on the benefit viewers would get from reading the eBook would achieve this outcome.
The control headline read, “Get your free “5 Shocking Cases of Election Fraud” eBook from The Heritage Foundation.”
The treatment headline read, “Uncover the Hidden World of Election Fraud and Protect Your American Freedom.”
We set this up as a 50/50 split A/B test and ran for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment headline, which focused on the benefits of reading the eBook, received a lower amount of conversions than the control headline. The treatment resulted in a 34.3% decrease in email acquisitions for All Traffic with a 99.9% level of statistical confidence. This also resulted in a valid lower donation rate downstream for the treatment, with a 57.3% decrease in transactions at 99.9% level of confidence.

Reasons for this result could include the treatment headline sacrificing clarity in order to illustrate the benefits the viewer could receive from this content. The headline "Uncover the Hidden World of Election Fraud and Protect Your American Freedom" doesn't clearly illustrate what the content offer is upon reading it, which may dissuade viewers from reading the page further and making a decision to download the resource.

Moving forward, it is essential to consider that a headline focused on the specific offer may be more effective in driving conversions compared to a headline that emphasizes the benefits. It is crucial to clearly communicate the value proposition of the offer to the audience to encourage them to take action.

experiments.title: How including examples from the eBook on a landing page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including examples of cases from the eBook on the sign-up landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will have more context about the E-book to decide if they want to download it
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 06/03/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running a campaign for an eBook on election fraud. In an attempt to raise conversion rate on the eBook’s landing page, we wondered if adding body copy with examples of cases from the eBook would persuade more people to download the eBook if they had more context as to what the offer contained.
We tested the control against the treatment which added the following copy:
“You’ll learn about a Judge of Elections who accepted bribes, another individual who devised a ballot harvesting scheme, and more. In this eBook, you’ll also learn about the consequences of these crimes against American liberty.”
The control and treatment each saw 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding examples of cases from the eBook on the landing page did not increase the conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the added body copy resulted in a 21.9% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control version. This suggests that the additional information provided in the treatment did not persuade more people to download the eBook.

Viewers may have thought the additional copy in the treatment "spoiled" two of the advertised five cases of Election Fraud or took the mystery out of the eBook and dissuaded them from following through to download the offer.

Future experimentation could include rewriting the page's copy to be more accessible to a wider audience through a lower reading level.

Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Pathway Magazine is a digital content offer that PTV offers as a new name acquisition effort. We began to see a decrease in performance for this offer in acquiring name so we decided to run a test on the ad creative.
The control creative is a colorful image with a cell phone mock up of the digital offer along with bullet points of value that the offer contains.
The treatment creative is a mock up of a iPhone notes page with the bullet points of value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Changing the image format to a notes list of value points resulted in a significant increase in email sign-up rate.
2. The treatment creative, with the notes list of value points, was more impactful in acquiring names compared to the control creative with a colorful image and bullet points.
3. The statistical significance at a 99.6% level indicates a high level of confidence in the results.
In future experimentation, we should consider the following:
1. The format and presentation of the offer play a crucial role in acquiring names. A more straightforward and value-focused approach, such as a notes list, may be more effective than a colorful image with bullet points.
2. Testing different visual formats and content layouts can help identify the most effective design for acquiring names.
3. Continuously monitoring and analyzing the performance of different creatives will help optimize future campaigns and maximize results.
Overall, the success of the treatment creative in this experiment highlights the importance of testing and refining creative elements to enhance performance in name acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How removing an eBook from Facebook creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that substituting the 3D eBook with a scientific image will lead to increased registration rates, as it is expected to captivate the attention of the targeted demographic more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/28/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of a non-profit organization specializing in scientific research, education, and charitable initiatives. The test was carried out as a paid social media advertising campaign on Facebook, comparing the effectiveness of two different creative images. Our objective was to ascertain whether the target audience of this non-profit organization would respond more favorably to a 3D image of an eBook or a photograph. The purpose of this experiment was to determine whether adjusting the creative approach could enhance audience engagement, ultimately leading to a higher rate of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that the treatment, which involved using a scientific image instead of a 3D eBook image, significantly increased registration rates across various segments of the target audience. The treatment resulted in a 247.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 100.0% level of confidence.

In addition, the experiment also showed significant decreases in Cost per Click (78%) and Cost per Registration (243%) with high levels of confidence, indicating that the treatment not only attracted more registrations but did so more cost-effectively.

Moving forward, the learnings from this experiment suggest that utilizing scientific images may be more effective in capturing the attention and interest of the target audience for the SETI Institute. Future experimentation should focus on leveraging visually engaging scientific images to drive higher engagement and conversion rates. It is important to continue testing different visual creatives to identify the most effective approach for engaging the audience and achieving the organization's objectives.

experiments.title: How does a button on a slide-out impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that incorporating the form directly into the slideout will streamline the process, reducing friction and the number of steps required, potentially resulting in a higher number of sign-ups. Conversely, a slideout without a form may decrease the perceived commitment, thereby encouraging more sign-ups after users have gained additional information on the landing page
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
In an effort to enhance email acquisitions for a content offer, an experiment was conducted for WUNC, a non-profit news radio station. This experiment was tested on the homepage of their website to determine whether prospects are more likely to sign up for an offer directly within a slideout or by being redirected to a landing page. The aim is to gain insights into the most effective strategy for maximizing email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which included a link to direct users to a separate landing page, resulted in a 56.5% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to an embedded sign-up form.
2. By requiring users to navigate to a separate landing page to provide their information, the treatment potentially decreased the perceived commitment, leading to more sign-ups. This suggests that after users have gained additional information on the landing page, they are more likely to sign up for a content offer
3. The results of the experiment showed a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the increase in email acquisitions was not due to chance.
The key learnings from this experiment can guide decision-making in future experiments. It is recommended to continue testing different sign-up processes to further optimize email acquisitions. Additionally, user convenience and reducing perceived commitment can be important factors to consider when designing and implementing sign-up forms on websites.

experiments.title: How moving a headline with value proposition on a landing page impacts course sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the headline with value proposition up for the landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases clarity of what the person will learn when they sign up for the course and reduces friction. because it increases clarity of what the person will learn when they sign up for the course and reduces friction.

Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/19/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary has multiple free online courses that they use to acquire new names for their email list.
Evangelism has been a popular course that they have been advertising for years. When looking at all of the landing pages separately, we noticed that some landing pages had headlines “above the fold” (visible before someone has to scroll on the screen) while others did not. Evangelism had no headline above the fold.
In a follow up to this test, we decided to test again moving the headline with value proposition up higher on the landing page. One thing we did do differently this time is that the only thing we changed was the placement. We did not add any additional language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the headline with the value proposition up on the landing page led to a significant increase in email conversions for Dallas Theological Seminary's Evangelism course. This change increased clarity for visitors about what they would learn from the course, ultimately reducing friction and prompting more sign-ups.

In the future, Dallas Theological Seminary should consider the importance of positioning critical information, such as a value proposition, prominently on their landing pages to improve conversion rates. This experiment underscores the impact that small changes in layout can have on user behavior and reinforces the significance of clarity and reducing friction in driving conversions. Building on this learning, future experimentation should continue to focus on optimizing the placement of key elements on landing pages to maximize engagement and drive desired actions from visitors.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a landing page headline impacts course sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the value proposition up higher for the course landing page will achieve an increase in course sign ups because it increases the clarity of what the reader will get out of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2024 - 01/26/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary has a number of online courses that they use to acquire new names for their email list. Their latest course on 1 & 2 Peter was experiencing a low sign-up rate.
After looking at all of their most popular courses, we uncovered inconsistencies in the headlines. 1 & 2 Peter named the course at the top of the page, but after scrolling, there is a secondary headline featuring copy that appeared to have a much stronger value proposition, explaining what the reader will get if they sign up for the course.
So for this test, we decided to switch the headlines to see if it would impact overall course sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the value proposition higher on the course landing page resulted in a significant increase in course sign-ups. The treatment group, which had the value proposition moved up, saw a 32.2% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group. This increase was statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.6%.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the placement of the value proposition plays a crucial role in attracting potential learners and convincing them to sign up for the course. Moving forward, it is recommended to prioritize highlighting the benefits and value of the course at the top of the page to increase clarity and remove any potential friction that may prevent sign-ups.
Further experimentation can be done to optimize the messaging and design of the value proposition. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the value proposition to determine the most effective wording or format that resonates with the target audience. Continuous A/B testing can help refine and improve the course landing page to maximize email acquisition and overall conversions.

experiments.title: How a fullwidth, plain-text email affects click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send will achieve an increase in email clicks because the email will seem more organic, personal, and less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2023 - 11/04/2023
Our weekly email sends have been the same format for quite a while. We tested into using plain text format to create a more human-looking email a while ago and were curious if we could take it one step further to  seem even more organic. Typically our email text is centered so that when it’s opened it’s still quite obvious it’s a bulk send, not a one-off email.
The only thing change in the treatment of the email is the format. The copy, subject line, sender, etc. all stayed the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a full-width text format for our weekly email send can significantly increase the number of clicks. The treatment group, which received the full-width text format, experienced a 27.6% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group.
This indicates that the full-width text format created the perception that the email was sent straight from Gmail rather than a bulk send. This likely caught the attention of recipients and encouraged them to click on the links within the email.
It is important to note that the treatment showed a significant 214% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This means that even though the treatment group had higher click-through rates, fewer people actually took the step to open the email. The treatment would have no impact on this though since the test was run on the email layout, not the content or email "envelope."
To use this learning in future experimentation, we might want to explore different formats that combine the organic feel of the full-width text with elements that entice recipients to open the email.
For example, we could experiment with subject lines that highlight the content or benefits of the email, as well as using personalization techniques to make the email feel even more tailored to each recipient.
Overall, this experiment has shown that the format of our weekly email can have a significant impact on recipient engagement. By continuing to test and iterate on different formats, we can improve our email performance and ultimately achieve our goal of increasing click-through rates while maintaining a high open rate.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using this personalized slide out approach for new web visitors. experiments.title: How using organization credibility language on a survey page affects email sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including background information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson on the landing page will increase the percentage of survey takers because it will provide a sense of credibility and authority for visitors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2023
ACI is a conservative organization that is guided by the four cornerstones: life, liberty, faith and community. They were running a survey to address the state of religious liberty in America and were driving paid Facebook traffic toward this offer. Once the user completes the survey, they will be brought to a donation page asking them to donate to support the organization and their efforts.
In this experiment, we wanted to test how adding more language around who Dr. Ben Carson is and what ACI is doing to protect individual liberties would affect the survey completion rate. The additional language gives more context about how the survey relates to the goals of ACI and provides more context about Dr. Ben Carson’s beliefs. Rather than asking the user directly if they are concerned about religious liberty in this country, we added language that allows the user to align their beliefs with both ACI and Dr. Ben Carson.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding background information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson on the landing page did not increase the percentage of survey takers. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease of 10.9% in email acquisition with a 99.9% level of confidence compared to the control group.
This suggests that providing additional information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson may not necessarily enhance credibility and authority for visitors. It is possible that the additional information may have overwhelmed or distracted the visitors, leading to a lower completion rate.
In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to focus on streamlining the landing page and making the survey process more straightforward and engaging. Rather than relying on extensive background information, it may be more effective to highlight the value and relevance of the survey to the visitors, and clearly communicate the purpose and potential impact of their participation.
Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the qualitative feedback from the survey takers to identify any specific pain points or areas of improvement in the survey experience. This can help refine the survey process and increase completion rates in future experiments.

experiments.title: How an ultra-personal, interruptive ad format affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ultra-personal format in an unfamiliar context will produce higher acquisition rates and a lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA — despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 2 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to grab the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test, we pitted our usual control — a 3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field — against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.

The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 17
 	86.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%
 	87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 55
 	56.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.19
 	46.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90

experiments.title: How an exit intent pop up affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup offering a free ebook for users exiting a page that advertises a for-purchase item will achieve an increased acquisition of email because we believe that targeting the same motivation (scripture memory) at a free price point will acquire those who were exiting because of the price.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2023 - 03/30/2023
The Navigators’ Topical Memory System is a for-purchase book that helps the reader memorize scripture. The landing page for this offer briefly outlines what the TMS is, important points, and an ask at the end to purchase the book. We hypothesized that by adding an exit intent pop-up on the page for a free eBook on a similar topic (How to Memorize Scripture) would not interfere with those who are purchasing the TMS book, but would acquire the emails of those who were planning to exit the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a free ebook with an exit-intent popup can significantly increase email acquisition. The treatment group saw a 147.9% increase in email acquisition for all traffic, and new visitors, organic search visitors, desktop visitors, social visitors, male visitors, and visitors aged 18-24 saw substantial increases in email acquisition with a high level of confidence.
What does this tell us about users exiting the page? The eBook popup is about the same topic as the TMS page—but where the TMS page is urging users to make a purchase, the popup entices them with a similar offer free of cost. Because this popup is only setup to appear when the user goes to exit the page, this wouldn't detract from those who were going to purchase the book. What it does accomplish is allow The Navigators to acquire the email of someone who would've exited the page and been lost.

experiments.title: How an organic style ad impacted conversions on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a Facebook ad that looks like an organic Facebook post for potential pledge signers will result in an increase in email conversion because the ad comes across more like a message from a human and less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/19/2024
When promoting a pledge asking people to add their signature of support, we wondered if we could increase click through rate by developing a treatment ad that looked more like a personal request and less like an organization-centric marketing ad. Historically, we’ve seen a higher response rate this style of ad improve CPC and CTR in previous tests.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a Facebook ad that resembles an organic Facebook post, rather than a traditional marketing ad, can significantly increase email conversions. The treatment ad, which looked more like a personal message, resulted in a 43.6% increase in emails acquired compared to the control ad. This improvement was statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on this finding, it is recommended to continue using the treatment ad format in future experiments and campaigns. By creating ads that mimic the style of a personal message, the organization can enhance its engagement with potential pledge signers and increase the likelihood of conversion. It is important to prioritize a human-centered approach in ad design and make the content feel more relatable and less promotional.

With almost identical spend on each version, clickthrough rate on the treatment version improved by 43%. Cost per click decreased 28%. Sign up rate improved by 45%. Instant donor conversion rate improved by 14%. Gross Cost per Name decreased by 50%, Gross Cost Per Donor decreased by 56% and Net Cost Per Donor decreased by 76% - leading to an improved ROI by 231% on the treatment. We believe one reason we saw these results is due to the clarity of the call to action by putting the desired action front and center in easy to read large text directly on the image so it is more clear to the ad viewer what the next step to take is. in future tests, we can test new messaging on the same style of graphic to see if we can continue to improve response by finding the right CTA.

experiments.title: How the presentation of the format of a content offer impacts email sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showcasing the digital resource on an iPad mockup on the landing page will lead to an increase in email sign-ups because there is increased clarity on what the person will receive.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2023
Each year around Christmas, Dallas Theological Seminary creates an eBook devotional to send to their email list.
This year, they wanted to see how the image on the landing page impacts overall email signups. Historically, they have used a 3d eBook image of the cover of the eBook. They wanted to test how showcasing the devotional as a digital resource by mocking it up on an iPad would impact email conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that showcasing the eBook as a digital resource on an iPad mockup on the landing page led to a significant increase in email sign-ups. The treatment group, which saw the iPad mockup, had a 3.9% higher conversion rate compared to the control group, which saw the traditional 3d eBook image.
This indicates that providing a clear visual representation of the digital resource, such as through an iPad mockup, helps potential subscribers better understand what they will receive. This increased clarity likely contributed to the higher conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue showcasing the digital resource on an iPad mockup or similar visual representation. This can be applied to other landing pages or promotional materials to improve conversion rates and increase engagement. It is important to continue testing and iterating on visual elements to optimize conversion rates and overall effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How using a pledge vs a direct account registration ask through Facebook Lead Ads affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe we’ll receive cheaper names through a pledge lead ad than through a direct account creation ask because signing a pledge is less of a commitment.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/05/2023
This organization is trying to acquire more new accounts for its school curriculum program. We wanted to see how lead ads would affect sign up rate and cost per new account, but we decided to test two different types of lead ads to see which “ask” performed better.
The first type was a teacher pledge. This Facebook ad led to a form in Facebook where they submit their name and email address to sign the pledge. Then their account is created using a zapier connection. Their log in information is emailed to them.
The second type was a direct ask to make an account. This ad lef to a form in Facebook where they submitted their name and email address to create their account. It was created and their log in information was emailed to them.
Although the first type is an indirect ask to create an account, we anticipated we’d receive cheaper names since the ask is more straight-forward, and has less commitment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the teacher pledge ask performed better than the direct account creation ask in terms of acquiring emails. The treatment saw a 25.7% decrease in emails acquired compared to the control. This finding is statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on this result, it is recommended to continue using the teacher pledge ask in future experimentation for acquiring new accounts. This type of ask leads to more email acquisitions and is more effective in generating new accounts.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the length of a survey impacted clicks on a donor email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding transparency about the length of a survey for donors will achieve an increase in survey participation because the expectations on the length of the survey are clearly communicated. because the expectations on the length of the survey are clearly communicated.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/06/2023 - 09/08/2023
When sending out a survey to donors, we wondered if including the number of questions in a survey within an email would enhance its appeal and clarity for recipients. We believe that by stating the number of questions upfront, recipients can gauge the time commitment required, making the survey seem more manageable and less daunting. This transparency builds trust and sets clear expectations, making the value proposition of participating in the survey more compelling. It assures recipients that their time is valued and that the survey won’t be overly lengthy or burdensome.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding transparency about the length of a survey can significantly increase survey participation. By clearly communicating the number of questions upfront, recipients are able to gauge the time commitment required and make an informed decision about participating in the survey.

The treatment group in this experiment experienced a 47.6% increase in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the transparency about the survey length was effective in capturing recipients' attention and motivating them to click. Additionally, the Clicks to Opens metric showed a substantial 42% increase, further suggesting that recipients found the survey more appealing and were more likely to engage with it.

To utilize this learning in future experimentation, it is important to continue incorporating transparency and clear communication in the design of survey invitations. Including information about the number of questions or the expected time commitment can help set realistic expectations and encourage more recipients to participate. It is also worth exploring other ways to enhance the appeal and clarity of survey invitations, such as emphasizing the value and impact of the survey or providing a brief overview of the topics covered.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of transparency and clear communication in engaging donors and increasing survey participation. By incorporating these elements in future experimentation, we can continue to improve the effectiveness of our survey invitations and ultimately gather more valuable insights from our donors.

experiments.title: How the placement of a content offer on blog article pages impacts email sign up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-out style interrupter to promote an eBook offer on blog article pages will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it will capture the reader’s attention mid-article instead of having to scroll to the bottom.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/10/2023 - 10/30/2023
Care Net has typically run what we call a “dear reader” style ad at the end of their articles. For example, the reader would be on a blog article page, read the entire article and then after, would be presented with what looks like a continuation of the article but starts with, “Dear reader,” and then presents the content offer to the reader.
We were curious to see how many people weren’t actually scrolling to the bottom of the article and therefore, weren’t seeing the content offer. So we decided to develop a slide out that used the same copy and also included the same image of the eBook, but would be presented to the reader once they scrolled through 50% of the article.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The use of a slide-out interrupter significantly increased the number of emails acquired for all traffic by 638.1% compared to the traditional "dear reader" style ad at the end of the article.
2. The slide-out interrupter was successful in capturing the reader's attention mid-article, eliminating the need for them to scroll to the bottom to see the content offer.
3. The slide-out interrupter was able to effectively promote the eBook offer, leading to a higher conversion rate.
4. The results of the experiment were statistically significant at a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a strong likelihood that the treatment was the cause of the increase in email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using the slide-out interrupter on blog article pages. This approach has proven to be more effective in capturing the reader's attention and increasing email acquisition. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations in the timing or design of the interrupter to optimize its effectiveness further. Furthermore, considering the success of the interrupter in promoting the eBook offer, it could be applied to other content offers or calls to action on the website to improve conversion rates.

experiments.title: How including a video on a slide-out impacts transactions

rows.name: Treatment - Security Box with Lock and Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-out featuring a video will drive more transactions than a photo. The video adds a personal and human touch, allowing prospects to see and hear directly how their donation will benefit WUNC. This engaging approach is likely to make them more inclined to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2023 - 08/18/2023
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to determine whether a slide-out with a video or a sticky bar with a photo would drive more transactions. Both the slide-out and the sticky bar featured WUNC’s Capitol Bureau Chief, delivering a value proposition on why prospective supporters should contribute to WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-out featuring a video outperformed the sticky bar with a photo in terms of driving transactions for WUNC. The video likely added a personal and human touch, making prospects more inclined to contribute. The treatment group saw a significant 107.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 94.2%. Although this result is approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the intended effect.
Moreover, Revenue also experienced a 9% increase with a 98% level of confidence, indicating that the video slide-out had a positive impact on the financial outcome as well.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of different visual elements in driving conversions. The results suggest that incorporating videos with a personal touch can be a successful strategy for engaging potential supporters and ultimately increasing transactions and revenue for WUNC. It would be beneficial to conduct more experiments with varying types of multimedia content and to continue analyzing the impact on key metrics. Additionally, exploring the optimal timing, placement, and design of such visual elements could further enhance their effectiveness in driving desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How using a quote on a landing page affects email sign up rate

The Heritage Foundation is offering an ebook about the Second Amendment. In order to try and increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, they wanted to test adding a quote from a legal fellow about the importance of your second amendment rights.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from a legal fellow about the importance of second amendment rights to the landing page for a second amendment ebook download had a positive impact on the email sign-up rate. The treatment group, which saw the landing page with the quote, had an 11.8% increase in Emails Acquired compared to the control group. This result is statistically significant with a confidence level of 95.8%.
Based on this learning, it is recommended to include quotes or statements that emphasize the value and importance of the ebook content in future landing pages. This can help potential downloaders understand the significance of the ebook, which may encourage them to sign up for the email list.
Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B experiments to further optimize the email sign-up rate on landing pages. By testing different elements, such as quotes, headlines, and calls-to-action, the organization can continue to improve the conversion rate and acquire a larger audience for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How an “Organic Status” Facebook Ad Affected Conversion Rate

rows.name: NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Control
NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an “Organic Status” ad for Facebook users will achieve an increased conversion rate because  the ad will stand out amongst other sponsored posts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/10/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. We decided to test if an organic looking facebook post used as an ad increased clicks and conversion rates compared to their traditional looking ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning in this experiment is that Facebook users are more likely to click on an ad if it seems like it is an organic post. Because the ad looks like an organic status post, it stands out to users, so they don't immediately assume it's an ad and scroll past it. Making the ad stand out more to users increased the number of clicks by 49.5% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence & decreased the Cost per Click by 19.6% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence as well. I believe this positive effect was also impacted by the fact that the treatment seemed like a personal recommendation versus just a promotion of a product. It is important to note that these ads ran exclusively in-feed on Facebook. All other ad placements were turned off.
It is also interesting to note that the older the audience, the higher the conversion rate. This could be due to the fact that an older user may not be able to tell as easily a a younger user that it is an ad and not an organic post making them more likely to click on it.
Moving forward, we should continue to test this type of "Organic Status" ad on various types on content offers that appeal to varying types and ages of audiences. We should also try applying the strategy of making an ad look more organic in other ways, such as a normal status post, and maybe try an organic looking Instagram & Facebook story post as well.

experiments.title: How a video-based landing page affects email signup rates for a new YouTube fundraising show
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a text-based landing page with a video-based landing page will increase registrations by “providing a sample” of the content’s value and showing rather than telling why people should want to tune in.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2023
We recently decided to pursue a new way to share what works in fundraising by launching a YouTube show called “Decoding Generosity”.
And to promote this new ‘edutainment’ experience, we wanted to know how using a video on a landing page would affect sign-up rates for a prescreening of the show.
This test was run in the context of a broader promotional strategy testing the concept of creating a unified in-funnel experience using video as a means of “showing” rather than telling about the show.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using a video-based landing page instead of a text-based landing page produced a significant 57% increase in the registration rate.

We believe there are several reasons that the video-based treatment produced these results:
Show, don't tell. The video-based landing page matched the medium of the content we were promoting, allowing us to preview clips from the show to stoke their desire to tune in and "show" that the claim presented in our offer was genuine
 	Front and center. By reducing the supporting copy, the value-driven headline and subhead were given more visual prominence
 	Quality matters. The high production quality of the video likely played a role in the overall effectiveness of the treatment
 	Context matters. Traditionally, we have seen that text-based landing pages far outperform video-based landing pages. But it's important to consider context. This promotion was run in the context of a B2B promotion (rather than a fundraising context) and the landing page was part of a broader video-based promotional experience meant to "keep people in the story" without feeling like they were in a funnel**

experiments.title: Will shortening a promotional email increase click-throughs?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening a promotional email for our general subscribers will achieve an increase in click-throughs because the email is more direct, human, and will create a higher sense of urgency by focusing exclusively on the upcoming ticket price increase.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2023 - 07/24/2023
At NIO Summit, there’s a lot going on. Beyond the in-depth sessions that dig deep into the most recent data and strategies in digital fundraising, there’s a whole theme that we lean into—heavily.
Each month when there’s a ticket deadline it’s often challenging to pick and choose which unique value of NIO to drive forward to the audience that will make them want to come. And often this leads to long emails with a lot of details.
After seeing a very parsed-down email from Sticker Mule about a deal on t-shirts that they were running, we wondered how that might apply to an upcoming ticket deadline promo we were planning to send to our subscribers.
We wanted to see if communicating all the many benefits of attending NIO Summit in a single email was creating mental friction. And whether a simpler, more direct email, focused on a single message might eliminate that friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both emails had about a 20% open rate, the treatment email resulted in a 138% increase in clicks. Beyond an increase in clicks, it’s important to note that 3 people who received the treatment email bought tickets to NIO Summit, while no tickets were bought by people who received the control email. 

This supports our hypothesis that our typical value-heavy promotional emails may be seen as cluttered and create mental friction. And that the shortened promotional email may increase click-throughs by creating more clarity and a higher sense of urgency.

We plan to test this same hypothesis in other contexts. Our next iteration will be to test this with an email releasing a new online fundraising resource. 

 

Winston's Analysis:

The key learning from this experiment is that shortening a promotional email to focus on a single message can lead to a significant increase in click-through rates. The treatment email, which was more direct and focused exclusively on the upcoming ticket price increase, resulted in a 138.4% increase in clicks for all traffic.

This suggests that providing a clear and urgent call-to-action, without overwhelming the audience with too much information, can be more effective in motivating subscribers to take action.

Furthermore, the experiment also revealed a 591% decrease in Clicks to Opens. This indicates that the shorter email may have created a higher sense of urgency, resulting in subscribers clicking on the email without spending much time engaging with its content.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of shorter, more focused emails on click-through rates. This can be applied not only to promotions for ticket deadlines but also to other types of email communications.

Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the conversion rates and overall engagement of subscribers who clicked on the shorter email versus the longer email. This will help determine the quality of the clicks and the effectiveness of the treatment in driving desired actions.

Overall, the key takeaway is that simplicity and directness in email messaging can lead to higher engagement and click-through rates. Communicating a single clear message that creates a sense of urgency can be a powerful strategy in future email promotions.

experiments.title: How social proof and communal language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update(Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using social proof and communal language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will identify with being a compassionate person and will want to join others who have donated.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using social proof/communal language to let donors know that they’re joining a larger group of compassionate people when they give. The last test we ran for this experiment generated a decrease in gifts. We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
The first time we ran this experiment CaringBridge was undergoing a change in donation platform. This additional variable introduced during the experiment did not give us the clean data needed. We rested this concept to gather better data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN emails led to a significant decrease in donations for all traffic. This experiment resulted in a 34% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99% - making this a valid result. This confirms that the initial experiment is also a valid result in the decrease in donor conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to explore other messaging strategies that may resonate better with the audience and use other psychological principles to move prospective donors to action.

experiments.title: How more user-friendly value proposition copy impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that more user friendly value proposition copy for course prospects will achieve an increase in email sign up rate because prospects will be more likely to read the full value proposition and will better understand the value that the course will bring to their lives.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/22/2023
DTS is getting a low email conversion rate through their Galatians online course. In reviewing the existing course sign up page, we wonder if the formatting and org-centric messaging is deterring people from signing up for the course. The existing page includes lengthy paragraphs and unusual spacing, and focusing a lot on the organization rather than the prospect.
Therefore, we built a treatment variant that breaks up the lengthy paragraphs into smaller ones, implements typical spacing, and focuses more on what value the course offers to the prospect rather than why the organization created the course. Let’s see which performs better…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment did not produce a lift in course sign up rate like we anticipated. It led to a 57.9% decrease in course signups with a valid 99.9% level of confidence.
Prospects did not respond well to our simplified, more user-focused value proposition copy. With DTS, it seems the user-centric approach over the org-centric approach is not always best. It may be that brand recognition is strong for DTS and users appreciate how DTS discusses why they created a course and what their overall purpose is.
And maybe despite breaking the two lengthy paragraphs into shorter paragraphs, the fact that there were more paragraphs and overall, and lengthier copy turned away a greater number of users. It may be that users were already motivated to sign up for the course, but seeing multiple paragraphs ahead of the sign up form felt like a barrier to sign up.
Overall, there were multiple elements being tested in this experiment. To gain confident results, it's probably more effective to isolate the elements to determine what specifically is leading to a decrease or an increase.

This can help identify any specific issues or concerns that need to be addressed in order to optimize the landing page for maximum conversion.
Overall, this experiment serves as a reminder that assumptions and hypotheses should always be tested before implementing changes. It is essential to constantly iterate and improve based on data and customer feedback to achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How testing different Dear Reader survey topics impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a dear reader asking for opinions on the polarizing topic of the border crisis in Texas for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because readers will be more drawn to that topic than of school choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 07/03/2023
TPPF has two active surveys: School Choice and Border Crisis. We want to see which survey topic is most appealing to our reader audience and can collect the most emails, so we’ve separated them via a 50/50 Dear Reader experiment.
These Dear Readers are implemented at the end of policy issue articles, so 50% of readers will be asked to complete the School Choice Survey and 50% will be asked to complete the Border Crisis Survey once they are done reading their respective article.
Let’s see which survey is most appealing to our audience and acquires the most emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The implementation of a "dear reader" treatment asking for opinions on the polarizing topic of the border crisis in Texas resulted in a significant increase (99.4% level of confidence) in email acquisition. The treatment group experienced an 80.2% increase in email acquisition compared to the control group.
The treatment also resulted in one downstream donation compared to none to the control.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the topic of the border crisis in Texas is highly relevant and engaging for page visitors, more so than the topic of School Choice. This suggests that future experimentation should continue to explore this topic and its potential impact on user engagement and email acquisition, and that this org should roll out the Border Crisis Dear Reader to 100% of traffic.
It is also worth noting that the experiment ran for 13 days, which may not capture all potential long-term effects. Future experiments could consider running for a longer duration to assess any sustained impact on email acquisition.
In summary, the key learnings from this experiment emphasize the importance of selecting relevant and engaging topics to drive user engagement and email acquisition.

experiments.title: How an organic looking Facebook Ad impacts ad engagement and email acquisition in a content offer funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an organic looking ad design will increase the number of emails acquired by increasing engagement with the ad.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2023 - 05/30/2023
KCBI is a Christian broadcast ministry in Dallas Texas. For their latest devotional eBook content offer, they wanted to test a more “authentic” and “organic” looking design to their ads. They hypothesized that a plain image with text against a solid colored background, mimicking Facebook’s status update template, would increase engagement on the ad because it wouldn’t look like an “ad” compared to their standard design that featured the eBook cover in the image and ad copy describing the resource. The only copy in the treatment would be the copy in the organic looking image.
They set it up as a AB test in Facebook and ran it for 10 days with equal budget behind each variation
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organic design had had a significantly lower CPM of just $7.55 vs $20.75 on the control. However, the Link Click Through Rate on the treatment was significantly lower than the CTR on the control. Just 45% on the treatment vs 78% on the control. This meant that the treatment had a smaller lead in cost per link click.  $0.96 on the treatment vs $1.38 on the control.
Ultimately, we were trying to get more subscribers, not just clicks. The treatment had a 90% lower subscriber rate which led to a $10.23 gross cost per name for the organic design vs a $2.37 gross cost per name for the control.
What this shows is that we don't just need more clicks, we need the right clicks on our ads and the lack of additional value propositional copy in the ad led to a significantly higher proportion of unmotivated traffic to the landing page. Additional testing is needed to see if an "organic" ad design with ad copy would perform better than our standard ad creative.
