experiments.title: How testing different Dear Reader survey topics impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a dear reader asking for opinions on the active topic of school choice in Illinois for page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because readers will be more drawn to the topic of school choice.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2023 - 06/10/2023
IPI has two active surveys: State of Our State and School Choice. We want to see which survey topic is most appealing to our reader audience and can collect the most emails, so we’ve separated them via a 50/50 Dear Reader experiment.
These Dear Readers are implemented at the end of policy research articles, so 50% of readers will be asked to complete the State of Our State Survey and 50% will be asked to complete the School Choice Survey once they are done reading their respective article.
Let’s see which version acquires the most emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Dear Reader pointing readers to our School Choice survey did not perform as well as its counterpart - the State of Our State survey. In fact, the School Choice variant produced a 42.3% decrease in email conversion rate with a valid 99.9% level of confidence.

The results give us valid proof that more page visitors were interested in offering their opinions on the general well being of our state covering a number of topics compared to those who wanted to offer their opinions on a specific topic. Is this evidence that we should boast more general surveys over single-issue surveys moving forward? Not necessarily, as it's possible that school choice simply wasn't a topic that is affecting that many people right now, or that people care deeply about.

The value proposition for each Dear Reader followed a similar template, so without a different approach in value proposition, the results really do boil down to the topic presented.

For our next test, we will swap the School Choice survey for the Illinois Forward survey, which is about the state's fiscal standing and overall responsibility. We'll found out if that topic outduels the State of Our State survey.

It's important to test different dear readers to see which resonates best with our readers and deserves the long standing spot for our standard policy research dear readers. We will continue to test to determine which one has the greatest potential to rack up emails and enhance engagement.

experiments.title: How removing lead-in copy from a survey impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in survey submissions because there will be fewer steps to arrive at the beginning of the survey questions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2023
Because most traffic was coming from a Facebook ad that gave the context for why taking the survey was important, we hypothesized that the preamble on the landing page to begin a survey was creating unnecessary friction in the process. We tested removing this section and diving straight into the questions to see how it would impact conversion at the landing page level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we observed after running the experiment was the treatment showed a 58.0% increase in emails acquired with a statistical level of confidence of 99.9% so the results are valid. This experiment suggests that minimizing the number of steps to arrive at the survey questions can improve visitor engagement. The visitors were already consuming the value proposition at the Facebook ad level and did not need further motivation on the landing page to continue the process. We learned that we simply needed to get out of their way and let them begin without the extra step causing friction.

In future experimentation, we will run additional tests that make it simple, quick, and easy for visitors to navigate through the pages. We can start with numbering the questions presented so the user has a better idea of how many questions they will be asked to answer and assess how long it will take them to finish the work involved.

experiments.title: How reformatting a landing page survey impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplified formatting through a survey for survey participants will achieve an increase in email sign ups because participants will have an improved experience by being able to more easily read the survey questions and follow along with the survey.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 06/24/2023
IPI’s Illinois Forward survey is producing a low 27.8% sign up rate. When reviewing the page and taking into account consumer feedback, we attributed the struggling metric to heavy friction through the survey formatting. The existing page includes yellow, all caps survey text that may be difficult for users to read, plus the survey questions are not numbered as surveys customarily are.
We wonder if reformatting the survey to use standard font color and style will make it easier to read, and numbering each survey question will make it easier to follow. You can see the noticeable differences in formatting via the screenshots below.
Let’s see how these simple, user-friendly adjustments impacted survey sign ups…
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test validated in favor of the treatment after running for just 6 days! The treatment produced a 14.1% lift in email conversion rate with a 99.6% level of confidence.
This is a prime example of reducing friction for the user by making their experience more user-friendly and through simplified styling/formatting. Just by making the survey questions more legible and easy to digest, we saw an immediate lift in email sign ups.
We have rolled the treatment out to 100% of traffic. We'll want to apply these changes to future surveys as well.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a petition page affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an element of social proof for potential petition signers will achieve a higher email conversion rate.

Timeframe: 10/19/2022 - 11/12/2022
MSLF is running a petition as an acquisition offer. We wanted to test how adding social proof to the petition page of how many people have already signed the petition would affect email conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The social proof element caused a 6.8% increase in email acquisition with a 99.8% level of confidence. We believe this means that users are more likely to sign a petition when they see the evidence of others signing and believing in the value proposition as well.

Although we saw a valid increase in email acquisition, we saw a directional decrease of 28.6% for revenue received on the following instant donation page. We would like to test this concept further on other acquisition offers to see if we have the same results for revenue.

Revenue experienced a 28.6% decrease with a 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a Pop-Up on the Journal Page Increased Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup with value copy for readers on a user’s page, but who do not follow them will achieve an increase in follows because the copy helps impart the importance of staying updating and how they can offer support by following.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2023 - 04/07/2023
CaringBridge is a platform where users can update friends and family about their health journey. In the journal view of a user’s page, CaringBridge wanted to capture users who were browsing the user’s entries but did not follow them.  We hypothesized that a simple popup with value copy about staying updating and informed on the user’s health journey would increase the number of follows.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a pop-up with value copy about staying updated and informed on the user's health journey can significantly increase the number of follows on a user's page. The treatment group saw a 138.8% increase in Follow Site (Journal Pages) for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This improvement was seen across all visitor types, including desktop, mobile, tablet, new, returning, direct, email, organic search, and paid search visitors. However, visitors aged 45-54 had a 221% decrease in follow site (journal pages) with a 97% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the age group that did not respond positively to the pop-up and adjust the messaging accordingly. Additionally, testing different variations of the pop-up, such as other types of value copy or presenting it at various points during the user's journey on the platform, could reveal even more effective ways to increase follows. Overall, the results of this experiment demonstrate the value of reminding users, not only that they weren't already following, but also the important of how their follow supports the user.

experiments.title: How increased appeal through value proposition and social proof impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increased appeal through value proposition and social proof for potential coursetakers will achieve an increase in course sign ups because potential coursetakers will be inspired by what the course offers them and by the overall popularity of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/08/2023
In an effort to increase course sign ups through their Revelations course registration page, DTS has developed a treatment to communicate enhanced appeal for the offer and leverage social proof.
For the treatment, we’re refocusing the value proposition on the potential course taker rather than the organization and the course professor. We want to test the angle of “what can this course do for me” or “what can I get out of this course”. And we also want to leverage social proof to see if joining “one of our most popular courses” or “30,000 people who have already enrolled” will motivate people to take the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our combination of increased appeal and social proof produced a valid 62.7% decrease in course sign ups during a time span of only 13 days.
It seems this approach was not well-received by our audience, as we saw a decrease in all segments and down-funnel at the donations and revenue levels as well.
Perhaps our audience does appreciate the background of the professor of the course and learning about the organization's purpose for their courses as they get ready to sign up – more so than learning what they may get out of this specific course. Additionally, social proof may not move the needle if someone is interested in the course from a personal education standpoint rather than a "popularity" stand point.
Ultimately, we may want to test different value proposition that better compliments the course instructor and organization's mission instead of taking an entirely different angle like we did in this experiment. We might also test different types of social proof such as testimonials instead of popularity call outs.
We can isolate these elements to get better direction for future tests.

experiments.title: How clarity in a subject line impacted email clicks

rows.name: Looking out for your child’s education
A free educational program for kids

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to a subject line for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because recipients will gain a strong initial idea of what the message is about and what it might offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 06/08/2023
We want to test three different subject lines for ACI’s Little Patriots Pledge email to see which generates the most clicks. The purpose of the email is to send recipients to the pledge landing page where they would pledge to use and expand the Little Patriots curriculum.
The control subject line broadly mentions education
Treatment 1 subject line mentions a specific type of education
Treatment 2 subject line mentions an actual product – a “free educational program”
Let’s see which approach towards clarity in a subject line generates the most email clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Clarity proved to be the key to this subject line test. Each treatment produced a valid increase in email clicks. Treatment 1 produced a 145.8% increase in clicks while treatment 2 produced a 158.7% increase.

Our audience responded well to clarity. They appreciated knowing more information as they were considering opening the email, and surely the "free" callout in treatment two resonated strongly with them.

Interestingly, when looking at open rate, treatment 2 resulted in a decrease while treatment 1 resulted in a 2.6% increase with a 98% level of confidence. So treatment 2 produced a significant increase in opens and clicks.

Moving forward, we should implement more subject lines that focus on clarity, giving our audience up front transparency and value.

experiments.title: How a newsletter sign up compared to an ebook sign up

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/15/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. To convert web traffic to email subscribers, we tested a slide out with fields to sign up for the weekly What’s Trending Newsletter vs. an ebook offer.
WUNC has struggled in converting web traffic into email subscribers, in a previous test we experimented with presenting a newsletter sign up via a slide-in and saw a 395% increase in emails acquired https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6001
For the next iteration, we are testing the newsletter offer vs a content offer to see if the item presented has an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment conducted by WUNC found that the slide-out offering an ebook generated more email subscribers than the one offering a weekly newsletter. The treatment offered a 63.5% increase in emails acquired for all traffic, with a 99.4% confidence level.
The key learnings from this experiment are that offering an ebook is a more effective way to acquire email subscribers for WUNC. In future experimentation, WUNC should continue offering an ebook in their email sign-up prompts and test variations of the newsletter content to optimize email acquisition. Additionally, testing different types of incentives to encourage sign-ups could be a useful experiment to run.

To increase email acquisition on Avila’s new Into The Deep course, we decided to add the course’s trailer to the landing page to see if it would drive conversions.

This means that adding a video did not provide more information about the course and did not convince more visitors to sign up for the course.
In future experimentation, we should consider testing other factors that may lead to a higher email sign-up rate, such as changing the language or formatting of the landing page or offering a special promotion for signing up. It is also important to continue testing and experimenting to find the most effective strategies for increasing email acquisition and achieving the organization's goals.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials to an education program sign up page impacted sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including parent testimonials of the program on the main registration page will result in an increase in email acquisition because the social proof of other’s reviews will encourage more people to sign up for the program.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2023 - 04/14/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to test how adding testimonials from parents about the value of the Little Patriots program would affect the email acquisition rate for ACI.
We split traffic 50/50 – 50% received the control registration page while the other 50% received the treatment version which included the testimonials/reviews.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including parent testimonials on the registration page does not necessarily lead to an increase in email acquisition rates. In fact, the treatment version with testimonials saw a 10.4% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic, with a 96.1% level of confidence that this result was statistically significant. This suggests that the testimonials may have been distracting to the overall goal of email acquisition, and added unnecessary friction to the registration process.
The treatment saw a 9.2% decrease in email acquisition among new visitors with a 90% level of confidence. This is encouraging as even new visitors to Little Patriots come to the registration page already confident in the product. They do not need testimonials to convert.
Based on these findings, future experimentation should focus on testing the impact of testimonials on different types of visitors, as well as exploring alternative forms of social proof, such as trust badges or customer ratings. Additionally, it may be useful to test the impact of different types of language and messaging on the registration page to identify the most effective call-to-action for each type of visitor.

experiments.title: How a more detailed landing page with longer copy affects sign-up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more detail to the copy for landing page visitors will achieve higher sign-up rates because it will create greater urgency around the issue the asset addresses and better explain what the prospect will gain from accepting the offer. because it will create greater urgency around the issue the asset addresses and better explain what the prospect will gain from accepting the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2023 - 02/01/2023
For this experiment for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how longer value proposition copy affected sign-up rates. Because sign-up rates were so low, we redesigned several elements of the landing page in an effort to convey more value and urgency around the offer—an interactive map that scores the integrity of elections in each state.
We crafted the headline to include the word free and show what someone would learn from the offer. In the body copy, we included some specific examples of alleged voter fraud to increase urgency around the problem and thus the need for the resource. And in the bullets, we removed the “and more” bullet and instead included all 12 metrics the scorecard used to assess election integrity.
We ran this experiment for 30 days with 50% of traffic seeing the control and 50% of traffic seeing the treatment
experiments.keyLearnings:
At the conclusion of this test, we observed a 12.7% decrease in sign-up rates with a 99.9% level of confidence, proving our hypothesis that longer value-proposition copy would increase sign-up rates.

Upon review of these results, we believe that the length of the copy gave the appearance of a "wall" of text the visitor would have to read, creating enough friction for the prospect to click away. The longer copy may have done more to halt the momentum of the prospect than it did encourage them to sign up.

Because the landing page visitor has already expressed interest in both the offer and the problem it addresses, we hypothesize that it may be more effective to reduce the amount of copy and create a more frictionless path to acquisition once the prospect lands on the sign-up page.

As such, we plan to follow up this experiment with a new treatment that removes the first 3 paragraphs of copy from the control (much of which is restated from the Facebook ads through which the prospect has clicked—and get straight to the point by immediately telling the reader what they will gain by accessing the scorecard, making it easier and quicker for them to sign up.

experiments.title: How the use of a well-known figure influences action in an email
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
talking about Dr. Carson as the reason behind the crash rather than focusing on just the large volume of people for email subscribers will achieve an increase of clicks because people will be more motivated if moved to action by someone they trust over a large volume of unknown people.
because  people will be more motivated if moved to action by someone they trust over a large volume of unknown people.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2023 - 02/10/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute was launching their new Little Patriots site. Due to some unexpected press coverage, they got a flood of traffic to the new site which brought it down. They wanted to use this as an opportunity to update their subscribers on the impact and let them know that the site is now accessible. This email proved to be an opportunity to drive more traffic to the program so they decided to run a test on what content would drive more interest. They decided to test two angles: “Popularity” vs. “Dr. Carson” with popularity being about the huge interest nationwide and Dr. Carson being about how his activities are tied to these efforts. This email was sent to a small subset of the subscriber file with the winner being rolled out the the entire audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that talking about Dr. Carson as the reason for the crash of the site was not more motiviating than just talking about the huge interest throughout the country. This suggests that users are more likely to respond to tangible and proven success stories versus abstract stories, even if they involve a figure they trust. This can help inform future experiments and help target audiences more accurately with messaging that’s more likely to resonate. We should focus on stories and results that are easy to understand, have visual appeal, and showcase authoritative figures that evoke trust and have real-world results.

First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs more of an invite to attend church.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment has shown us that the more personal and invitational approach to the Facebook ad resulted in higher click conversions. We saw a 11.1% increase in Clicks when compared with the traditional approach. This demonstrates the potential impact of creating a more personal and warmer welcome to church via Facebook ads. For future experiments, we should continue to focus on creating more inviting and conversational advertisements to increase engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement to a pledge form impacted sign-up rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding privacy language  for pledge signers will achieve a change in sign up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2022 - 08/22/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth. In July of 2022, they released a new book, America is a Christian Nation, as their featured book of the month.
For this experiment, we created a pledge based on some of the points made in the book as a way to acquire new names onto the file. In this experiment, we tested whether adding privacy language on the pledge form would impact sign-up rates.
The language (not reflected in the screenshots below) was: “Pathway to Victory takes data security and privacy seriously. The safety of your data is one of our utmost priorities.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were very interesting. Given that Pathway to Victory had concerns over running a pledge, and in the past many signers had expressed concern about what happens to their email address, we believed that by adding the privacy statement, Pathway to Victory would increase conversion rates.
However, the treatment version with the statement had an 11% decrease in sign-ups, with a 99.9% level of confidence. This could be due to the placement of the privacy statement and how it rendered on certain browsers, or the fact that by placing the statement there, it made signers more wary of giving PTV their email address.
We will continue to test this concept on future pledge and email acquisition pages.

experiments.title: How adding clarity in a Facebook ad impacted email conversions on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the ad copy for Facebook audiences will achieve an increase in pledge signatures.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/19/2022
During calendar year-end, we sent an offer to the housefile asking them to commit to ACI’s Four Pillars Pledge. We saw strong results from the email subscribers so we took the offer to Facebook for additional conversion opportunities for existing leads and prospecting to acquire new names. The email version included a preview of all four belief statements they would be asked to sign on the landing page, so we wanted to test including this in the Facebook ad as well. We ran this against the control, which kept some mystery to intrigue the ad viewer to sign the pledge.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the short ad and the long ad side by side for two weeks was the long treatment version increased email conversions by 15.2% with 99.% level of confidence. We believe this increase is due to giving the reader the option to review the values they would be affirming by signing the Four Pillars Pledge, so they were already primed to say "Yes" and agree to these same items when they were presented on the landing page. Not only did this improve our email conversion rate, but we also saw a 14% lift on donations downstream with 53% level of confidence. Additional benefits were that the treatment ad reduced cost per name by 21.5% and reduced net cost per donor by 44.8%. Going forward, we will run the long version of the ad as the new control and develop a new hypothesis to run against it to see if we can continue to improve performance.

experiments.title: How more focused offers increase your lead quality in acquisition campaigns

rows.name: Facebook Lead Ads w/Facebook Ad Template Offer
Facebook Lead Ads w/better qualifying offers
LinkedIn Lead Ads w/better qualifying offers

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using offers that do a better job of prequalifying my audience for acquisition campaigns will achieve a higher number of “qualified leads” (and a lower cost per qualified lead) because we believe the offer itself plays an important role in pre-qualifying the audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 01/31/2023
As a part of recent experimentation with using Facebook Lead Ad forms in our own acquisition efforts, we observed a substantial decrease in the cost per lead, without decreasing the disengagement and unsubscribe rates of the leads generated.
But, with this much higher volume, we received a substantial number of personal email addresses, which made it difficult to understand whether or not we were actually generating leads from our prime audience (fundraising professionals and/or marketers at nonprofit organizations that raise a certain number of dollars per year).
Our hypothesis was that the offer used in December of 2022 and previous experiments could have more “generic appeal” to any marketer (not just those who work at nonprofit organizations).
We also wondered whether Facebook advertising is the best platform for acquiring leads in general, as well as qualified leads tied to qualified nonprofit organizations.
Therefore, we ran a month-long experiment testing two offers that are more specific to nonprofit organizations across both Facebook (using the lead ads conversion type), while also trying those same (more specific) offers on LinkedIn advertising (using their lead ad equivalent).
Our goal was to compare the results acquired during this experiment time period against the results collected during the previous month’s experiment with Facebook Lead Ads with an offer that would (theoretically) have more generic appeal.
We had four metrics in mind to determine success of this treatment type, which are:
Cost per lead (of any type)
% of leads generated that have a “generic domain” (something like: “@gmail.com” as opposed to a work email address)
% of leads generated that are tied to a “qualified nonprofit organization”
Cost per qualified lead generated
experiments.keyLearnings:
As it turns out, the offers used to better qualify the audience indeed increased the number of qualified leads generated through our acquisition efforts during the duration of this experiment.
In fact, both platforms substantially increased the number of qualified leads generated for our advertising dollar spent. But, the question really is: "By how much?"
We observed that the "better quality offer" treatments across both platforms decreased the cost per qualified lead in the following way:
Facebook: -82.9%LinkedIn: -19.8%
Key Takeaway: So, not only did we increased the volume of qualified leads, we did so by dropping the cost per qualified lead by significant amounts.
Cost per lead trends compared in this way:
Facebook: -48.4%Linked: +1,182%
Key Takeaway: While running the "better quality offers" on Facebook, we actually decreased our cost per lead overall, but the cost per lead (of any type) generated on LinkedIn increased substantially. Therefore, at least in the way the LinkedIn ad campaigns were set up during this experiment period of time, we would not suggest shifting advertising budgets toward LinkedIn to replace Facebook lead generation campaigns.
Finally, our last measurement was to review the % of leads generated with a "generic email domain" — here is how the treatments performed against the control campaigns:
Facebook: -23.9%LinkedIn: -12.9%
Key Takeaway: The "better quality offers" indeed decreased the volume of leads generated with a "generic email domain," as well.
Summary of Findings:
There are actually three key takeaways that we have from this experiment, which are:
The relevance of your offer to your ideal customer/audience is a key factor for increasing both your campaign's response rates overall, but also can attract better quality leads, as well. If your campaigns aren't specific enough to your ideal audience, consider developing new, more relevant offers that can prequalify the audience at the ad impression level.Facebook Lead Ads continues to perform well, as we observed improvements in all success metrics using that tactic. LinkedIn Ads cost (at least in the campaign settings we used) are tremendously more expensive when compared to Facebook Lead Ads, and although we observed higher rates in which that platform produced "better qualified leads," the cost to produce any lead at all on that platform leads us to conclude that we should not shift large amounts of our advertising budget to that platform for net-new lead acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How condensing copy on a pledge impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the length of copy for pledge signers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because they have already been “pitched” the intro copy in the email or ad they clicked over from.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/01/2023
When analyzing an email acquisition page for opportunities to improve conversion, we wondered if the length of copy introducing the statement of belief was dissuading visitors from making it all the way through to the email capture portion of the page. We hypothesized that most visitors to this page had enough information about why it was important that they add their name to the statement of belief based on the amount of clarifying copy in the ads and emails that were sending traffic to this page. We wondered if we were getting in the way and needed to reduce the time required to consume introductory copy before getting the visitor to the beginning of the statement of belief for signing.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 30 days, we saw email conversion rate decrease by 8% on the condensed copy treatment with 99% level of confidence which leads us to believe the additional copy was not a problem or interference for visitors arriving to the page. However, we still think the control conversion rate has room for improvement. In future testing, we will isolate the truncated version specifically for mobile visitors so we can reduce the amount of scrolling time required in the user experience to make it all the way through the statement of belief.

experiments.title: How using an identity resolution service impacts your first-party record match rates within Facebook (Meta)
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we match our first-party contact list at a higher rate when using LiveRamp’s “identity resolution” technology once it was pushed into Facebook (Meta) Audiences?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/26/2023
LiveRamp is a data enablement platform, that claims to take first-party contact data points provided to them and match those contacts within their platform using a process called “identity resolution” to match your contacts within advertising platforms in a more reliable and consistent way, such that the “match rate” increases dramatically.
Since iOS 14.5, advertising platforms like Facebook (Meta) have had an more difficult time matching uploaded contact records, as well as less visibility into online behavioral tracking data, and also online purchase behavior.
With lower match rates and less information flowing back to the algorithm to learn and refine lookalike models, some clients have experienced less return on ad spend (ROAS).
With all of this in mind, we decided to experiment with what the match rates on Facebook (Meta) would be by uploading contacts via the HubSpot CRM integration, when compared to uploading them to Facebook (Meta) via LiveRamp.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed that the LiveRamp "identity resolution" upload process into Facebook (Meta) Ads Manager significantly improved our first-party contact match rate within the advertising platform — matching +94.9% more of the list that we uploaded.
Now that we know that the LiveRamp "identity resolution" service does indeed match your first-party contacts in a much better way, the real question is:
Do the lookalike audiences modeled from this LiveRamp list produce a more reliable lookalike modeled audience to use for acquisition campaigns?
It stands to reason that this could be the case. Why?
Because if our source list for lookalike modeling uses nearly 2x more contacts, then it would be reasonable to assume that the model it produces is far richer and the prospecting audience it produces is using twice as much data to model prospecting audiences to market to with your acquisition campaigns.
Further experimentation is required, specifically as it pertains to nonprofit organizations who are uploading donor data.
In fact, we're in the process of launching a handful of experiments on this very topic, and will share our findings when they are available.

experiments.title: How the placement of a call to action impacted email acquisitions on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that communicating the value prop in a more prominent location for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in program sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 12/28/2022
As we began driving paid traffic to the Little Patriots home page from multiple channels, we were noticing both high bounce rates (58%) and low traffic to the registration page (3.28%) which made us wonder if it was clear to new visitors how to sign-up for Little Patriots upon arriving to the website. In reviewing the site, there were multiple calls to action that seemed equally weighted and there was a lot of friction in the way interfering users from clearly understanding how and why to sign-up for the program. We discovered that the MOST important action we wanted users to take was being presented as the LEAST visible element on the page, likely causing visitors to miss it altogether. Since the goal for visitors was to sign up, we were curious if instead of having the sign up info presented at the bottom of the page, if we could increase conversion by putting the sign up info in the top content block on the page to reduce confusion about what the desired call to action was in order to drive higher name acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for a week, was that the treatment with the value proposition and call to action placed at the top of the page increased email acquisition rate by 154.9% with 99% level of confidence. We believe this is because we moved the content block promoting and communicating the value of the extremely high-value free coursework which serves as the main driver of name acquisition for LP from being buried at the very bottom of the page where visitors had to hunt for the information. We made it easier immediately upon arrival to understand what the desired action was by removing confusion and friction: were visitors supposed to watch a video? explore programs and new episodes? make a gift? These were all options on the page that had varying weights assigned by their placement and level of which they were featured on the site. When we gave visitors a clear entry point at the top of the page and communicated the WHY, we found that more visitors took the intended action to sign up. Going forward, we will roll out the treatment design in order to improve the visibility of Little Patriots coursework while reducing friction and improving UX in an effort to increase name acquisition rates to make better use of the paid advertising dollars being spent to drive traffic to the site.

experiments.title: How including an image of a well-known personality affects click thru rate in email prospecting
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a well-known personality will increase click thru rate by building credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2022
In an effort to increase click thru rate in email prospecting for a public policy client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of a well known personality.
The offer deployed was a children’s book premium offer. The control version used the image of the children’s book, while the treatment version utilized the image of the well-known personality who wrote the book.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.5% increase in click through rate (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we were pleased to conclude with clarifying results the validity of our hypothesis. By adding an image of a well-known personality, the image will serve to build credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
Additionally, while not statistically significant, but still significant to note, the relative difference in donors with increased click thru rate was 76.2%.
The control version received 4 donations while the treatment variant received 28 donors—a 76.2% increase. While the level of confidence was only 79.5% and did not meet the 95% threshold to validate, we still believe these results are still of importance to note.
These results are a positive indication that similar tests should be run for other public policy and faith-based clients that can utilize well-known personalities for marketing purposes.

experiments.title: How adding bullets/star bullets to registration page affects sign-up rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a bulleted list of value proposition language for prospective course takers will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2022 - 11/05/2022
DTS offers free online courses using recordings of actual seminary classes. This three-way split test appeared on the Exodus course landing page.
This is a multivariate test. We first wanted to test if adding a list of value proposition language in a highlighted box would better communicate the value that each free course includes and increase conversion rate.
We then wanted to test whether using star emojis as the list’s bullet point icons would attract attention and help communicate value more than normal bullet points, leading to an increase in course signups in all traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the both treatments produced a small change in conversion rates. The bullet point treatment produced a decrease of 2.2% in course registrations and the star emoji treatment produced a lift of 1.8%, but due to an inadequate sample size, these results did not validate. This makes it difficult to draw a valid conclusion from this experiment.
It's interesting to note that in the email channel, the star emoji treatment produced a valid lift of 13%.
Possible reasons for this increase could be:
the star emoji reminding users of the star emojis seen in an online product review (i.e. Amazon) and signaling the impression of valuethe color and shape of the star emoji attracting the user's attentionthe size of the emoji treatment's container attracting the user's attention moreso than the bullet point treatment's container
As a result, we'll look into rolling this out to email traffic only since the email channel is the main source of traffic for DTS. We'll also look into retesting only the emoji treatment by running it during the launch of a new course to try to get a better sample size and valid results in all traffic channels.

experiments.title: How Facebook Lead Ads impacted email signup rates and lead quality

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Facebook Lead Ad campaign type for lead campaigns targeting net-new prospect audiences will achieve an increase lead volume at a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/06/2022
Since the iOS 14.5 software update (among other things) we have noticed a continuous increase in the cost to acquire a new subscriber within our acquisition campaigns here at NextAfter from Facebook advertising.
In an effort to continuously test ways to decrease the cost per lead metric for our own advertising and marketing campaigns, we wanted to experiment with trying the Facebook Lead Ad campaign type.
In previous tests (many years ago), we observed that the quality of the leads generated was far inferior to those collected from the more “recommended” experience of promoting ad clicks to a hosted landing page, making the case as to why someone should download the offered resource.
As a part of this experiment, we used the same resource as the offer, locking the creative used within the ad, targeting the same audience, but the treatment used a “Facebook Lead Ad” conversion point, as opposed to taking ad clicks from Facebook itself to our hosted landing page to secure the email address.
We were curious as to whether this treatment could not only lower the cost per new subscriber but also whether or not the “lead quality” would be negatively impacted, as well.
It’s worth noting that (at least for us) a quality lead is someone who we can tell (either through the information provided directly, or through a quick Google search for those leads) whether or not they were confirmed to work for a nonprofit organization, at least in the marketing or fundraising teams.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99% level of confidence, we observed an increase in email signup rates of +131.5% through the Facebook Lead Ad treatment campaign experience.
We also observed:
A valid increase in ad clickthrough rate by +24.3% (LoC: 99.1%), andThat the CPM (cost per one thousand impressions) also decreased by -35.8%, as well.
Overall, this treatment experience decreased our cost per lead by a whopping -72.3%!
But, the real question is: Did we just create a bunch of "junk leads" by using the Facebook Lead Ad campaign?
To determine this, we researched the lead using the data provided on the form submission to see whether or not the person requesting the resource was working at a charitable organization within the marketing or development/fundraising groups, or as a decision-maker within the organization's executive team.
Of the leads generated by experience type, here is what we found:
61.6% of the Control experience leads were determined to be "qualified."46.2% of the Treatment experience leads were determined to be "qualified."
So, the claim that the Facebook Lead Ads would produce a lower quality is true — but, from a certain point of view.
The truth of the matter is that although we generated a lower percentage of "qualified leads" from the overall amount of leads generated, the cost per "qualified lead" was actually decreased by -63% when compared to the control experience.
In fact, the cost per qualified lead from the Treatment experience was even -40% less expensive when compared to the total cost per lead from the Control experience.
There are two more observations that we made, as well...
When someone completes the Facebook Lead Ad form, the form itself offers marketers with the opportunity to offer one more thing to the person who just submitted the form. It's worth noting that when we promoted a site-hosted webinar recording that was relevant to the offer used within the experiment, we observed that 64% of the leads generated in the Treatment experience also chose to click the button (to go to our website/watch the webinar offered). And,Facebook Ads Manager's web interface is notoriously bad at properly tracking/reporting accurately under the Control experience type of campaign, however, we noted that the Treatment version of the campaign had 100% accuracy in reporting leads generated, cost per lead, etc.
When it's all said and done — here is what we found:
Facebook Lead Ads will very likely decrease your cost per lead/subscriber.But, as you scale up the number of leads, you'll lose a bit on the percentage of those leads being of "high quality."Regardless of that fact, it's worth using this approach because you get so many more leads (and an overall higher volume of "qualified leads" as a part of the higher total of leads overall) with this approach.
So, what comes next? We'll plan to circle back to the leads generated in this study and look at two more data points downstream, which are:
The percentage of leads who opt-out by the end of the new subscriber welcome series. And also,The percentage of leads who opt-out, are disengaged, and/or who have never opened a single email 60-days after securing the lead.
More on those studies soon, but for now — it's advised that you consider using Facebook Lead Ads in this post-iOS 14.5 world of continuously decreasing performance in Facebook Advertising to drive net-new subscriber growth for your organization and marketing campaigns.

experiments.title: How a new content offer with strengthened value prop in a dear reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting a new content offer for website visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2022 - 12/02/2022
When running an in-site content offer, we were seeing decent response rates from web visitors but we were curious about the lifespan of having a single offer available. This free eBook had been available and marketed to the audience for several months, so at some point returning visitors to the site would no longer engage if they had already received the offer. We had a new content offer to launch, but rather than replacing the existing content offer we wanted to understand how the audience would respond comparatively. We split the traffic 50/50 between the two offers to determine how this change would impact both email acquisition and instant donor conversion before rolling out the new offer to 100% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for one week, was that the treatment—a new devotional eBook—achieved a 1,698% increase in email acquisition rate with 100% level of confidence. We were surprised to see this increase was not limited to returning visitors, but new visitors saw similar lifts as well. Traffic from email and social and both desktop and mobile all saw valid increases in email acquisition. Donations also experienced a 51% increase with 88% level of confidence. We believe this increase is partly due to our hypothesis that a new offer would be more appealing than an offer visitors could have already seen multiple times. But we also believe the new offer was presented with an increased sense of relevancy and urgency that strengthened the value proposition and made it more appealing for visitors to get. The copy encouraged visitors to get their copy now in the last remaining weeks of the year so they could begin the devotional in the new year which was quickly approaching. This specificity on the timeline helps to answer the question in the mind of the visitors - why should I get this offer TODAY, rather than any other day or not at all? This relevance/urgency is an element missing from the control that could explain why we saw such a significant lift in results.

experiments.title: How introducing “donors like you” language impacted clicks in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that casting an email recipient into the role of a donor before they donate will increase click-through rates by using the persuasion principle of consistency to prompt them to follow through.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 07/27/2022
In an effort to increase click-through rates, we wanted to know how “casting” the recipient into the role of donor before they donate would impact engagement. In his experiment, we kept all other copy the same, switching only language that mentions needing “your help” to “help from donors like you”. We sent each version of the email to 50% of the house file to assess the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By casting the email recipient into the role of a donor, we were able to realize a 17.8% lift in click-through rates, with a 97.5% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis. We believe this lift occurred because by asking for help from "donors like you" we preemptively created a feeling of commitment which prompted more people to seek consistency by clicking through.

While past experiments have shown that "you" language increase engagement, we wanted to take this a step further by speaking to a future you that is already making a difference by donating to the cause at hand.

This treatment achieves two things en route to increasing engagement:
It lets the recipient know that they are just the kind of person the client needs to help them accomplish their goal, and that those who do donate are people who align with similar values and goals
 	By casting the recipient into the role of a donor before they donate, the potential donor wants to be consistent by translating their values into action. The thought pattern "I care about these issues and I am someone to whom this organization is looking to help, but am unwilling to take actions" creates an uncomfortable feeling of cognitive dissonance wherein the potential donor feels one way but does not back that up with their actions. This treatment gives them the opportunity to put their values into action, thus increasing engagement

experiments.title: How adding a journal entry step into the new site signup process affects first journal creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a journal entry step for people registering for a new site will achieve an increase in used sites.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/18/2022
CaringBridge was seeking to get more people to start sites, which keep them connected to loved ones through difficult health journeys. However, they did not just want people to start sites, they wanted them to write their first journal, which constitutes a “used site” and leads to more downstream engagement.
They decided to test placing a “write your first journal” prompt into the process of signing up for a new site, with an optional “skip this step” to get more people to write a journal.
They only tested this with 10% of the traffic to mitigate risk.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, despite the small sample size, delivered a valid 70.3% increase in "used sites", or sites with a first journal entry. Previously, the "write your first journal" step had been at the very end of the process, and far fewer people had actually taken the final step.
Interestingly, adding this "friction" into the process also

experiments.title: How adding language that conveys the value of joining a monthly newsletter near the form affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conveying the value of joining a monthly newsletter will increase email acquisition by giving visitors a tangible reason to sign up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/17/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates of website visitors on the homepage, we wanted to know if conveying the value of joining, and what visitors can expect to receive when signing up, would increase the rate at which people were willing to sign up.
Previously there was very little supporting copy near the signup form, with only the phrase “Subscribe to receive updates and event invitations.” presented in small font beneath the form.
For this test, we split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for just over two weeks before validating our results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 17 days, we were able to validate a 71.2%  lift in email acquisitions from visitors who saw the treatment page.
For the treatment, we wanted to convey enough value, social proof, and exclusivity to give visitors an enticing reason to submit their email addresses and agree to receive email updates from this public policy organization.
To add social proof: we leveraged the considerable size of the organization's housefile (146,000+) to express to the visitor that many other people with the same concerns, values, and interests have already signed upTo add exclusivity: we made it clear that they would be receiving emails from Dr. Ben Carson, a well-known and admired public figure, when they signed up. We also used the verbal cue, "exclusive, subscriber-only content" to reinforce this exclusivityTo add value: we clarified what subscribers receive when they sign up ... updates, educational content that teaches America's founding principles and delivers expert explanations, key insights, and critical analysis on the toughest challenges facing our nation today.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that simply asking someone to give you their email address with a generic sign-up prompt is not enough to earn their trust, pique their interest, and motivate them to join. Instead, organizations should consider what value subscribers receive when they join (what's in it for them), and communicate exactly WHY someone should subscribe and WHAT sets your email updates apart from other organizations.
Email marketing remains one of the most cost-effective, powerful tools an organization has to grow their housefile, build advocacy, and convert them into generous donors ... but as people become more and more selective about what messages (and from whom) they want landing in their inbox, communicating "what's in it for them" is no longer an option.
By setting yourself apart and conveying clearly the value people can expect to receive when they sign up, your organization has a huge opportunity to significantly improve your email acquisition rates today!

experiments.title: How re-directing traffic from a blog to a lead capture page affected email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redirecting traffic from the blog to bible study resource landing pages for Searchers on Google will achieve growth in discipleship.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2022
Originally, we were driving as much free traffic we could to maximize the free ad grant dollars to mission aligned pages. We then wanted to test if shifting our goals from blog traffic to capturing emails using landing pages.
Our goal is to offer transformational content for the purpose of resourcing and equipping those interested in discipleship with free resources.
Before, we would direct them to our blog.
After, we direct them to free resource landing pages.
Bible Study Resources is the subset of the traffic we focused on for this test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
A note on the Google Ad Grant:
While we had assumed this outcome (an increase in email acquisition) would be the case, it was still a worthy test as there are obstacles to work through with the Google Ad grant quality score filter that could've limited reach to the lead capture pages.
We aimed to overcome this filter with a better user experience which is indicated in a few metrics: a better conversion rate, time on page, and how many additional pages they visited. In this case, we achieved this with a better conversion rate which improved our quality scores and met the quality score threshold for the ad grant.
In this experiment,
We found that the blog page didn't serve our new goal well: Offering transformational content (via email) for the purpose of resourcing and equipping those interested in discipleship with free resources.
The blog converted at 1.7% CVR compared to the lead capture pages which did a better job of converting at 8.7% CVR (Treatment 1) and 6.5% CVR (Treatment 2).
The final benefit to seeing the conversion rate increase with the bible study resources is that we're able to convert this free traffic into an asset: a re-addressable audience via email at a later time.
Control:
No Capture
Clicks: 7,680
Emails: 132
CPA: $262
CVR: 1.7%
Treatment 1:
How to Study the Bible
Clicks: 878
Emails: 76
CPA: $78.85
CVR: 8.7%
Treatment 2:
Bible Study Bundle
Clicks: 12,297
Emails: 796
CPA: $80.73
CVR: 6.5%

experiments.title: How listing out articles in a ebook impacted email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being specific with article lists for ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2022 - 10/02/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
For this specific ebook, which was a collection of essays already published as part of past Hoover Digests (their quarterly publication), we wanted to test whether a listing of the essays included in the ebook would make an impact on email acquisition rates. The control version had more generic statements as to the content of the ebook.
Our hypothesis was that by giving potential downloaders specific information, including listing some of the more well-known authors included in this ebook, would have a positive impact on acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This offer was promoted through multiple digital channels, including email prospecting, Facebook, and paid search. For the duration of this experiment, the treatment version saw a 8.8% increase in email signups, with a 98.9% level of confidence. However, despite this increase in email signup rates, the treatment version received 18% less donations than the control version.
Our hypothesis was that while being more specific about what the ebook contained did decrease the friction for potential sign-ups, the instant donation page that people were directed to did not have specifics that were relevant to the ebook.
It would be interesting to look at the downstream performance of the treatment donors and see whether they contributed to Hoover later on, after consuming the ebook.
Further iterations of this test should include testing copy on the instant donation page to make it tie in more with the specific offer.

experiments.title: How promoting a free ebook offer instead of a general subscription impacted email acquisition on a commentary page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free eBook at the bottom of the Commentary page for page visitors when submitting their email will achieve an increase in email acquisition because they will be more drawn to this offer after reading gospel commentary than they would be receiving email updates from TGC.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/18/2022 - 11/18/2022
The Gospel Coalition has a Commentary page in which visitors can study and learn about different books of the Bible. Towards the bottom of the page, there is an email subscription form where visitors can sign up for email updates. We wondered if this existing email acquisition opportunity could be optimized to be more alluring and enticing. So we developed a treatment that offered a free eBook for visitors when signing up and submitting their email. And the offer isn’t just for any eBook, but for TGC’s most popular eBook. Would this opportunity acquire more emails than the original opportunity?
We tested this on the TGC home page and saw negative results for the treatment; now we’re testing it on the Commentaries page to see if it aligns better when implemented there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (eBook offer) saw an increase in email acquisitions across all segments. Overall, the treatment produced a 0.92% increase in email acquisitions at a 99.9% level of confidence. The control acquired zero emails for the duration of the test. The results tell us that page visitors were more excited to receive a free eBook than they were to sign up and receive email updates from TGC.

It's worth restating that we ran this same test on the TGC home page and the treatment saw negative results. The difference is people often go to the home page to sign up for email updates and they expect an opportunity to sign up for email updates to exist on the home page. That is not the case for TGC's Commentary page. People visit this page to acquire specific knowledge and understanding. Thus, as they keep scrolling down the page, they're intrigued by an offer that also offers them the chance to acquire knowledge and understanding. They don't care at this stage to sign up for general email updates.

This concept is worth testing on pages for which the free offer aligns with the page content. It would be worth testing on other content-heavy pages.

experiments.title: How lookalike modeling for offer downloaders impacted email signup rate in a prospecting campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that narrowing our lookalike models to just the audience that downloaded the offer being promoted and expanding the number of lookalike models used for our Facebook prospecting campaign will achieve a higher email signup rate, because it’s more narrowly modeled after audiences that converted on the offer in question.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/09/2022
As a part of our attempts to drive down the cost to generate a new email subscriber on Facebook acquisition and paid prospecting campaigns, we wanted to experiment with going deeper and wider into the lookalike audience modeling capabilities for Facebook.
Although we were using some lookalike modeling in our control audiences, we stopped at the most “narrow” matching (looking at roughly the top 1.2MM lookalikes, that were matched at the 99-100% level for all emails on our existing subscription file).
We decided to experiment with building a wider set of lookalike audiences from just subscribers that converted historically on the same offer — and see if it would increase our signup rates, and/or decrease our cost per new email signed up from the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.6% confidence level, we observed an increase in email signup rates of +45.1% by focusing our lookalike modeling on offer-specific lookalike audiences.
In fact, this experiment also validated an increase in ad clickthrough rate of +56.3% (with a 100% level of confidence, even though the ad used across the experiment was identical between the two audiences).
The end result is that our cost per new email subscriber actually dropped by -12.1% (which is a great thing)!
So, right off of the bat, our ad became more attractive to the more acutely focused, offer-specific lookalike audience models — even though the "similarity" level of this treatment audience was decreased from just a 99% match down to a 90%+ match on this specific offer's downloaders historically.
Further experimentation may be required, but as it stands now it looks like at least for our own marketing activities, that we should create 90-100% match audiences, but make the source of those lookalike modeled audiences specific to offers used in our paid advertising campaign to generate net-new subscribers moving forward.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a Facebook ad impacts conversion and cost.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2018 - 02/02/2018
Care Net offers a curriculum for churches to start a pro-life ministry called Making Life Disciples. To get more people to sign up for the curriculum, they pushed the offer through paid Facebook ads. They developed a control that had a quote on it about God being able to make abortion unthinkable through the church. They hypothesized that they might be able to motivate more people to convert if they gave more context in the ad through more value proposition copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment significantly increased conversion by 859%. This tells us that when we provide more context, communicate the value of the offer, and have a call-to-action in the text copy of our ads, we can increase conversion. The expanded value proposition not only got more people to click through to the landing page, but these people were much more highly motivated to convert. This treatment increased conversion, but even more, it decreased cost per conversion substantially from $32/conversion to $5.30/conversion.

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn't have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.

This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: How third-party validation affects email acquisition for a free digital offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity and adding social proof increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2016 - 07/01/2016
The Heritage Foundation was giving away a digital version of their popular 475-page Guide to the Constitution in exchange for an email address. They had a landing page that was converting 58% of visitors. But this meant that 42% of visitors weren’t downloading the digital Guide, so their team wanted to try four tweaks to increase conversion.
One confusing part of the offer was that the digital version of the Guide was in the form of a microsite, so they were really offering “access” rather than a digital download. This caused potential confusion to the user, so they marketed the offer as a digital download (since there was an available PDF).
Then, they launched an A/B test to see if the new page increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment converted 65.4% of visitors to accept the offer -- an increase of 11.2%! This shows that the new landing page tweaks did in fact increase clarity and add some social proof that helped convince the visitor to take action.

While this is a great lift and results in more emails for the same spend, it still means that 35% of our traffic is not converting—which means more testing and optimization is needed!

experiments.title: How a value proposition built upon “what” instead of “why” impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase email acquisition by limiting friction and emphasizing the value to the visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2016 - 02/15/2016
The treatment ended up decreasing email acquisition by 6%. Our hypothesis is that this was not caused by reducing friction or emphasizing the value to the visitor but because we removed the “why” behind the course and focused solely on the “what.”
The paragraph removed talked about the mission of DTS and it allowed visitors to the page to align around this same mission and mentally put themselves in the same audience. Those that felt they belonged would be more likely to take the desired action of signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary had recently launched their first online course, a study of the Gospel of John. The course was offered for free in exchange for a visitor’s name, email, and address. With significant traffic being sent to the site, we wanted to find the best way to communicate the value to the visitors.

Using the learnings from experiments run with similar organization, we tried applying two key principles to the copy on the page:
We emphasized what the visitor would get by subscribing instead of what they would have to do
 	We reduced the friction associated with the process by removing copy that we felt was unnecessary to the conversion.

experiments.title: How removing transactional language on a free trial offer impacted ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing transactional language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in website visits.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2021
When reviewing ads for a free trial offer, we wondered if the language “no credit card needed” was a motivating factor for people to sign up or if it was distancing potential visitors by creating a more transactional mindset and reducing the appeal and overall value of the offer. We wanted to test removing the “no credit card needed” language to see how it impacted clicks on the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we thought that removing transactional language would come across as a more humanized approach and personal in nature, what we found was that removing the "no credit card needed" language caused a 25.9% decrease in traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence. What this data shows us is this language actually decreases anxiety and removes friction from the process, making it a lower perceived amount of effort to accept the offer because people are not worried about having to remember to cancel their free trial at a later date to avoid being charged any membership fees. Because we achieved valid results, we were able to take this learning from a Facebook ad and apply it to other areas and entry points where a free trial is offered on the website and add in this language to increase conversion rates.

experiments.title: How the way an offer is presented impacts name and donor conversion

rows.name: From Anger to Affection - Video Series
From Anger to Affection - Online Course

experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of language is more appealing to people for the content offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2019 - 06/05/2019
Focus on the Family has developed video series to use as new name and donor acquisition offers. These offers are promoted through paid Facebook ads that lead people to an acquisition page and an instant donation ask. They have called these offers “video series”. These 4-6 session video series also include key discussion points for each session/lesson and Scripture memory verses to help reinforce the content that people consume and learn.
NextAfter has developed similar offers like these for other organizations, but call them, “online courses”.
In an effort to optimize these offers for Focus on the Family, and learn what is most appealing and motivating to prospective names and donors, we proposed testing the kind of language used to promote and describe them. The control group used “video series” through out the acquisition funnel and the treatment group used “online course” in all of the copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we hypothesized that the "online course" would increase donor conversion because an online course has a higher perceived value than a video series, we found that the "online course" language actually decreased donor conversion by 25%. In addition to this, we also saw a 5% decrease in names acquired with the "online course" language. The "online course" version didn't perform as well as the "video series" one because people may have actually been too intimidated by the thought of taking an online course. Calling something a "video series" probably has a higher appeal and feels more accessible to more people than an online course does. This resulted in less people signing up for the online course and even less people converting as donors.

The video series acquisition offers have been a huge success and driver of new names and donors for Focus on the Family. The learnings of this experiment are incredibly valuable for the video series they already have developed and the asserted effort and resources they are putting in to making more video series to use for acquisition in the next year.

This approach is also something we'd like to test with other organizations who are offering "online courses" to see if the results would be consistent.

experiments.title: How inline acquisition offers in place of ads affected clickthrough
experiments.researchQuestion: Will replacing the mid-roll advertising banner with an inline acquisition increase clicks for this placement?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2017 - 11/09/2017
Focus on the Family currently uses DoubleClick for Publishers to manage ad fill throughout their website.  Most of the ads in rotation are for their own resources, giving opportunities, and some third-party offers. One such placement is a mid-roll banner targeting long-form article pages.  They hypothesized that an inline email acquisition offer that is contextually relevant for their audience may increase clickthrough and help capture more of the high volume of new visitors viewing these articles.
They already had a free online Marriage Assessment which has resonated well with visitors to their marriage and parenting content, so they split the traffic to articles in those categories and delivered an inline offer for the assessment in place of the mid-roll ad from DoubleClick to half the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inline treatment increased the number of offer clicks by over 1,870%! This learning shows how the alignment of an offer, as well as its treatment, can be put to work to acquire more names. We hypothesize that a large portion of this impact is due to “banner blindness”—a web usability phenomenon where visitors consciously or subconsciously ignore banner-like information.  Banners also limit the amount of value proposition you can effectively communicate, which can decrease perceived value.
With high traffic and known content themes, we plan to test further into this placement—including moving it to the end of the content where we hypothesize we may be able to capitalize on the natural pivot/decision point created by completing the article.  Other potential tests include the headline, button, and value proposition copy as well as the inclusion of supporting graphical elements.

experiments.title: The impact of clarifying why we need their email address on a pledge form
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to the form around the email field for visitors to our page will achieve a greater email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2021
For our primary pledge acquisition offer we wanted to test whether adding additional copy around the email field would clarify for the visitor why we needed their email address and lead to greater conversions.
We decided to add, (get updates about the fight against abortion and infanticide) next to the email field.
experiments.keyLearnings: People were far less motivated to complete the form when we added that copy. We believe that putting that additional text next to the email field caused people to think less about the pledge they were signing and more about a subscription for "updates," decreasing the conversion rate. It shifted the value proposition from standing against infanticide to email updates. We will explore other ways of letting people know they will get emails from us without decreasing conversion.

experiments.title: How making an end-user click “See More” in a Facebook ad impacts click-through to the acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would increase click-through and ultimately name acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 09/07/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. They use paid Facebook ads to push the offer. Through previous testing, we had learned that longer Facebook ad copy increases name acquisition. To build upon those learnings, we tested ad copy that was even longer resulting in the end-user having to click “See More” to read the entirety of it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version with the extended copy increased click-through by 82%! What this tell us is that people are not only more highly engaged with long ad copy, but even longer ad copy requiring people to click “See More” increases engagement and results in more people clicking through to the landing page and converting. The “See More” version provided additional information that the end-user needed to make the decision to click-through to the landing page and ultimately sign-up for the course.
