experiments.title: How changing the CTA button on a Facebook ad to download now impacted email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a download now button for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2022 - 12/31/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
During the holiday season in 2021 we ran a test where we changed the CTA button on a Facebook ad promoting a specific ebook from ‘learn more’ to ‘download now.’ Our hypothesis was since we were asking people to download the ebook on the landing page by providing their email address, using a ‘download now’ CTA button would reduce the friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: By simply changing the CTA button to download now, Hoover had a 48.3% increase in email acquisition rates, with a 99.9% level of confidence. Tying a download of an ebook into the button copy made a significant impact  for Hoover. This is a test we should continue running on multiple offers.

experiments.title: How increasing the number of hyperlinks in an email affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing the number of links in an email increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2016 - 01/16/2017
The treatment produced a 27% increase in clickthrough rate, which tells us a few things about our readers. First, it tells us that including more links incentivizes more of them to click, or take the first initial action at the moment that the value proposition hits home. Secondly, it tells us that we had previously been losing potential donors who didn’t make it all the way through the copy to the call-to-action.
This points to an important principle: our donors and prospects have varying levels of motivation. Some are ready to give and need very little convincing, while others are more deliberative and might need more copy to be incentivized to click. Typically, we attempt to address these varying levels of motivation through multiple emails, but this experiment points to the opportunity that we might be able to reach multiple levels of motivation in the same communication.
experiments.keyLearnings:
CaringBridge had always included a “focused link” in their email appeals, which gave readers a single link to click to take action. Historically, this had been a button, but previous testing had shown that a text link performed better.
They wanted to test the effect of giving donors more places to click throughout the email—essentially linking up any text that contained a CTA, so if the donor was motivated, they could take action right then. They produced a treatment that had the exact same copy, but included more clickable links.

experiments.title: How a concealing headline affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a headline that teases the central issue of an offer increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
The Hoover Institution recently launched a new eBook offer titled, ‘Make America Exceptional Again.’ As they began to advertise this offer on Facebook, they wondered: Will a headline that teases the central issue of the eBook increase email acquisition? They set up a treatment page and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Hoover Institution found that this treatment version did, in fact, increase email acquisition rate by 30%. It’s clear the second headline increase page visitor motivation. As we look at the difference between these two headlines, we can see that the first headline promises to offer a solution to a very specific problem. The second headline, however, focuses on a broader, more concealed problem–as if the reader was being let in on a secret. It also doesn’t offer an immediate solution.
For these reasons, it’s likely that the visitors who visited the second version of the page felt more motivation to ‘find out’ the answer. Further testing will be conducted on this page, as well as the second ‘Thank you’ page to help improve overall offer performance.

experiments.title: How adding an in-line dear reader on blog and article pages impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line content offer for blog and article visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 11/06/2022
Content offers for TGC were being run as display ads on the site. There were also email capture forms in the footer of each page offering people a relevant newsletter subscription based on the content they were consuming. But with high volume traffic to blog and article pages, we wondered if including an in-line free content offer that included the eBook cover image, some light value proposition, and a CTA would increase the number of emails acquired from website visitors. We split traffic for this experiment 25/75 with 75% of visitors receiving the treatment which included a dear reader at the end of the article or blog.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found after running this experiment for 1 week was that there was a 27,919% increase in email conversion rate with 100% level of confidence. We saw valid increases across every channel, every device type, and every visitor segment. We quickly learned that web visitors responded well to this free content offer when it was featured in a highly visible placement on the web pages they were visiting. We have rolled the dear reader out to 100% of traffic and have several additional test opportunities to move forward on now that we have installed this feature. In the future, we want to test the impact of embedding the form itself so people can enter their name and email address directly on the site without clicking over to the landing page. We also want to test this against different content offers available so we can have something to offer for returning visitors who have already received this specific ebook.
Our most notable increases were for desktop visitors and returning visitors:
Desktop visitors had a 12,780.6% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 16,432.8% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increasing landing page congruence affects cost per subscriber
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis was that by increasing congruence (or visual consistency) between the landing page and the ad, we could improve landing page conversion and lower the cost per subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2014 - 12/25/2014
The Hoover Institution wanted to add new subscribers to its highly-regarded military history and foreign policy newsletter Strategika. Facebook ad targeting allowed them to find new subscribers by targeting these specific interests, but after a few weeks of the campaign, cost-per-subscriber was still higher than original projections.
The landing page did a good job selling Strategika — it constructed a strong value proposition, showed plenty of credibility, and made a clear call-to-action that highlighted all of the benefits of subscription. But we hypothesized we could increase the conversion rate on the landing page by making a single simple change — making the landing page look more like the ad.
The ad had a historic picture of American soldiers storming a beachhead in World War 2. We changed the landing page to have this same background, and shortened the copy to fit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did this new page with increased congruence convert 40% more people to subscribers, because Facebook was optimizing for converisons,  it lowered the cost per subscriber by 86% — a huge savings that allowed Hoover Institution to effectively acquire nearly twice as many subscribers with their original budget.

experiments.title: How a survey question in a Facebook Ad impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that by using a survey-style ad (with a “Yes/No” question included in the ad image) will increase clicks on the ad so much, that we can generate more emails at a more efficient cost.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2022
As a part of our own marketing and prospecting via Facebook, we decided to try a new survey-style ad image to see if we could generate a much higher volume of ad clicks, and subsequently from that extra traffic, convert more people on the subsequent landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed a decrease in email signups by -57.8%.

Our hypothesis completely failed with our audiences on Facebook Advertising, because this decrease in email signups was actually driven by fewer clicks overall (we saw that this treatment ad decreased ad clicks by -53.6%, also with a confidence level of 100%).

Ultimately, we'd like to re-try this experiment, but next time with a different question in the treatment ad. With evidence that these style of ads work well in other cases, the thought here is that perhaps the question is too vague. Furthermore, could the length of the question also be impacting ad clickthrough rates?

Would asking a more simple question (like: "Do you want to get more from your Facebook advertising?") get straight to the point, and resonate with a larger portion of the audience? It's worth testing, so we will soon.

experiments.title: How replacing a static image with a video on a Facebook ad affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ad in video format will increase click-through rates for Facebook users by better catching their attention and better communicating the value one receives by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2022 - 10/18/2022
Many of Dallas Theological Seminary’s Facebook ads for their free online courses are similar, using a color-rich background including the names of the courses and the teachers who present them. In an effort to combat low click through rates, we hypothesized that although viewers were seeing ads for different courses, because these ads looked so similar, it was causing viewers to assume they were seeing an ad for the same course time and time again, eventually prompting them to tune the ads out.
We also wondered if the static ads were inadequately communicating the value one could receive by enrolling in one of Dallas Theological Seminary’s free online courses. We wanted to know if we could increase click-through rates by presenting a video ad that would 1) Catch viewers attention and 2) Better communicate the value of registration.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad media, splitting traffic between he control and treatment over a span of 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 93% LIFT in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that a more interactive media format would result in more viewers clicking the ad. We believe that this treatment was successful for two primary reasons:
First, we believe that the new media format broke up the monotony of similarly formatted ads being shown to the same people. A different ad from a familiar organization, we believe, cued viewers that a new offer was being advertised.
Second, we believe that the video allowed viewers to see for themselves the quality of the coursework and the expertise of the teacher (these courses are adapted from on-campus seminary lectures) which increased the perceived value of the offer by improving its credibility and exclusivity, which likely provided more incentive for the viewer to click.
Moving forward, we will attempt to gather more video samples that we can use to promote more courses in their catalog.

experiments.title: How video ads affect email acquisition rate for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: Will video ads produce an increase in email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2017 - 03/03/2017
DTS was promoting their new course, called Can You Trust the Bible?. They had a special video from their Academic Dean to promote it and wanted to test using this video to promote the course. Video ads had shown in some experiments to improve conversion for offers, although DTS had previously used static ads.
They split audiences and tested each ad unit to see which resulted in a better cost-per-acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video ads produced a 37% decrease in conversion when tested against the static ads. While a deeper look at the data showed that many more people engaged with the video ads (which means they clicked somewhere on the ad), the video ad was either too distracting or not compelling enough to convince them to take the next step and sign up for the course. This seems to indicate that, in this case, video is more of a distraction than a motivator to take the course.
This doesn’t entirely rule out video ads on Facebook. There have been experiments where video ads produce a lift, and they can be effective at remarketing. DTS will continue to test video as a promotional tool for their free courses.

experiments.title: How a value proposition built upon “what” instead of “why” impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase email acquisition by limiting friction and emphasizing the value to the visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2016 - 02/15/2016
The treatment ended up decreasing email acquisition by 6%. Our hypothesis is that this was not caused by reducing friction or emphasizing the value to the visitor but because we removed the “why” behind the course and focused solely on the “what.”
The paragraph removed talked about the mission of DTS and it allowed visitors to the page to align around this same mission and mentally put themselves in the same audience. Those that felt they belonged would be more likely to take the desired action of signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary had recently launched their first online course, a study of the Gospel of John. The course was offered for free in exchange for a visitor’s name, email, and address. With significant traffic being sent to the site, we wanted to find the best way to communicate the value to the visitors.
Using the learnings from experiments run with similar organization, we tried applying two key principles to the copy on the page:
We emphasized what the visitor would get by subscribing instead of what they would have to do
We reduced the friction associated with the process by removing copy that we felt was unnecessary to the conversion.
We then tested these two pages against each other and monitored results.

experiments.title: How a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ Link Affects Email Unsubscribe Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a ‘Campaign Opt-Out’ link Decrease Email Unsubscribe Rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2017 - 09/13/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was running a campaign for North Texas Giving Day, a huge fundraising opportunity each year for North Texas nonprofits. As they planned for this campaign, they wanted to give their audience a way to remove themselves from further communication about North Texas Giving Day if they weren’t able to join this year. They wondered: If we allow individuals to ‘opt-out’ of a campaign, will the unsubscribe rate decrease? They created a treatment with one line of copy and a campaign ‘Opt-out’ link for viewers to click, and then they launched an A/B split test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version decreased the unsubscribe rate by 33%. It’s clear that this treatment reduced the friction and anxiety of the email viewer by offering a clear alternative option for dismissing themselves from this campaign communication. This test shows us that some individuals may not want to unsubscribe from all communication, but when they are left with no other option, they will.
By providing a clear and direct way for viewers to ‘raise their hand’ and decide that they won’t be able to join in a fundraising campaign, we can decrease our email attrition rate and keep a wider audience engaged for a longer period of time with the mission of our organization–in this case, the equipping of Christian leaders.

We were pushing out a Christmas Devotional offer and wanted to re-send to any recipients who had not yet opened or clicked on the original offer to download. The control copy was short and to the point so we wanted to test whether adding additional language—highlighting the value of the resource and emphasizing why this offer would be of particular use to the recipient—would impact clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the treatment copy actually reduced clicks by 20%. While the treatment copy uses a more humanized approach and introduces a personal recommendation, the call to action changed from "Click below to get your 2020 Christmas Devotional" to "Let me know what you think!". In future experiments, we would want to test including the communal-style language in the body copy while keeping the call to action at the end of the email succinct and clear.

They also tested a new headline that strengthened the value proposition of the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment decreased conversion by 67%. The removal of the scripture reference from the treatment copy likely had a significant impact here, as this was the main reason the “new” control won in the first place. Also, the headline in the control was much more instantly gratifying to the end user rather than the headline that emphasized the work that goes into strengthening their prayer life.

experiments.title: How the subject line of an email affects open rate

rows.name: It will be given to you

experiments.researchQuestion: Does mystery or “generosity” better motivate subscribers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2017 - 11/30/2017
Buckner International sent out an email before Giving Tuesday that let readers know to watch their email for a gift they would be sending them tomorrow. We decided to do a subject line test to see if we could learn more about what motivated Buckner subscribers. For this campaign, we wanted to test whether a mysterious subject line would perform better than one that indicated something was going to be given to them.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this experiment, the subject line that increased a sense of mystery (“Coming tomorrow”) resulted in an increase in opens by 13%. This would seem to indicate that the subscriber base is a bit suspicious of “gifts” being given to them via email. The mystery of “tomorrow” was more motivating to them.

experiments.title: How focusing on the benefits of the offer impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will focusing on the benefits of the action the offer promotes increase email acquisition for a biblically focused offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 07/31/2017
This test teaches us the importance of testing each of the reasons/needs for someone to engage in an offer.
While the control’s benefit is perfectly fine, it is limited in value according to this audience. By connecting the offer to a deeper, more powerful value/desire in the audience (i.e. leaving no stone unturned for your kids versus making prayer more interesting/less boring), more people responded.
As a result, the new ad achieved a 79% increase in emails and reduced cost per email by 43%.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International had been running a devotional offer for parents as a means of email acquisition for roughly 2 months. We had performed a series of experiments to boost the performance of the campaign but none had given us the kind of lift that we look for on a campaign like this.

We noticed that the cost per email had begun to rise over time. Instead of turning off the campaign, we decided to try out one more experiment. In this instance, we wanted to see if we could reframe the offer to highlight the benefits of prayer itself (not just the benefits of the offer) and incorporating actual scripture into the ad.

experiments.title: How “free” language affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the word “free” increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2018 - 03/27/2018
Hoover Institution leveraged a free eBook to grow the size of their email file. They were curious about the language used in the ads—the control ad had a call-to-action of “Get the facts on healthcare now”, but they knew from previous experiments that the word “free” was a powerful motivator. They set up a second ad with the call-to-action “Get the free eBook now” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new ads with “free” in the call-t0-action produced a 20.7% decrease in clickthrough rate. There are at least two possible reasons for this:
The target audience is more interested in something with perceived authority and factual basis than something that is free, possibly due to the intellectual reputation of Hoover.The rest of the ad copy mentions "facts", so the "free" language might produce a break in congruency rather than a lift due to incentive.
While a valid decrease was confirmed in clickthrough rate, there was no statistically valid decrease in conversion rate, which means that additional testing is needed on the ad and landing page.

The simplified email resulted in a 14.8% increase to the percentage of visitors going to the landing page. Our hypothesis is that the lack of design seemed to be more genuine and less “marketing” oriented. Historically, the email template used in donor communications was heavily designed with an organizational wrapper. We wanted to see if sending the same email message but in a stripped-down design would have an impact on the percentage of people that clicked on the link to download the prayer book.

experiments.title: How the amount of value proposition on a page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the amount of value proposition on a page affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2016 - 12/09/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting the Index of Military Strength, a publication that gave insight into the U.S. government’s military spending and readiness. They put together a landing page to promote this publication, but had the feeling that their copy was too short and didn’t properly “sell” the offer.
They created a second page that listed out many additional details about the Index, expanding the value proposition. Though the copy was much longer and it pushed the email acquisition form down the page and below the fold, they hypothesized that it would result in increased signups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The added value proposition increased email acquisition rate by 25.6%, which meant many more emails were acquired for the same amount of ad spend. This reiterated the principle that, although a free offer doesn't have a price, visitors must be sold on an offer all the way up to the point that they accept it by submitting the form.

This prompted more testing to add more evidentials into the page that supported the additional claims of value.

experiments.title: How Imagery Affects Conversion in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding additional clarity to the Facebook ad image increase course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2017 - 10/18/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign for a new online course, we wondered if we needed to add more clarity to the Facebook ad image. In order to more clearly depict the title and purpose of the course, Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we added the Facebook logo leading towards a dollar icon on the ad image.
This experiment was conducted with the Network for Good audience ad set.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new Facebook ad image led to a 68% decrease in course registration, despite appearing to ad more clarity. Since the control ad had previously been running, it’s possible that the additional social proof from likes and shares contributed to it’s better performance; although, likes and share don’t always make that significant of difference.

It could also be that the new imagery, although more accurate and clear, made the course look similar to other marketing courses out there that appear gimmicky or too good to be true.

experiments.title: Which email sign up headline produces the highest conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What email value proposition will produce the most email sign ups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2015 - 08/28/2015
Every once in a while we get around to running tests on our own website to make sure NextAfter is practicing what we preach. So we set up two different email signup pages encouraging visitors to get the This vs. That weekly email which showcases one of our latest test results. The goal was to see which email sign up value proposition would drive the most conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test is another example of how clarity trumps persuasion. Clearly stating what a person is signing up for, getting a test each week, is a better description than talking about how a person will benefit. The surprising aspect to us was that even with the low traffic to the page the test validated.

The copy used to promote offers is the most important element in getting someone to say yes. Just throwing something up on the page and not testing it would have seriously affected the number of email sign-ups the page generated.

experiments.title: How ad creative affected clickthrough rate and follow-on conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does ad copy that incentivizes a specific action outperform ad copy that highlights “exclusive access”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2015 - 10/19/2015
While the difference in clickthrough rate between the two ads was nonexistent, there was a massive disparity in conversion rate. The ad that incentivized visitors to see the video now resulted in 90% more conversions than the one that highlighted exclusive access. This proves the point that we must be measuring every layer of the conversion process to determine the impact of ad creative.
experiments.keyLearnings: Heritage Action was running a campaign to acquire email addresses by leveraging exclusive video content from one of their events. They tested two ads against each other with identical creative but differing calls-to-action. One used the CTA “Watch the video now”, while the other said “Get Exclusive Access Now”. The two ads had an identical clickthrough rate of 3.8%, so we dug deeper to see if the ad creative (which went to the same landing page) resulted in different conversion rates after the click.

This name acquisition page was part of a larger fundraising campaign to acquire and convert new donors for the organization. This test helped refine the overall messaging of the campaign prior to a full-scale launch to the entire email file.

experiments.title: How authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does increased authority, active language, and clarity affect email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 11/09/2017
Leadership Institute recently re-launched a course about the history and principles of conservatism, called Conservatism 101. As they prepared to market this course through Facebook, they developed four different treatments for the advertisements. Testing proved that the third and fourth treatment produced a significant lift to clickthrough rate, but they wanted to gather more data to validate if one of these resulted in more downstream conversions—in this case, more sign-ups for the course.
The primary difference in the two treatments was the image and the copy within. The first treatment had a patriotic background that described what the course was: “Conservatism 101: An Introduction to the American Conservative Tradition”. The second, however, featured a picture of Mark Levin, a popular radio host and lecturer in the course. This image contained call-to-action copy as well: “Join Mark Levin in this new free course, Conservatism 101.”
The team isolated these two ads to determine which ad was more effective at driving downstream conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The second treatment, with Mark Levin and the call-to-action copy in the image produced a 63.7% increase in downstream conversions. This showed that the increased authority and active call to action was able to shape motivation to affect downstream actions. Ultimately, Leadership Institute didn’t just want more clicks—they wanted more clicks with a higher motivation.

This test prompted a new round of testing that used the winner as the control to continue to increase results.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/09/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below reflect are from the ad set targeting the Network for Good Facebook interest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this audience, the result was a resounding “No.” The Network for Good audience had a 75% decrease in course registrations when shown the bullet points ad.

Each post had a comparable amount of clicks to the landing page, implying that people who viewed the control were more primed to complete their registration. It’s possible that by adding these bullet points, it made it easier for someone to skim through the post and not experience the full value proposition of the offer.

This learning should not be taken out of context and applied to every audience. Other ad sets targeting different audience segments are approaching validity with the Bullet Point ad as the winning treatment.

experiments.title: How third-party validation affects email acquisition for a free digital offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity and adding social proof increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2016 - 07/01/2016
The Heritage Foundation was giving away a digital version of their popular 475-page Guide to the Constitution in exchange for an email address. They had a landing page that was converting 58% of visitors. But this meant that 42% of visitors weren’t downloading the digital Guide, so their team wanted to try four tweaks to increase conversion.
One confusing part of the offer was that the digital version of the Guide was in the form of a microsite, so they were really offering “access” rather than a digital download. This caused potential confusion to the user, so they marketed the offer as a digital download (since there was an available PDF).
Then, they launched an A/B test to see if the new page increased conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new treatment converted 65.4% of visitors to accept the offer -- an increase of 11.2%! This shows that the new landing page tweaks did in fact increase clarity and add some social proof that helped convince the visitor to take action.

While this is a great lift and results in more emails for the same spend, it still means that 35% of our traffic is not converting—which means more testing and optimization is needed!

While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: How making the phone number optional affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the phone number field optional for visitors to the Global Online Fundraising Scorecard page will achieve more report downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2021 - 02/19/2021
In this experiment, we had launched The Global Online Fundraising Scorecard in partnership with Salesforce.org. Salesforce often requires a phone number in order to follow-up with new contacts, but we wondered if we could actually get more total report downloads by making the phone number field optional.
The control used a required phone number field. The treatment used an optional phone number field.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The optional phone number field increases conversions by 20.6% at a 98% level of confidence. This is further evidence that requiring information that is "non-essential" to the visitor introduces friction that can keep someone from saying "yes" to your offer.
However, the caveat is that we ended up with fewer phone numbers from the treatment page.
The control version (required phone number) captured phone numbers for 30% of page traffic. The treatment (optional phone number) acquired the phone number for 16.3% of traffic.
A next step from this experiment would be to evaluate the quality of the phone numbers and follow-up calls from the "Required" version versus the "Optional." Intuition would say that phone numbers given willingly would be more receptive to a phone call. But phone numbers given when it was required would be less receptive. However, more analysis will be needed to determine this.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2020
For our bi-weekly digest email where we send through a few different links and resources related to online fundraising, we wanted to see how we could increase engagement (opens) and also, hopefully, increase the number of clicks. We’ve tested a number of different subject lines like personalization but, generally speaking, we default to something that is very clear as to the utility someone would get from opening the email. So we wanted to see if a pretty unique, creative subject line would stand out more.
In this case, we used a treatment that made it seem like we forgot to create the subject line so it was like a note to ourselves to insert a great subject line. We used some square brackets [like this] to call it out further.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With almost a 100% LoC (99.9%) the unique, mystery subject line led to an increase in opens which more than anything underscores how changing up the type of subject lines used can help keep people engaged. In this case, the [square brackets] may have helped it stand out but our theory is the more humourous and unique element of the subject line helped lead to more opens.

Interestingly, with a true 100% LoC (100%) we saw a 93.3% increase in click rate and a 63.8% increase in click to open rate (99.9% LoC) with the unique, mysterious subject line. This underscores both the relationship between the open rate and click rate and how the subject line can influence a reader's motivation before they even get into the body/content of the email itself.

experiments.title: Will a shorter, more direct email lead to more free registrations?

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/17/2020
NextAfter’s annual conference — the Nonprofit Innovation & Optimization Summit — moved to a virtual event due to COVID-19 but the change also further delayed the launch and promotion. For the first launch email, we wondered if a shorter, more to the point email may actually do better in driving clicks, engagement, and registrations (free and paid) as a lot of people on our list we (assume) know the event, know it’s quality, and may even be expecting the announcement so we didn’t need to spend as much time in the email explaining the event and sharing its value.

experiments.title: How framing offer content in a Facebook ad impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that frames the offer content adequately increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2017 - 10/27/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their online course Revelation when they noticed an opportunity to test. In their original Facebook course promotion ad, they called on viewers to ‘study with one of DTS’ renowned professors, for free.’ Through previous message testing, they’ve seen that this is a strong value proposition for a lot of their online resources and material. However, they wondered: Will a stronger focus on the book being studied (i.e. Revelation) and its complexity increase motivation and email acquisition rate?
They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that their treatment version did increase email acquisition by 143%. It’s clear that offering more context to the book being studied in the course increased motivation of viewers on Facebook and drove far more course signups then the control version. While the original value proposition remains strong, it was much shorter in length.

This has prompted further message testing that focuses on individual course content, as well as the true value of learning from actual seminary professors for free.

				× 143.0% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects name acquisition conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a clarified value proposition and shorter copy increase name acquisition conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2016 - 08/08/2016
By clarifying the value proposition and removing friction in page length, we saw a 80.5% higher conversion rate. This confirmed out hypothesis that we could increase name acquisition conversions by clarifying the value proposition with a more direct call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of the Shoes for Orphan Souls campaign, Buckner International created a pledge for visitors to sign. The primary goal of the page was to collect email addresses. We hypothesized that the value proposition on the control landing page was too ambiguous so we set out to clarify the value proposition on the treatment, as well as shorten the copy.

On the control page the value proposition was captured in the headline “Protecting the Vulnerable and Lifting up the Fatherless”. This could have a variety of meanings to different visitors.  The treatment built on earlier learnings about the Christian motivations of typical Buckner donors and focused on the Biblical imperative to care for orphans: “Stand up for orphans and vulnerable children”.  This was a more direct and clarified value proposition and call to action.

experiments.title: How urgency impacts email acquisition rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that stresses the urgency of a course launch increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2017 - 10/16/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was making preparations to launch its new ‘mini’ course on the life and work of Martin Luther. They wondered: If we tell visitors to pre-enroll in this course by stressing the urgency of the upcoming launch date, will email acquisition increase? They launched an A/B test leading up to their launch date to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version that stressed the upcoming launch date did, in fact, increase email acquisition by 88%. By using the actual date of the launch (October 16, 2017) and the word ‘Monday’ in their call-to-action, Dallas Theological Seminary was able to signal to viewers that this was an urgent offer with a specific launch date. It likely made viewers feel that they were going to miss out on a particular event, even though the course was still offered after the launch date.
This strategy has prompted further tests, especially as Dallas Theological Seminary builds plans to launch new courses.

experiments.title: How a concealing headline affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a headline that teases the central issue of an offer increase email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2017 - 12/18/2017
The Hoover Institution recently launched a new eBook offer titled, ‘Make America Exceptional Again.’ As they began to advertise this offer on Facebook, they wondered: Will a headline that teases the central issue of the eBook increase email acquisition? They set up a treatment page and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Hoover Institution found that this treatment version did, in fact, increase email acquisition rate by 30%. It’s clear the second headline increase page visitor motivation. As we look at the difference between these two headlines, we can see that the first headline promises to offer a solution to a very specific problem. The second headline, however, focuses on a broader, more concealed problem–as if the reader was being let in on a secret. It also doesn’t offer an immediate solution.

For these reasons, it’s likely that the visitors who visited the second version of the page felt more motivation to ‘find out’ the answer. Further testing will be conducted on this page, as well as the second ‘Thank you’ page to help improve overall offer performance.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition in a Facebook webinar ad affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a value proposition centered around the need for a new fundraising model lead to more webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/28/2018
In this experiment, we wanted to see which value proposition focus would get the most webinar registrations for the NextAfter.com traffic audience. The control focused on the idea that organizations get cut out of the process when relying on Facebook’s fundraising tools. The treatment focused on the need for a new fundraising model that doesn’t rely on the typical “best practice” ideas and strategies.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment saw an 83.2% increase in conversion rate, and a lower cost per registration. This is helpful to know as we continue to develop and market resources centered around using Facebook for fundraising – it will cause us to focus more on the specific contrast between our Facebook model and the typical approaches.

experiments.title: How the use of an illustrated image over a real photo in a Facebook ad can impact click-through and acquisition
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad would acquire more email addresses
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2017 - 05/09/2017
People were not only more motivated to click on the illustrated image over the real photo in the Facebook ad, but once they got to the landing page they were more highly motivated to get the offer as well. There was a 66.6% increase in emails acquired with the illustrated image over the real photo. The increase in click-through and acquisition can be attributed to the call-to-action being reinforced on the image, the congruency of the image used in the ad and the imagery on the acquisition page and because the image was more centric to the faith of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Missionary Oblates developed a Novena prayer in honor of Mother’s Day. They promoted this content offer through paid Facebook ads in an effort to acquire new names. We traditionally use images of real people in Facebook ads, but we wanted to test the image of the ad with something more illustrated. The illustrated image was also the same image used on the cover of the Novena prayers and acquisition page.

experiments.title: How congruence between ad and landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing congruence affect course signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
The new ad that used the same imagery as the landing page increased conversion by 47.6%. This shows that congruence is a powerful, if often subliminal tool. While the ad image might not be something that the visitor actively thinks about, the repetition of the imagery throughout the funnel reminds them subconsciously that the elements are all linked.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their second course, Genesis, on Facebook. They wanted to emphasize the academic nature of the course, so they launched ads that showed a person taking the course while studying. But they hypothesized that they might be able to increase conversion by employing the principle known as congruency: aligning each element of a marketing funnel to increase response.
They pulled the image of the Sistine Chapel from the landing page and put it into the ad, and launched an A/B test to see if they were able to affect conversion.

experiments.title: How the right offer affects emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer in the footer of the NextAfter website will lead to the greatest number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2015 - 11/10/2015
When we first launched the new NextAfter.com website, we had some internal debate about the best offer to use in the footer of the site. Historically, we had offered a subscription to our weekly newsletter, This vs. That, which allowed subscribers to receive one experiment per week from the Research Library.
Since this was our main website, we hypothesized that maybe the more general offer of “Staying Informed” would work better for the visitors. We decided to set up a test that would allow us to evaluate these two different offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: We discovered that the “Stay Informed” offer led to an infinitely better number of emails captured (none versus some is by definition infinitely better). While the This vs. That newsletter is still one of our more popular offers, a more general contact form was a better fit given the motivation of the people visiting the website.

experiments.title: How increasing landing page congruence affects cost per subscriber
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis was that by increasing congruence (or visual consistency) between the landing page and the ad, we could improve landing page conversion and lower the cost per subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2014 - 12/25/2014
The Hoover Institution wanted to add new subscribers to its highly-regarded military history and foreign policy newsletter Strategika. Facebook ad targeting allowed them to find new subscribers by targeting these specific interests, but after a few weeks of the campaign, cost-per-subscriber was still higher than original projections.
The landing page did a good job selling Strategika — it constructed a strong value proposition, showed plenty of credibility, and made a clear call-to-action that highlighted all of the benefits of subscription. But we hypothesized we could increase the conversion rate on the landing page by making a single simple change — making the landing page look more like the ad.
The ad had a historic picture of American soldiers storming a beachhead in World War 2. We changed the landing page to have this same background, and shortened the copy to fit.
experiments.keyLearnings: Not only did this new page with increased congruence convert 40% more people to subscribers, because Facebook was optimizing for converisons,  it lowered the cost per subscriber by 86% — a huge savings that allowed Hoover Institution to effectively acquire nearly twice as many subscribers with their original budget.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 39.2% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How using an email forward can affect response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will retargeting a “non-opens” audience segment with an email forward result in a higher response rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2016 - 10/27/2016
When promoting an upcoming NextAfter live broadcast, we wanted to retarget those who did not open the initial email inviting them to the event.
We wondered if creating an email that had the appearance of an email forward could help make the resend email feel even more like a personal follow-up, and prompt more people sign up for the live broadcast.
experiments.keyLearnings: The email forward saw a 64.9% drop in response rate. Our hypotheses is that the email forward gave the appearance of being spam, thus resulting in a lower response rate.

experiments.title: How clarity in the design of a Facebook ad can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad design will acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 09/21/2016
When promoting the Why Should I Give to You? research study on nonprofit value proposition, we came to the realization that our Facebook ads were a little bit hard to read. The yellow text against the light gray background seemed to get lost when scrolling through a Facebook feed.
This led us to the hypothesis that a clearer design would yield at least a greater conversion rate. As a result, we developed a treatment version of the ad that presented the same information, but with a clearer design that would stand out more in the Facebook newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 56.7% decrease in conversion rate. Initially, this was very confusing. How could a design that appeared so much stronger be so ineffective by comparison? We almost didn’t even run a test on this new design because we were so certain it would outperform the control.

Taking a second look, we realized that the slight change of phrasing in order to improve the design aesthetic may have decreased the clarity of the content of the offer.

There are two main learnings we can glean from this test:
There are no marketing experts, design experts, advertising experts, etc. There is only experience. And the only way to know what is more effective is to test
 	Language and copy that makes sense to you doesn’t always make sense to your audience. In this case, the copy in the treatment design made complete sense to us, yet it appears to not have been quite as clear to the Facebook audience.

experiments.title: How removing the call-to-action at the top of a landing page impacts name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the design and navigational elements increase the visitors getting to the assessment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 10/30/2017
The treatment decreased the amount of people getting to the assessment by 28.6%! While we hypothesized that the simplified page would help decrease friction and motivate more people to click-through because the focus of the page was on the value proposition, we learned that:
People coming to this page were already highly motivated to take the assessment. The additional value proposition focus wasn’t needed to convert these people.
By removing the call-to-action at the top of the page, we made it harder for these highly motivated people to click-through to the actual assessment.
We recommend building upon these results and learnings and continue testing separate elements on this page to get a better understanding of the motivation of the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family has a Marriage Assessment on their website to help couples grow and strengthen their marriage. Before an end-user gets to the actual assessment there is a landing page with value proposition and a call-to-action. In an effort to increase conversion on this page of people getting to the assessment, they developed a treatment. They hypothesized that by simplifying the page – including removing the call-to-action at the top of the page – they might be able to motivate more people to take it by leading with the value proposition of the assessment. The goal of the treatment was to eliminate any elements on the page that would cause potential friction in the conversion process. They split the traffic coming to the assessment page and tested the control against the treatment.

experiments.title: How adding an additional newsletter opt-in checkbox affects form completion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an extra form field checkbox affect email acquisition rate on a petition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2017 - 09/19/2017
The treatment, with the additional checkbox at the end of the form, produced a 9.6% decrease in email acquisition rate. The team at Heritage decided, after analyzing the results, that this decrease was acceptable, since 63% of people who signed the treatment page also opted into the Morning Bell—which resulted in several hundred new subscribers with increased value.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Heritage Foundation was running an open letter to grow its email file and motivate the Senate to take action on judicial appointments. While this offer was running, they performed an analysis that found that subscribers to their Morning Bell were more likely to become donors. They decided to add a checkbox to the form to allow signers of the open letter to opt in to the Morning Bell. They hypothesized that it would decrease conversion, but the data showed that as long as the decrease was slight, the increased value of the added subscribers would more than make up for it. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.

Getting a phone number from a new name is very valuable to the organization. They wanted to test if the conversion rate of the page would be impacted if they asked for a person’s phone number.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding a phone number field to the acquisition form decreased conversion by 36%. Even though the field wasn’t required, the perceived perception of having this field on the form caused cognitive friction to the acquisition process resulting in a significant amount of people abandoning the form and not signing up for the online course.

experiments.summary:

The treatment decreased the amount of people getting to the assessment by 28.6%! While we hypothesized that the simplified page would help decrease friction and motivate more people to click-through because the focus of the page was on the value proposition, we learned that:
People coming to this page were already highly motivated to take the assessment. The additional value proposition focus wasn’t needed to convert these people.
By removing the call-to-action at the top of the page, we made it harder for these highly motivated people to click-through to the actual assessment.
We recommend building upon these results and learnings and continue testing separate elements on this page to get a better understanding of the motivation of the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family has a Marriage Assessment on their website to help couples grow and strengthen their marriage. Before an end-user gets to the actual assessment there is a landing page with value proposition and a call-to-action. In an effort to increase conversion on this page of people getting to the assessment, they developed a treatment. They hypothesized that by simplifying the page – including removing the call-to-action at the top of the page – they might be able to motivate more people to take it by leading with the value proposition of the assessment. The goal of the treatment was to eliminate any elements on the page that would cause potential friction in the conversion process. They split the traffic coming to the assessment page and tested the control against the treatment.

experiments.title: How clarity in the call-to-action affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action with a compelling video increase course signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2017 - 02/19/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course called “Can You Trust the Bible?” They had shot a video of their Academic Dean explaining the course, and wanted to see how it performed against a direct invitation from the President to join the course. So they created two entrance paths to the course. The first was their control—a direct ask, via email, from the President of DTS. The second was an invitation from the President with a call-to-action to “watch the video to learn more about the course”. They hypothesized that the softer call-to-action would drive more traffic to the landing page, and the short video with a clear call-to-action would produce more overall conversions.
They split their file and closely monitored the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 32.6% decrease in signups from the control. This shows that the softer ask does not produce more motivation to sign up for the course, even with the added benefit of the video. There could be a few factors at play here. First, all clicks are not created equal—a click to “learn more”, in this case, is far less valuable than a click to “sign up”. Second, video might actually be obscuring the value proposition by not allowing the user to read it in their own time. Many people can read much faster than a video can communicate information, which leads them to be more likely to accept an offer where they control the cadence of the value proposition.
This does not necessarily mean, however, that the video doesn’t have a place in the marketing schedule of a new course—it may just mean that it needs to be placed later in the schedule to motivate users who were not incentivized by the original message.

experiments.title: How an email acquisition opportunity is presented on the website impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a slide in email acquisition offer impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2021 - 02/23/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The copy and and trigger timing of the pop-up has been tested in the past with little to no impact on conversion, so we hypothesized that the placement of the feature may impact conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that functioned more as a slide in feature on the bottom of the page than a forced pop-up. No copy or design of the feature was changed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant decrease in donor conversion from the slide in feature compared to the forced pop-up. With an almost 41% decrease in emails acquired, we can learn that the placement of the offer does have an impact on the number of people signing up to stay connected with Save the Children. The pop-up is almost much more disruptive - drawing attention to the feature and capturing more names than the slide in.
Continued testing is needed to really understand the motivation of this audience and what elements of friction exist in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How reducing friction on a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in petition signatures.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/19/2022 - 10/09/2022
When reviewing a petition landing page for possible barriers, we noticed there was a conflicting call to action on the page standing in the way of the email acquisition form: Click here for a printable version. We were curious if the link to the PDF was distracting users from completing the online form and was interrupting the cognitive forward momentum of page visitors. We designed a simple treatment: removing the interfering call to action from the landing page. We split traffic from 2 petitions 50/50 to this experience to observe what would happen to our email conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that reducing friction by removing the printable PDF link and call to action resulted in a 4.1% increase in email conversion rate with 99.4% level of confidence. Taking out this one step in the process led us to conversion lifts among all devices and all age segments of our audience. However, we also noticed a notable shift on a downstream metric that should be considered. Donations experienced a 48.5% decrease with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to run additional tests of this same concept on other petitions to see if these results are consistent. Due to this shift in donor response, it's possible that the printed version of the PDF is provided some sense of credibility to petition signers that is leading them to be more willing to convert on the instant donation page that follows it. If this is the case, we will need to test into an alternate method of displaying the option to access a printable version that does not interrupt the flow of the page or prohibit people from signing the petition.

experiments.title: How an “us vs. them” copy angle on a petition landing page impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an “us” vs. “them” copy angle in which we isolate the opposition and unite against the opposition for potential petition signers will achieve an increase in sign up rate and conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/02/2022 - 10/01/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition is designed to build momentum towards a free and independent Texas, calling out important fundamental beliefs among Texans and asking people to stand behind them by signing the petition. The petition landing page was experiencing a low sign up rate, and we wondered if the lead in copy to the petition was lacking motivation.
We tested an “us vs. them” copy angle against the control copy which was more organization-centric and focused on the importance of keeping Texas true to its values as more and more people migrate to the state. For the treatment, the “us” is loyal conservative Texans and the “them” is politicians, policymakers, and progressives working against “us”. We thought calling out the “bad guy” and stating how we’re united against the “bad guy” would enhance motivation and convince more people to sign the petition. We also thought the motivation would carry over to the instant donation page and have a positive impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Sign ups (email acqusition) showed little difference between the control and treatment; the treatment saw a 2.3% decrease in sign ups with only a 50.7% level of confidence. However, the treatment saw a valid 74.4% decrease in donations. 11 donations were made to the control compared to only 3 to the treatment. This tells us that any motivation created from the "us" vs. "them" copy angle did not carry over to the donation page.
Interestingly, mobile users made all 11 donations to the control and the treatment actually saw more donations among desktop users. Mobile also had far more samples than desktop. This indicates that mobile users may not have been turned off by the "us" vs. "them" copy angle, but that they preferred briefer copy as they were reaching the actual petition. This experiment may be worth testing again, but with a briefer version of the treatment. If we do retest and the control wins again, then it would be more clear that the control copy angle is more impactful than the treatment copy angle.

experiments.title: How adding clarity in the headline impacted email acquisition

rows.name: Digital Discipleship Landing Page Headline B

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing clarity and appeal in the headline for landing page visitors on an acquisition offer will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2022 - 09/29/2022
When launching a new offer for email acquisition, we hypothesized that the headline “Get your FREE copy of Digital Discipleship” did not give readers enough clarity or appeal to want to get the resource. We proposed a treatment that simply added value proposition for why someone might want the offer and how it would benefit them: “Get your FREE copy of Digital Discipleship and learn how to address the challenges of faith in the digital age” We split traffic 50/50 to each version of the page, making no other changes except the additional text in the headline for this test.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for 2 weeks was the treatment produced a 22% increase in email conversions with 99% level of confidence. The lifts were valid across every channel. We believe this is due to the enhanced perceived value of the offer by explaining what the visitor would "get" when they received the resource as we demonstrated through the headline copy what they would learn and how it would benefit them personally. Based on the results of this test, we will move to testing this same theory in other places the offer is advertised on the website and see if enhancing the call to action with some additional appeal and clarity will increase motivation to click the display ads when presented.

experiments.title: How not requiring questions on a survey affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that not requiring questions for the national scouting survey will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/15/2022
Boy Scouts of America is running the National Scouting Survey through paid acquisition on Facebook. The survey consists of 12 questions and all had previously been required. There were a variety of multiple choice questions and text questions.
We wanted to test how changing the requirement for answering all questions would affect the email acquisition rate therefore we removed the requirement from all questions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey page with questions not required resulted in a 7.2% increase in email acquisition rate with a 97.2% level of confidence. We believe the lift happened because there was less friction within the survey by not requiring the user to answer every question.
We've concluded that making the questions optional could have caused less engagement with the survey itself, but all in all, it caused the user to be more likely to give us their name and email address.

experiments.title: How a simplified eBook ad image impacted email signup rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the eBook image in our ad for Facebook prospects will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/03/2022
As a part of our experimentation with new subscriber acquisition offers, we theorized that the amount of text and call-outs on the control ad image used to promote our Facebook Ad Template offer created anxiety — which is defined as the “psychological resistance to take action” — by looking so busy.
Therefore, we decided to simplify the eBook image cover and use it in an ad to see if we could generate more clicks and increase the number of people signing up after they clicked the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the treatment produced 53.2% fewer email signups.

Our hypothesis that simplifying the eBook image cover actually worked against us in this case. Now the question becomes: Why?

There are some other data points that are worth considering, as well. They are:
We observed that the treatment ad variant had a 1.35x higher CPM — meaning that Facebook charged us more to reach 1,000 people when compared to the control audience.
 	When the above note is combined with the fact that we saw a 2.5x higher cost per click — the treatment never really had a chance, but it's worth noting also ...
 	Even once we generated a click from the treatment ad variant, we saw 12% fewer people signing up for the offer once they had arrived on the landing page.
less is not more

experiments.title: How changing Facebook ad copy for a resource offer impacted email sign ups for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding new copy and images for potential email sign-ups will achieve a change in clicks and sign-ups..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/09/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of their most popular offers is a brochure focusing on the most popular Parables of Jesus.
For this experiment, we tested both a different copy treatment with a different approach to what the brochure offers, as well as images that showed the inside of the brochure.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the copy and images so they gave a Facebook audience more insight into what they were going to be receiving, we saw a 199% increase in email sign up rates with 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the control copy was too generic to interest people and get them to fill out a form on the landing page with all their information (the resource is mailed); once they got an 'inside' view of the content, they were much more likely to sign up to receive it.
This is an experiment we ran across multiple offers like this for Pathway to Victory, with similar results. The next iteration of this test would be to focus on isolating which component of the test (copy, image, etc) really led to the experiment results.

experiments.title: How offering up a digital version of a premium increased conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering an instant digital download for resources seekers will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2022 - 05/31/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
This is an extension of an experiment we tested with a single premium in April; for this experiment, we were testing the Jesus Map premium resources.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There are a couple of key takeaways from this experiment:
The email conversion rate increased by 21% with the digital premium offer. This was most likely due to not requiring a physical mailing address (shorter form equals less friction), but could also be related to the instant gratification a potential sign-up would get with a digital download, versus having to wait for the premium to be mailed to them.
 	The cost to acquire an email address with the digital premium was 9% higher.
 	There was also a 30% decline in the number of donations post-sign up with the digital version.

experiments.title: How the placement of a lock icon impacted sign ups on a petition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the security of contact information for petition signers will achieve an increase in email conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2022 - 08/25/2022
We had previously run an experiment on petition pages that added a lock icon and security statement in the petition signature box on an email acquisition page but the results were flat and unclear if making this change had any impact on conversion. We wondered if the icon itself was too far removed from the security statement “We take data security and privacy seriously…” for page visitors to make the connection between the two elements. We created a new treatment that moved the padlock icon next to the privacy statement to enhance the safety of the information being requested for submission on the page in an effort to reduce anxiety and friction about giving away contact information. We were curious if emphasizing the commitment to security at the point of decision-making near the submit button would convince more people to convert.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for 2 weeks on multiple petition pages was that the treatment version led to a 6.9% increase in conversion rate with 98.1% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We saw increases in every audience segment across multiple devices. Because this was the only element that was altered, we are confident that the new placement on the page helped serve as a visual reminder of security that reduced visitor anxiety and made them more comfortable providing their contact information and clicking the submit button. Moving forward, we will update the icon placement for other active email acquisition offers to benefit from this lift in conversion.

The Navigators was running an offer on Discipleship 101/Spiritual Mentorship. In order to increase the email acquisition rate on the landing page, we wanted to redesign the landing page so that it closely resembled the landing page of another Navigators’ resource. We wanted to see if this new template would result in more names. This new template would push the name acquisition form to the top of the landing page in hopes to get the end user to act quicker in filling out the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 16% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 99%. These results indicate that the user actually needs to read through the value proposition copy laid out on the landing page before deciding to fill out the form. The new template did not allow for sufficient enough copy at the beginning of their experience to explain the value of the resource they were about to receive.

experiments.title: How focusing on the outcomes you can achieve with an offer impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the outcomes of getting an offer for our target audience will achieve an increase in email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/16/2022 - 08/05/2022
As a part of our own optimization efforts for an existing offer to fundraisers, we decided to look critically at one of our most popular offer landing pages, and come up with a new approach to see if it would improve email signup rates.
In reviewing the “control” version of the landing page — the message was focused on “what you will get.” We theorized that our target audience would only want to get this offer to achieve something. That led us to think through what it was that the target audience would want to achieve with this offer — and that led us to derive that they would like to raise more money online, and produce better results.
Therefore, we wrote a treatment to our landing page focused on “outcome-oriented” language, as opposed to what you get right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97.2% level of confidence, we increased email signup rates by +15.4%.
This is an exciting study because it proves that focusing on what motivates your target audience to take action and download your offer should truly be the center of the messaging as to WHY you want them to have it.
There can be other motivation types beyond "raising more money" or "increasing performance" that may motivate this audience to download at a higher rate, so further experimentation may be required, however, these results are promising and should be considered when developing new offers, or trying to optimize old ones.

experiments.title: How adding an exit intent pop up to blog pages impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent pop up with an ebook offer for blog readers will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 07/01/2022
Reasons to Believe’s main site traffic is generated by their blogs. They’re a content-heavy organization and their blogs are a prime opportunity to invite people to take the next step. While we already had dear readers on their blogs, we hypothesized that not many people scrolled to the bottom of their blog pages – especially given the length of many of their articles. In order to introduce another opportunity for name capture, we decided to test adding an exit intent pop up with an ebook offer. We also embedded the name acquisition form to this pop up in order to reduce any friction for the site visitor. We hypothesized that adding this exit intent pop up would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 44% lift in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 98%. Our hypothesis was proven correct that giving people another opportunity to receive an offer by giving their email would increase email acquisition rate. By making it an exit intent pop up, we were not interrupting their reading experience and did not wait for them to scroll to the bottom of the article. In turn, this also drastically impacted Google Grant results that generate traffic to these pages. We saw the most names acquired in any month through this experiment.

experiments.title: The impact of clearer and more efficient value proposition copy on email conversion for a content offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy length and increasing the clarity and strength of the value proposition will increase email conversion by reducing friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2022 - 08/14/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They use content offers and premium offers on Facebook to acquire new donors and have been very successful with this approach. For their content offer, Soul-Care Staycation, they wanted to increase email acquisition on the landing page. In reviewing the page, we found that the copy was long on mobile devices, the primary source of traffic to the page. It also lacked a clear headline and the value proposition could have been stronger. We hypothesized that a stronger, more effecient value proposition would increase email conversion.
For the treatment we changed the headline from simply the title of the free resource to one that conveyed more of the value of the offer, “your free guide to essential soul-care.” We also made the copy more conversational and included highlights of the eBook’s exclusive nature and content designed to help the subscriber “take a breath and focus on what’s truly important.”
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that this clearer, more efficient value propositional copy increased email acquisition for all traffic with a 92% Level of Confidence, but most importantly, increased it for NEW visitors which were the primary source of subscribers for this acquisition offer. In reviewing the data for other segments we didn't have concerns with rolling out the treatment for all traffic. We will use a similar approach when launching new content offer landing pages in the future.
