experiments.title: How an inline offer compares to an exit intent for pop-up email acquisition on a website
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: An inline offer will acquire more emails than an exit intent pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2020 - 09/25/2020
The Oblates were using an exit intent pop-up to acquire emails with an offer to get daily prayers sent via email. The effort was highly successful in acquiring emails, but presented a few drawbacks. First, exit intents can only be see on desktop computers. Second, the user experience is not ideal because people may feel pestered as they try to leave the page. So we wanted to test an inline offer for daily prayer instead of an exit intent. We needed to do it in a way that ensured that it did not diminish the success of having an exit intent pop-up.
experiments.keyLearnings: Overall, the inline offer increase email acquisition by 70%. And when we looked at just desktop traffic, which is the device that a third of their constituents use, it had no affect on email conversion rates.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

experiments.title: The impact on email acquisition of greater clarity, exclusivity, and credibility on a landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding exclusivity, clarity, and credibility to the landing page will increase email acquisition conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2022 - 05/26/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life is one of Buckner’s most important online premium offers on Facebook. It has the highest number of emails acquired, yet its landing page has one of the lowest conversion rates.
In reviewing the landing page, we believed that we could strengthen the value proposition by emphasizing the value of the devotional offer to families and reducing the frequency the copy mentioned the “Buckner” brand. We also wanted to add copy highlighting the exclusive nature of the offer and credibility from the many other families who’ve been helped by it. “Thousands of moms and dads like you have become more like Christ through Buckner International devotionals and resources. ”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for just over a month, we found a statistically valid negative impact on email conversion. While we know that greater clarity, exclusivity, and credibility improves conversion rates on landing pages, the copy we ran did not properly convey these elements to the visitor. We will revisit the page and test several new tactical elements before revisiting the value proposition and copy.

experiments.title: How clarity of the value proposition affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler copy approach and stacked design increase name conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2015 - 05/12/2015
The Heritage Foundation launched a petition against Common Core Education Standards. Despite reaching an ideal audience and achieving a low cost-per-email, the page still had room for improvement in terms of name acquisition. They hypothesized that stopping “Common Core” might be too specific of an ask, if they weren’t familiar with the policy fight itself. They introduced new copy that focused on the underlying principle of the argument — that the Federal Government shouldn’t control public education. Additionally, they placed the petition itself under the copy to allow the reader to fully digest the value proposition before completing it.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were concerned that the target audience wasn't as familiar with the specific term "Common Core" and would respond more favorably to the principles underlying the relationship between the federal government and education. This tells us that Common Core might actually be the best "opposition keyword", at least with this highly-educated group.

experiments.title: How radically redesigning a landing page with clarifying copy and removing navigation effects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the navigation and providing clarity in the page copy for users signing up for this online course will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2022 - 08/04/2022
Luther Seminary had been running their acquisition pages with navigation in the header and footer of the page. They wanted to test what would happen if we removed the navigation from the page to see if this would increase email acquisition.
We tested this concept on their Cosmic Adventure online course landing page. On top of this page having the navigation bars, the offer was not performing well for a consistent amount of time, so they also wanted to test changing the headline and body copy of the page to see if providing clarity for the user as to what they would receive in this course would help increase email acquisition.
The original page language was vague throughout the headline, sub-headline, and body copy. Within the new copy, we wanted to be very specific in the headline and subheadline by stating exactly what this course would do for them – give them answers to questions between science and faith through an informative course. Within the body copy, we wanted to focus on curiosity and clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment (treatment page 2) produced a valid increase in the email sign-up rate of 44.7% with a 96.6 level of confidence. In looking at all three variants, we believe that removing the navigation in the header and footer provided the biggest impact on our sign-up rate. On top of that, changing the page copy to be more clarifying for the user as to what this course will give them ultimately provided us with the valid increase needed.
It is recommended that this be tested on other acquisition pages separately, so we can see the true impact of removing navigation vs adding clarifying copy.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 113.8% increase with a 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How focusing on the individual’s felt need affects email acquisition rate on an offer landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing an individual’s felt need on a landing page will result in a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 07/30/2022
IMB had an ebook called “Loving the Lost” that had a pre-existing landing page and funnel. When we relaunched this offer with an entirely new funnel we wanted to use the landing page copy of the previous funnel and test it against our new landing page. The new landing page was focused on the micro-problem, the individual’s felt need. Our hypothesis was that people would be more likely to fill out the form if they understood how this ebook is helpful to them.
The control for this experiment used the pre-existing landing page’s copy. The treatment had the new individual-focused language. Traffic to this funnel was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 17% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 98%. This means more people were motivated to download the ebook when they understood how this resource would personally help them and solve their problems: how can you love people that you do not know. The control was much more focused on the macro-problem of lostness and had a short invitation for the individual to join the IMB in prayer. This indicates that IMB's audience wants to know how they personally are affected by the larger problem at hand and focusing on the felt needs of the individual can increase the motivation to take action.

experiments.title: How a more direct, urgent and exclusive positioning of an offer impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more direct, urgent, and exclusive in our invitation for subscribers and members at NextAfter  will achieve increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/03/2022
As a part of promoting a new course, the team at NextAfter was going to send a special invitation email to subscribers to invite them to participate in an online training cohort through its NextAfter Institute.
In reviewing the control email invitation — the team focused on how a “radical redesign” treatment may increase demand and ultimately drive a higher number of cohort signups.
In reviewing the control, we identified a number of variables that could serve as a testing treatment — but with limited time to get the email out on time, we decided to hit on all variables that we isolated.
Specifically, we identified 6 variables that could be tested, and the treatment addressed every one, which are:
Anxiety — defined as “psychological resistance” for the audience to take the presented action. In our control, we identified that putting too much information (the training schedule) could lead less people to sign up.
Brevity/Directness —the length of the email was long, and much of the messaging wasn’t necessary for the reader to determine whether or not they wanted to signup for the cohort. So, we theorized that shortening the message and being more direct could drive a higher signup rate.
Anticipating Objections — We thought through why someone would decline to participate in the training cohort. Our top reason is that the training schedule didn’t permit them from participating in the live training sessions (on-time), so we wanted to address that in the email message.
Urgency — Both treatments talked about the upcoming start date for the training cohort that we were asking them to sign up for, but we wanted to increase the urgency that someone had to take action right now.
Exclusivity — We wanted the reader to feel special that they were being personally invited, and a part of group that would have first access to the new course/training cohort.
Intrigue — We wanted to pique the reader’s curiosity and leave some of the control email (the schedule) behind the click. By giving away too much in the inbox, we could prematurely force our audience to make a rash decision against signing up for the course. Therefore, we wanted to tease out more clicks and allow the strength of the landing page (and the cognitive momentum of them clicking to “learn more” about the course) to carry them through the registration process for the training cohort.
As noted, because there were too many variables to isolate and too little time to reasonably test successively each concept, we decided to develop a treatment to drive all of these elements into the treatment message shown below.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we increased email signups for the new course (and training cohort) by +96.4%.
What's interesting is that we saw far more clicks (+76%, with a confidence level of 100%) on the treatment email, as well. There have been cases where if you pull back some information (through the "intrigue" concept), you can drive more clicks, but with a lower post-click conversion rate on the subsequent landing page.
This was not the case for the treatment email. In fact, it drove more clicks, and the landing page signup rate was actually validated (after the click) to increase email signups on the same landing page by +28.5% (with a confidence score of 99.6%).
This means that we didn't drive "exploratory clicks" from the treatment email, but rather that the clicks we drove from the treatment were more motivated to signup for the course than those clicking from the control email were.
Each of these variables should be isolated in subsequent testing to determine the efficacy of increasing motivation for each component used within the treatment message, but suffice it to say that you should consider each of these variables when attempting to drive a motivated click that is more likely to respond to your message in the way that you want.

experiments.title: How focusing on content of an ebook vs the context affected email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the offer as an “ebook”  for site visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2022
RTB has been running an offer called “If God created everything, who created God?” Throughout the promotion, this offer has been couched as a free chapter download of a book. The low sign up rate on the landing page indicated that there was friction on the page that deterred people from signing up. We hypothesized that framing this offer as a free chapter of a book added confusion for the user. If a person is new to the organization and they don’t know the book or the author, including this information on the landing page may be an additional barrier.
For our experiment we decided to exclude this information and simplify the presentation of the offer as just an “ebook.” We believe that the content of the ebook is more important to the end user than the context of the resource (being a chapter from a book).
Traffic to this offer was primarily driven by pad facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 23.6% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 96.7%. Our hypothesis was proven correct: site visitors were more inclined to request this free ebook when we simplified the language and focused on the content of the ebook. While the organization may deem it significant that this is a chapter from a newly released book, someone who is not connected to the ministry may not find that piece of information valuable.

experiments.title: How giving subscribers part of a content offer in the promotional email impacted clicks to the offer signup page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing subscribers with a sample of a content offer in the promo email will increase clicks to the offer signup page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/25/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas.  About a year ago, they created a “verse a day” subscription offer for their subscribers. People who enrolled received a verse or two in their inboxes each morning. They wanted to promote the offer to their file again but wanted to see if this time they could get more clicks to the landing page by providing subscribers with a sample verse like the ones they would get. We hypothesized that this would increase the appeal and clarity of the subscription offer and cause more people to visit the signup page. Simply saying, “we will give you a verse or two each morning” is far more vague than showing them the types of verses and content they will get each day.
experiments.keyLearnings: While both emails featured conversational and personal copy, the treatment email allowed subscribers to experience the content they'd be receiving if they signed up. This increased the appeal and clarity of the offer and led to more visits to the signup page. We will continue to test into ways of allowing people to "try before they buy" so we can reduce the mental friction that comes from not knowing what an offer will be like.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity in the body copy of a landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the body copy of a landing page will result in greater conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2022 - 05/28/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. For this experiment, they wanted to test if adding more clarity into the body copy of the landing page for a popular content offer would result in a greater number of email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was validated with a 98.8% Level of Confidence showing a decrease in conversion rate. The practice of adding more clarity into landing page copy has yielded positive results in previous experiments, but in this case we actually saw a decrease in conversion response. Several factors could be at play leading to this decrease...such as the overall layout of the image, form layout, and even too much body copy for this particular audience.
To continue to strive for improvement of conversions, we suggest trying a few tactical changes like moving first and last name form fields on the same line, and aligning the image of eBook with the body copy. In addition, we might try adding some social proof to the page.

experiments.title: How topic-focused versus Persona-focused calls-to-action affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that topic-driven CTAs for homepage traffic will achieve more site conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2022 - 06/06/2022
When initially launching NextAfter.com, we had implemented specific calls-to-action on the homepage targeting 3 key personas: nonprofit executives, fundraising directors, and fundraising practitioners.
We wondered if a more topic-based approach could actually lead to more conversions from the homepage. The thinking was that many people are coming to our website looking for specific solutions – and giving clear categories related to common problems would lead to a more relevant  engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The result was that a more topic-driven CTAs on the homepage were leaning towards a 9% decrease in overall conversions - but only at 72% level of confidence. Most interestingly – the topical CTAs led to a valid 25% decrease in conversion for returning visitors, but new site traffic saw a 28% increase in conversion at an 82% level of confidence.
This shows us that the traffic coming to our homepage is not of a singular motivation. There appears to be variance of motivation and intention of new VS returning traffic.
This will require more analysis of new VS visiting behavior to determine how we might customize a website experience for differing motivations.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 25.9% decrease in all downloads and registrations with a 100% level of confidence.New visitors had a 28% increase in all downloads and registrations with an 82% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a revised headline and CTA impacted signups for a weekly newsletter
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the value and clarity in an email newsletter signup form for potential newsletter subscribers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2022 - 06/06/2022
Our third test on this page had a significant variance in the sample size on the control versus the treatment so we wanted to relaunch the test to make sure the results were not muddied. We split traffic between the control page and a treatment page with a headline that had a stronger emphasis on the VALUE provided to the subscriber rather than the newsletter product itself: “Get weekly access to trusted, timely, gospel-centered resources.” We also created a treatment to the button copy to provide additional clarity in the call to action:“Send TGC Weekly to my inbox »”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment ran for one month and we observed a 24.2% decrease in conversion rate with 97.7% level of confidence so the results are valid. This time our sample sizes were accurately proportionate so these results override the increase we observed on the previous test. Our hypothesis is that getting TGC's "best content" was a more appealing description and "trusted, timely, gospel-centered resources" did not provide enough clarity, appeal, or value to convince users to subscribe to the weekly newsletter.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 33.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 40.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 28.6% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a delayed pop-up affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that triggering a pop-up in a shorter duration for site visitors will achieve a better survey completion/email capture rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 05/22/2022
We built a National Scouting survey pop-up modal to live on the BSA homepage to drive survey completions. Internal teams were concerned with site UX and asked that we set pop-up to fire after the user was on the page for 15 seconds. We believed a shorter duration of 5 seconds would produce a better result and so we created a split test for half of the users to see a pop-up at 15 seconds and the other half to receive it after 5 seconds had elapsed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The 5 second pop-up clearly outperformed waiting 15 seconds to trigger the modal. The 5 second variant acquired 53 emails versus the 23 acquired by the 15 second version. The 15 second version underperformed the 5 second version by 56% with a 99.9% confidence rate, proving that the shorted trigger duration was clearly the stronger performing variant.
Hypothesis for this performance has more to do with time on page and were users even on the page for 15 seconds to see the longer triggered variant on the page or had they already navigated elsewhere within the BSA site.
Follow up test will be to shorten the pop-up trigger from 5 seconds to 2 seconds. Test to follow this one will test trigger time versus triggering pop-up upon page scroll.

experiments.title: How conversion optimization for likely donors impacted results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for clicks or emails for a modeled donor audience will achieve increased results.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2021 - 10/31/2021
Heritage was looking for ways to expand beyond their existing donor audience to find new supporters. Wiland was able to create Facebook audiences of likely donors based upon Heritage’s existing donors. Since these were donor modeled audiences, they wanted to understand what optimization method would work best at finding new donors. We decided to test optimizing for clicks vs. emails vs. donors for a variety of email acquisition offers.
experiments.keyLearnings: After spending $1,000 on each optimization method, we found that optimizing for purchases was a statistically significantly better option for improving donor acquisition. This is in spite of the fact that the audience was already a primed donor audience. This tells us that Facebook's proprietary algorithm is able to overlay additional information to help further refine the audience selection which results in improved cost per donor and donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How an embedded form in a dear-reader ad affects course signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that embedding the registration for in the ad for blog post readers will achieve more course registrations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/20/2021
In this experiment, we launched a dear-reader ad on our blog posts to promote free year-end fundraising course. We wanted to know if it would be more effective to include a call-to-action button that drives people to the landing page to learn more and activate their course, or to include the full course registration form in the dead-reader ad itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the length of the registration form, it was more effective to embed the full course registration form in the dear-reader ad then to link readers to the landing page to learn more and register. Embedding the form led to a 94.8% increase in course registrations.
As we ramp up year-end content promotion and traffic generation campaigns, this optimized dear-reader ad should help us get the most from blog traffic generation.
It's noteworthy that this embedded form was even more impactful for desktop users as well as new visitors. It was approaching validity for mobile users (80% level of confidence). For returning visitors, it made minimal difference – the thought here being that returning users have a higher level of trust in our content and therefore their motivation is high enough to overcome the friction of an additional step in the process.

experiments.title: How justifying why you sent the email right away affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that justifying why we’re sending the email sooner in the copy for our email housefile will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/14/2020
We had previously run an a/b test where we saw that telling a longer personal story was more effective at generating clicks than a brief personal story. But I wondered if actually getting to the point of why we’re emailing right off the bat might be even more effective – while still utilizing a personal tone and format.
Version A led with a personal note about the holiday season and how our family was getting in the Christmas spirit. Version B jumped right to the primary reason I was emailing – to give them a free ebook to increase their year-end revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Getting straight to the point and justifying why we are emailing right away led to a 36% increase in people downloading the ebook. In fact, it also led to a valid increase in every key metric: opens, clicks, and conversions (see below).

While we have clearly seen the power of being human and personal in email marketing, this experiment shows us that our subscribers are looking at their inbox and engaging with emails that they consider to be important, helpful, and valuable. As a result, our email envelope and copy need to clearly convey the value of the content we're sharing.
7.1% increase in email opens (99% LOC)
 	32.4% increase in clicks (99% LOC)

experiments.title: How presenting a micro ask before the macro, impacts email conversion from a pop-up on the website
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a micro ask before the macro ask increase email acquisition from the pop-up on the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2021 - 05/27/2021
For the past five months, Save the Children has been testing into the email acquisition pop-up on their website. The majority of these experiments have been focused around messaging and value proposition. But all of these experiments have resulted in no positive or negative impact on conversion. As we continue to test into this feature on the website and look for ways to optimize the experience, we hypothesized that we might be able to engage more website users and ultimately increase name conversion by presenting an easier engagement experience on the pop-up before we ask them to give us their email address.
To test this, we presented a “yes”/”no” question that a user would answer before the email acquisition form field would show. The “yes”/”no” question was the same sentiment as the control – “Would you like to stay informed?”. The “Yes” button was highlighted red. When a person would click on the “Yes” button, an email form would slide out and the copy read, “Great! Please provide your email address to sign up.” If a person clicked “No”, the pop-up would simply disappear.
The control presented the same kind of email acquisition opportunity, but in the form of a statement – “Sign up to stay informed”.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for 10 days, we found that the "Yes"/"No" micro ask increased email acquisition by 35%! This valid lift happened on both desktop and mobile devices. In fact, mobile devices had the most significant valid increase in name conversion of 42% (99% LoC). This experiment shows us the importance of engaging people through an easy entrance point results in more people likely to convert. This micro ask increases a persons likelihood and motivation to complete the macro ask.

experiments.title: How an email acquisition opportunity is presented on the website
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a slide in email acquisition offer impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2021 - 02/23/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The copy and and trigger timing of the pop-up has been tested in the past with little to no impact on conversion, so we hypothesized that the placement of the feature may impact conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that functioned more as a slide in feature on the bottom of the page than a forced pop-up. No copy or design of the feature was changed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a significant decrease in donor conversion from the slide in feature compared to the forced pop-up. With an almost 41% decrease in emails acquired, we can learn that the placement of the offer does have an impact on the number of people signing up to stay connected with Save the Children. The pop-up is almost much more disruptive - drawing attention to the feature and capturing more names than the slide in.

Continued testing is needed to really understand the motivation of this audience and what elements of friction exist in the conversion process.

experiments.title: How simplifying a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the petition for simplified ad clickers will achieve an increase in email signup rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
As a part of an open letter (petition) promotional campaign that Americans for Prosperity was running, they wanted to see if they could experiment with increasing email signup rates on the massive increases in click-through traffic received from their simplified ad approach.
As expected, far more people were clicking, but their email signup rate was dropping because the pre-click and post-click experiences weren’t directly congruent if the end user did not read the corresponding ad text to know that if they clicked, they would be asked to sign a petition.
Overall volume of emails was much higher with the simplified ad experience, but the organization wanted to see if they could capture a higher percentage of this overall increase in visitors to the open letter (petition).
Therefore, we attempted to simplify the open letter by removing some additional detail from the page, thereby shortening the letter altogether to see if we could remove friction in the email signup process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in email signup rate of -11.9%.
Our theory is that the additional context and detail that was removed from the petition actually provided less on-page context for the higher number of visitors received from the simplified ad experience — which served as a foundation to motivate people who clicked without understanding what they were being asked to do to finish the petition signing process.
Further experimentation with creating alignment between the pre-click and post-click experience of the "simplified ad experience" is advised to focus on increasing email signup rates.

experiments.title: How reducing form field friction and anxiety affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does reducing form field friction and anxiety affects conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2016 - 12/23/2016
The shortened registration form produced a 8.9% increase in conversions on the page, which showed that there was a segment of the audience that either felt anxious about giving away that information or felt like it was too much work to fill out a longer form. The form is now reduced to the bare essentials of what data must be collected for the course registration, so more value proposition testing is prompted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new free course studying the book of Revelation. Traditionally, they had always asked for name, email address, and full home address in the registration process so they could send prospects direct mail if needed. However, the data had shown that these prospects did not respond well to direct mail unless they made an online gift during the course. Since DTS got all the home address data when they made a gift, they didn’t need to collect it at the beginning.
They were curious how this might affect course registrations, so they launched a test between a full signup form and one that just asked for name, email, and password. They split the traffic to determine the effect of removing the home address fields.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 8.9% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: The impact of a Yes/No Image Ad on Facebook for volunteer recruitment
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enabling immediate feedback via the image design for viewers on Facebook will achieve greater volunteer conversion rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2021
We wanted to test whether using an image that enabled viewers on Facebook to feel like they were providing immediate feedback would increase conversion. So, we designed a static image with the question of our Facebook Survey on it and a big Yes and a big No option for them to “click” on. While not serving as working buttons, we hypothesized that merely simulating a button would be enough to increase conversion rate.
We tested this against a short video ad created using Facebook’s image/video tool.
experiments.keyLearnings: The feedback ad improved conversion on every metric. Not only did we increase volunteer signups by 191%, we decreased the cost per signup 76% (from $37.95 to 8.93) and the cost per click 81% ($0.37 to $0.07). WE are testing this now on our monthly donor campaign to see if it has similar results.

experiments.title: Does a shortened landing page with added credibility language improve sign-up rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that swapping in credibility language for new names on the “Taboo Questions” landing page will increase sign-up rates by prioritizing copy that addresses the hypothesis (users aren’t sure who Time of Grace or Pastor Mike Novotny is) without simply adding to the page’s overall length.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2026 - 03/18/2026
Time of Grace landing page, “Taboo Questions With Pastor Mike” has struggled with a low sign-up rate of 10% – 14%. With ad targeting suppressing known audience and only being directed to new names, we hypothesized that credibility may be a point of friction. “Who is Time of Grace?” “Why should I care what Pastor Mike thinks about these topics”? An initial test that simply added this credibility language resulted in an even greater decrease. However, we hypothesized that it was not the credibility language itself. Rather, this latest iteration added in credibility language while also shortening the length of the page. This way, the treatment wasn’t simply giving the user a longer page to read, but was swapping existing copy for copy that addressed our hypothesis—keeping the overall length similar, or if anything a little bit shorter.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
Adding credibility language about Time of Grace and Pastor Mike Novotny led to a significant increase in email signups for all traffic on the "Taboo Questions" landing page. In contrast, this same language "failed" in a previous experiment, providing critical insight into test execution and Time of Grace's audience. This iteration shows the language addresses a friction point that users encountered on the landing page. Its success depended on being swapped in, not just added to and lengthened. Simply increasing its length previously had a negative impact. This demonstrates that an asset cannot "have it all" or use every tool at once. Instead, it must prioritize what donors need most.

experiments.title: How shortening a survey affects email conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the number of survey questions to the top 10 most compelling issues will cause an increase in survey completion rate because a shorter, more focused survey reduces respondent fatigue and keeps engagement high by highlighting only the most resonant and emotionally charged topics.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/30/2025
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a survey centered on President Trump’s agenda. The existing version of the survey featured 15 questions. While this provided comprehensive insight, it also posed a potential barrier to conversion due to its length. To optimize for better engagement and increased email conversion rates, we hypothesized that a shorter, more focused survey could outperform the original by reducing participant fatigue and enhancing relevance. This led to the formulation of a controlled A/B test where the only variation was the length and content of the survey.
This experiment was executed through a 50/50 split test across all incoming paid media traffic. For one full month, half of the audience received the original 15-question survey (control), while the other half was shown a trimmed version containing just the top 10 most compelling questions (treatment).
The idea for this test stemmed from a strategic insight: longer surveys often experience higher drop-off rates. We believed that by prioritizing the most emotionally resonant and broadly relevant issues, we could not only streamline the experience but also deepen engagement. The decision to cut five questions was guided by three criteria:
Redundancy – Questions that overlapped thematically with stronger, more comprehensive ones.
Narrowness – Topics that were too specific or niche to drive widespread action.
Relevance – Issues that, while important, were not seen as high-priority by the core Trump-supporting audience.
Questions removed included topics such as electric vehicle mandates, women’s sports, campus radicalism, entitlements, and military modernization—all of which, though valid, were deemed less galvanizing compared to issues like immigration, inflation, and election integrity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reducing the number of survey questions to the top 10 most compelling issues did not lead to an increase in survey completion rates. In fact, the treatment group actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 82.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of survey length on respondent engagement. While the hypothesis that a shorter, more focused survey would lead to higher completion rates seemed logical, the results of this experiment suggest otherwise. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to understand why the treatment group performed lower than the control group and to explore other potential factors that may influence survey completion rates.

When designing future experiments, it is essential to ensure that the hypotheses are based on solid reasoning and supported by data. It is also important to consider the potential impact of changes on the desired outcome and to set clear criteria for assessing the validity of the results. Additionally, incorporating feedback from participants and analyzing user behavior can provide valuable insights for optimizing future surveys and experiments.

experiments.title: Can Changing the Way We Frame a Newsletter Impact Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe framing the newsletter as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world will increase conversions by tapping into uncertainty avoidance and information overload.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2025 - 07/19/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, an NPR member station serving listeners throughout North Carolina. The test focused on the landing page for WUNC’s “What’s Trending” newsletter. We wanted to determine whether reframing the newsletter copy—as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world—would lead to an increase in email sign-ups. All other elements of the landing page remained unchanged.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment
Framing Boosted Sign-Ups
Reframing the newsletter copy to position it as a trusted filter in a fast-changing world led to a 41.1% increase in email sign-ups.
Statistical Confidence
The result had a 90.1% confidence level, suggesting a strong signal, though additional testing is recommended to confirm validity.
Gift Size Observation
Revenue performance was observed as a 1-to-1 gift comparison, with the treatment receiving a substantially larger gift. However, this is anecdotal and not sufficient to conclude a higher average gift resulted from the treatment.
Hypothesis Validation
The experiment supported the hypothesis that appealing to uncertainty avoidance and information overload can effectively drive newsletter sign-ups.
Recommendations for Future Testing
Test Messaging Themes That Address Emotional or Cognitive Needs
Building on the success of the “trusted filter” framing, explore other emotionally resonant themes—such as reliability, belonging, or empowerment—to identify which underlying motivations most effectively drive sign-ups.
Experiment with Messaging Hierarchy and Presentation
Evaluate how the placement, tone, and format of key messaging (e.g., headlines vs. supporting copy, minimalist vs. detailed explanations) influence conversion rates on acquisition pages.
Extend Testing Across Related Channels and Entry Points
Apply and test winning messaging strategies on other digital touchpoints—such as social media ads, on-site pop-ups, or podcast mentions—to assess consistency and scalability in email acquisition performance.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a unengaged nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a quote from VDH to the body of the email for Hoover’s unengaged nondonor audience will achieve an increase in email conversions and donations because the quote may act as a stimulating prompt, especially for readers predisposed to Hoover’s values or VDH’s worldview.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their unengaged non donor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s (VDH) latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age with a goal of reactivating them through compelling thought leadership content. The goal was to determine whether adding a VDH quote to the body of the email would increase email acquisitions and donations by leveraging name recognition and the intellectual appeal of Hanson’s thought leadership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Impact on donations:

Key learnings:
The Quote May Spark Reengagement - Among previously unengaged users, the VDH quote appears to have increased interest and action, leading to a 16.8% increase in email acquisitions and a notable lift in donations. This suggests that high-authority intellectual content can serve as a re-engagement tool when placed in the right context.
 	Curiosity and Gravitas Help Reposition Hoover’s Value - The quote likely acted as a thought-provoking hook that reframed the email’s tone—from promotional to contemplative. This could appeal to intellectually dormant segments who still identify with Hoover’s worldview but need a deeper reason to act.
 	Small Sample = Cautious Interpretation - While results are promising, especially in contrast to the flat or negative performance in donor and non-donor audiences, the sample size for donations is too low to draw firm conclusions. This underscores the need to retest at scale or over a longer window.
• Test VDH Quotes as Reengagement Tools
• Try Quote as a Subject Line or Lead -
• Segmentation Refinement

experiments.title: How creating clarity with bullet points on the landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reinforcing the article featured in the Facebook ad on the corresponding landing page for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates because clear and consistent messaging will give users greater confidence in what they’re signing up for, creating a smoother cognitive experience and reducing uncertainty.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2025 - 07/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal was to increase email acquisition for their Spring Digest 2025, a curated digital collection of thought-provoking articles and commentary.
The experiment focused on the landing page experience, specifically how well it reinforced the messaging from the Facebook ads driving traffic to the offer.
The control version of the landing page featured a set of general article highlights from the digest.
In contrast, the treatment version aligned the landing page content with the specific article referenced in the Facebook ad, including a featured political cartoon and title.
The premise for this test was rooted in message match—the idea that maintaining visual and verbal continuity from ad to landing page could increase trust, reduce friction, and improve conversions. This approach leverages psychological principles such as the Consistency Bias (people prefer information that aligns with what they’ve already seen) and Fluency Heuristics (easier-to-process content tends to be more persuasive).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Message Match Isn’t Always the Winner - While the treatment offered tighter alignment between the ad and landing page content, the control outperformed it in both email acquisition rate and donation volume. This suggests that too narrow a focus on a single article may inadvertently limit perceived value, especially if the highlighted article doesn’t resonate universally. Broad Appeal May Drive Higher Engagement - The control’s use of diverse article highlights may have created a stronger sense of breadth and value within the Spring Digest, increasing the appeal to a wider audience. Users may have felt they were signing up for a more comprehensive and enriching offer.High Average Gift in Treatment Signals Donor Intent - Though the treatment underperformed in email acquisition, the average gift amount was nearly 3x higher. This could suggest that users who did convert in the treatment were more primed or aligned with the specific content, possibly indicating higher intent or alignment with Hoover’s donor profile. However, since there was only 1 gift to treatment and 3 gifts to control this is not statistically significant. 4. Clarity vs. Curiosity - This experiment raises an interesting tension: should a landing page lead with clarity (specificity) or curiosity (variety)? The data implies that curiosity-based framing (control) can boost signups, while clarity-based framing (treatment) may filter for higher-value leads.
Future Testing
•	Segmentation Testing: Consider targeting high-intent users with treatment-style pages and broader audiences with control-style pages.
•	Message Framing Tests: Explore emotional vs. intellectual framing of article titles—does the tone of the content preview affect conversion?

experiments.title: How should we lay out a landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that applying a radical redesign to the landing page for ebook offers will achieve more email conversations because the radical redesign will remove friction and increase clarity of the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2025
For all moody ministries, we’ve been wondering if we’re presenting the landing page in the best way to optimize for email captures. After brainstorming, we decided that we wanted to try a radical redesign test to get rid of the footer, move the logo to the middle of the page, shorten the copy, and add an image of the ebook. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the radical redesign of the landing page for ebook offers resulted in a 12.8% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. This indicates that the changes made in the treatment version may have had a positive impact on attracting more email conversions.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate these findings. By continuing to test different variations of the landing page, we can gather more data to determine if the radical redesign consistently leads to higher email conversions. Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand why the treatment version performed better and make informed decisions for future optimizations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that simplifying the landing page and focusing on key elements such as the ebook image can potentially enhance the user experience and encourage more email sign-ups. This information can guide future strategies for improving email capture rates on landing pages for Moody Radio ministries.

experiments.title: How shortening the length of landing page copy impacted email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a shorter landing page, with less content and a more prominent form placement, will lead to an increase in email sign-ups by reducing friction and making it easier for visitors to find and complete the form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary created an ebook on the book of Romans to engage their audience and drive sign-ups. The team wanted to test whether a shorter content offer landing page would result in more sign-ups by reducing friction for visitors, particularly by eliminating the need for them to scroll down to find the sign-up form.
The Control landing page featured a longer length, with detailed content above the fold and additional sections below the form. The Treatment landing page was shortened, focusing more succinctly on the value proposition and the sign-up form without as much content above and below.
Our hypothesis was that reducing the length of the page would reduce friction and increase sign-up conversions.
We ran this as an A/B test, with 50% of traffic seeing the longer Control page, and the other 50% seeing the shorter Treatment page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test demonstrated that a shorter landing page led to a 37.1% increase in email sign-ups, though the statistical confidence of 92.2% means the result is promising but not fully validated. Several factors may explain this outcome:
Reduced Cognitive Load & Friction: By simplifying the landing page and minimizing content above and below the form, we reduced the need for visitors to process excessive information, leading them to more easily take the desired action.
 	Clearer Path to Action: With less content to distract from the call to action, the sign-up form became the clear focus of the page, potentially prompting more visitors to complete the form.
 	User Behavior: Shorter pages can align with the behavior of web visitors who may be less patient or more likely to convert when they feel they are not overwhelmed with excessive information.
further testing with other variations of page length and content may lead to even higher conversion rates

experiments.title: Does adding recency and social proof on a landing page impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the amount of downloads from this month for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it suggests recent ongoing popularity and drives urgency so people will feel like they don’t want to miss out. It also might feel more believable as a smaller number rather than a marketing exaggeration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/05/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on the landing page for the offer, Dueling Populisms, focusing specifically on its effect on email acquisition. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer overall VS the number of downloads this month to determine which messaging could encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Social proof language that highlights recent activity can have a positive impact on email acquisition. The treatment version, which featured the number of downloads for the current month, resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisition compared to the control version.

2. The treatment version created a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making the offer more compelling to potential donors.

3. While the results show promise, further testing is recommended to validate the effectiveness of using recent download numbers as social proof.

How to use what was learned in future experimentation:

1. Continue to test different types of social proof language to see what resonates best with potential donors. This could include not just recent activity, but also other forms of social proof such as testimonials or reviews.

2. Consider incorporating elements of urgency and exclusivity in future messaging to increase engagement and drive action.

3. Use this experiment as a foundation for further optimization of landing pages and donation offers, keeping in mind the potential impact of social proof on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How including relevant testimonials on the offer landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including participant testimonials for Jan 1 Scripture Memory Sign-up landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because sharing a positive experience from others will build more trust and confidence in the value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2024 - 12/30/2024
Each month The Navigators run a Scripture Memory Challenge via text. Before each campaign, we promote the next months challenge via Facebook ads, housefile emails invites, and text invites. This challenges have been very popular among their audience and their staff has had a lot of positive feedback about them. Therefore, we tested adding participant testimonials to the landing page for our December/January 2025 Challenge.
These testimonials were presented like a carousel that rotated through 7 different quotes every couple of seconds.
Our hypothesis was that adding social proof to the landing page in the form of testimonials would lead to more people signing up for this challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including participant testimonials on the landing page for The Navigators' Jan 1 Scripture Memory Challenge did not result in the expected increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment group saw a 4.7% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. While the results were statistically significant at a 92.3% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

This indicates that the addition of participant testimonials may not have had the intended effect of building more trust and confidence in the offer. It is important to note that further testing would be necessary to confirm these results and understand the true impact of participant testimonials on email acquisition.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of participant testimonials in different formats or with different messaging. Additionally, considering other factors that may influence email acquisition, such as the landing page design, call-to-action, or audience targeting, would also be valuable. By continuously testing and analyzing different variables, The Navigators can gain deeper insights into what strategies are most effective in driving email acquisition for their Scripture Memory Challenges.

experiments.title: How does adding the number of downloads as an incentive for a content offer impacts emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the number of downloads of the content offer for paid media audiences will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the number would reinforce credibility and incentives since it is so highly requested.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV offers numerous digital download content offers for free as a method to acquire new email addresses that can be cultivated into potential donors.
We decided to test PTV’s best-performing content offer, The Jesus Map, to share the number of downloads for the offer. We hypothesize that this number would help to add credibility and incentive as over 19,000 people had downloaded it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding the number of downloads of the content offer for paid media audiences led to a 17.0% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. This indicates that including social proof, such as the number of downloads, can potentially enhance credibility and incentivize users to engage.

2. Despite the positive impact on email acquisition, the statistical level of confidence was only 83.0%, falling below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered truly reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

3. On the other hand, the experiment also revealed a significant 763% decrease in transactions with a high level of confidence at 96%. This unexpected outcome suggests that while the social proof may have driven more email acquisitions, it could have potentially deterred conversions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider the following implications for future experimentation:

1. While social proof can be a powerful tool in driving user engagement, the impact on other metrics such as transactions should be carefully monitored. It is crucial to balance the benefits of increased credibility with potential drawbacks on conversion rates.

2. Future experiments should aim for a higher statistical level of confidence to ensure the reliability of the results. This may involve running the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or refining the data collection process.

3. It is essential to analyze the overall impact of any changes on multiple metrics to gain a comprehensive understanding of the experiment's effects. This will help in making informed decisions and avoiding unintended consequences.

In conclusion, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of carefully evaluating the trade-offs between different metrics when implementing changes. By considering these learnings and conducting future experiments with a more rigorous approach, we can better understand the impact of our interventions and make data-driven decisions to optimize our strategies.

experiments.title: Will using shorter copy on an content offer landing page increase email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers will achieve greater rates of email acquisition because there is less friction on the page to distract from completing a form..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/30/2024
Luther Seminary has a number of ebook content offers that typically perform well for their audience. We wanted to see if we could increase the email acquisition rate on these landing pages by using more focused, concise copy compared to the standard longer control copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:
The use of shorter, more focused copy on an acquisition landing page for website visitors interested in content offers resulted in a 37.1% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. The statistical level of confidence was 92.2%, which indicates a high likelihood that the increase in email acquisition was due to the change in copy length. While the results are encouraging, further testing would be necessary to ensure the validity and reliability of the findings.
 	Further analysis of the data shows a concerning drop in the average gift and overall revenue for this offer. While these metrics did not reach a statistical level of confidence, further testing should be mindful of the potential trade-off of acquiring more subscribers who are potentially less valuable to the organization.

experiments.title: How reordering a headline’s CTAs impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rewording the headline to make the primary focus the campaign for October Scripture Memory landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign ups  because making the campaign the focus relieves the privacy concerns and concerns of spam that are often associated with texts from an organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/11/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a Scripture Memory Text Campaign to help their audience memorize more scriptures.
However, there has still been some confusion that this is a text campaign – a series of 7-8 texts, 1 per day. Therefore, their team decided to change the headline to read: “Sign up for Text Messages and join us for a Scripture Memory Challenge!” in an effort to emphasize participants would receive daily tests.
We hypothesized that this change would result in a lower number of sign ups due to the perceived level of commitment, privacy concerns, and spam all associated with texts.
So, we decided to test it against a new headline that focused on the Scripture Memory campaign first, rather than just signing up for texts.
Our goal was to get people bought into the campaign first then agree to receiving text messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headline to focus on the campaign itself rather than the text messages resulted in a slight decrease in sign-ups, indicating that visitors were not impacted, neither positively or negatively, by the headline asking them to sign up for text messages first. While these results did not validate, it is a helpful learning that The Navigators audience did not have a heightened or lessened motivation to sign up by the commitment to texts.
In the future, it may be beneficial to strike a balance between promoting the campaign and addressing potential privacy concerns. A revised headline that emphasizes the benefits of the Scripture Memory Challenge while also addressing privacy and spam concerns could potentially yield better results. Additionally, it's important to ensure that any changes made are tested with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing them on a larger scale.

experiments.title: How changing ad creative impacted registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative to look like a book cover for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in registrations/sign ups for the content offer because the ad creative feels more like a book cover rather than a person’s face.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
The Hoover Institution is running one of its long-time content offers, Dueling Populisms. This offer has run for a while, so refreshing creative is a great way for this to seem new to the audience. After seeing a low CTR, we decided we wanted to test the beginning of the funnel with new creative to see if we could increase registrations. For this specific test, we tried testing an image that looks like a book cover and more historical rather than Victor Davis Hanson; however, users have often shown an increase in registrations when we use his face on an ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved changing the ad creative to look like a book cover, resulted in a 25.1% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. This indicates that using an image resembling a book cover instead of featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face did not resonate as well with the audience in terms of driving registrations.

2. Clicks experienced a significant 34.5% decrease, Cost per Click increased by 66.6%, and Cost per Registration increased by 45.7% in the treatment group. These metrics suggest that the new creative may not have been as engaging or appealing to users, leading to a decrease in engagement and an increase in the cost of acquiring registrations.

3. Specific audience segments, such as visitors aged 65+ and Tablet visitors, also showed significant decreases in complete registrations. This indicates that the new creative may have particularly missed the mark with these demographics.

4. While the results show a clear preference for the control ad featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face, the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%. Further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. Audience preference: The audience may have a stronger affinity for ads featuring a recognizable personality like Victor Davis Hanson. It would be beneficial to continue testing different creatives to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

2. Iterative testing: Testing different ad creatives, messaging, and visuals is crucial for optimizing campaign performance. It is essential to analyze the results of each test carefully and iterate based on the learnings to continuously improve outcomes.

3. Audience segmentation: Understanding how different audience segments respond to various creatives can provide valuable insights for targeted campaigns. Segmenting the audience based on demographics, interests, or behaviors can help tailor the creative to specific groups for better engagement.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of testing and iterating on ad creatives to find the most effective approach for driving registrations. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to experiment, the Hoover Institution can optimize its marketing efforts and achieve better results in the future.

experiments.title: Does using a PDF icon on a content offer’s Facebook ad impact signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a PDF icon on Facebook creative for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is clarity in the type of offer they are receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
PTV offers multiple digital content offers in an effort to acquire new names, new donors, and to keep known contacts and donors engaged with content.
One of PTV’s most successful offers is about Billy Graham. In an effort to increase the success of this offer, we decided to test adding a PDF icon on the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment, which involved adding a PDF icon to the Facebook creative, resulted in a 10.0% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 83.8% statistical level of confidence. However, since the confidence level is below the 85% threshold, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making

experiments.title: How humanizing Facebook Creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on optimizing click-through rates and conversions for an eBook promotion are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an ad with targeted imagery of a person engaged in outdoor activities, resulted in a 35.0% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control ad with the eBook's cover image.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 92.6%, indicating that the effectiveness of the treatment is approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm its impact.

3. The hypothesis that targeted imagery helps audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook, fostering a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates, was supported by the results of the experiment.

Moving forward, future experimentation should consider the following points:

1. Continue to test and refine the use of targeted imagery in advertisements to enhance engagement and conversions. Further experiments can help strengthen the validity of the findings and optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

2. Explore different types of imagery and messaging to determine which combinations resonate best with the target audience. This can inform future advertising strategies and improve campaign performance.

3. Implement A/B testing on a larger scale or over an extended period to gather more data and increase the confidence level of results. This will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of different ad elements on audience behavior.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that targeted imagery can have a significant effect on engagement and conversions. By leveraging this insight and continuing to test and refine strategies, WUNC can enhance the effectiveness of its promotional campaigns and drive better outcomes for future eBook promotions and other marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How humanizing a dear reader impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a human invitation for website visitors will achieve an increase in newsletter signups because we know that people tend to respond to things that are “alive” in some way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2024 - 05/07/2024
When launching a newsletter and advertising it to site visitors below blogs/articles on the webpage, we wondered if we could replicate the success we had seen through other orgnization’s testing of effectively increasing email acquisition by humanizing our communications. We developed a treatment that leaned into the humanity of the newsletter offer and that emphasized that you’re going to hear directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happening inside the Canadian government if you sign up for weekly newsletter updates. We ran this experiment for 4 weeks to determine just how adding a clear value proposition and highlighting a familiar face from your organization can improve response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a human invitation for website visitors can significantly increase email acquisition for the Canadian Taxpayers Federation's newsletter. The treatment, which emphasized hearing directly from Franco—an insider on the latest happenings inside the Canadian government, led to a 57.6% increase in email signups compared to the control group.

While the results of this experiment are promising, the statistical level of confidence of 90.9% falls slightly below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. This suggests that further testing may be necessary to confirm that the treatment consistently has the intended effect.

In future experimentation, the Canadian Taxpayers Federation should continue to explore the impact of humanizing communications and highlighting familiar faces within the organization to engage website visitors. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test different variations of the human invitation, such as featuring other insiders or providing specific examples of the type of content subscribers can expect to receive. By iteratively testing and optimizing these approaches, the organization can continue to improve email acquisition and overall engagement with its audience.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to petition summaries led to increase in emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding descriptions to the petition summary cards for non-paid website traffic will lead to an increase in petition signatures because it adds more clarity and strengthens the motivation of the user clicking through.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/17/2024
In this experiment, Canadian Taxpayers Federation was exploring how they might optimize the top of their petition funnel. Historically they have spent a lot of time and effort on optimizing paid ad campaigns driving traffic to specific petition offers. But they wondered if they could drive more motivated visitors to petition pages from their existing website traffic.
Their original petitions landing page had lots of summary cards showing the title of the petition, a representative image, and a “Sign the Petition” call-to-action button.
They wondered if adding brief descriptions to the petition summary cards would give more context to the user and ultimately lead to more petition signers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding the brief descriptions to the petition summaries led to a 17% increase in petition signatures.

 

While historically this page has not been a significant driver of petition signatures, this experiment shows that the potential is there to drive more motivated users into a key acquisition funnel. It just requires some optimization to understand what will drive motivated click-throughs from existing website traffic.

 

Future experimentation should focus on template-level ideas that can increase the clarity of these petition offers as well as drive more motivated traffic through to the next step.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher click-through rate because users will be able to see what they’re requesting before clicking out of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the calendar to the email did not have a positive impact on the click-through rate to the landing page. In fact, the treatment group showed a 3.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group with an 80.3% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence to determine the true impact of adding an image to the email. Additionally, other factors such as the content of the email, the subject line, and the timing of the email should be taken into consideration in future experiments. It is also important to continue testing different variables to optimize the email and landing page conversion rates for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout Impacts Sign-Up Rate on Offer Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the How to Memorize Scripture landing page will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because it removes anxiety for those who might wonder if their information is secure or why The Navigators needs their email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2023 - 06/10/2023
The Navigators’ landing page for How to Memorize Scripture was experiencing a slowing in sign-up rate. We hypothesized that adding a security callout at the bottom of the form will increase sign-up rate. This test was conducted on another offer, and was expanded to How to Memorize Scripture to see the result of more testing.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment led to a 12% decrease in sign-up rate with a 94% level of confidence. Although, this is technically just under the accepted threshold for validity (95%) this in conjunction with similar results from the same test on a different offer does  indicate that perhaps this learning should be observed for Navigators' audience.
Why would a callout for security cause less people to sign-up? Perhaps in an attempt to limit anxiety around giving up an email, the callout actually introduced it. If their security hadn't been a consideration before, and the test then bring attention to it — it may have led users to overthink about "Do I really want to give my email? Is this safe?"
The takeaway here is that perhaps security wasn't a big concern for Navigators' audience. An effort to improve the sign-up rate on the page, should move on to testing other factors that might be inhibiting conversion.

experiments.title: How the structure and design of the email acquisition page impacts email signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more designed landing page that presents the reader with a different experience showing components of the course will lead to a higher email sign-up rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2023 - 07/02/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a study guide about the Book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We want to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page therefore we decided to test the design of the landing page.
Historically, we’ve tested into a more simple donation page, so with this treatment, we wanted to see if a more designed approach that presents the characteristics of the study guide in a new way would lead to more people signing up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment, which involved a more designed landing page, resulted in a 9.2% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic compared to the control page with a 90.4% level of confidence.
