2. Desktop visitors had a significant decrease of 216% in email acquisitions compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 99%.

Based on these findings, it can be concluded that the more designed landing page did not lead to a higher email sign-up rate. In fact, it had a negative impact, particularly among desktop visitors.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate (Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity for site visitors  will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate  because it reduces anxiety and increase the perceived value of what they are receiving by subscribing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2023 - 07/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate. We ran this test once and saw a lift in acquisition rate among returning visitors but a decrease among new visitors. The initial test had header language that encouraged people to commit to disciplemaking. Our hypothesis was that it was too large of a commitment for new site visitors to make. So we kept the spirit of the test (adding clarity) and changed the headline to be more accessible for people new to the Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a directional 30% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 89%. While not a statistically valid result, it affirms our hypothesis that the initial header may have been too much of a commitment for newer site visitors. New visitors experienced a 33.2% increase in name acquisition with a level of confidence of 87.7%. Using the new header and continuing to add clarity in the CTA copy resulted in an increase in names acquired. In fact, new visitors experienced the larger impact in name acquisition.

experiments.title: How a mysterious subject line + conversational email copy impacted email clicks

rows.name: Version A - Subject Line: The taxes you pay

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a mysterious subject line coupled with conversational email copy for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because more people will open the email to find out the meaning of the mysterious subject line and the conversational appeal of the email will lead more people to click to find out more about the focus article of the email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/31/2023 - 06/09/2023
IPI is sending a monthly cultivation email to their housefile email list. In order to learn about our audience and what motivates them to open emails and click within emails, we’ve set up a subject line + body copy experiment.
1/4 of our list will receive
control subject line and control body copy
treatment subject line with control body copy
control subject line with treatment body copy
treatment subject line with treatment body copy
The treatment subject line plays into curiosity and intrigue, while the treatment body copy takes an especially conversational approach, sharing the perspective of the sender while also asking for the thoughts and feedback of the reader.
We want to see which combination generates the most clicks within the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment #2 (treatment subject line + control email copy) produced a nearly valid (89.9% level of confidence) 11.9% increase in email clicks. Treatments #1 (control SL and treatment email copy) and #3 (treatment SL and control email copy) produced negative results with invalid level of confidences.

Thus, treatment #2 was the big winner here. Our audience has responded well to mystery subject lines in the past, and we now have more evidence that this approach to subject line framing is effective. These type of subject lines creative intrigue and curiosity, prompting recipients to open the email to find out what's inside. The control subject line did not have the same curiosity, as it exposed the topic up front.

When we look specifically at open rates, treatment #2 produced a 49.3% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence; treatment #3 produced a 24.5% increase in open rate with a 100% level of confidence.

Ultimately, we should lead many more cultivation emails using mysterious subject lines.

As for the body copy element, perhaps the conversational approach consisted of too much fluff, as the control – less conversational – variant was a bit more to the point and brief, and resulted in higher click rates. We may want to be more considerate of our recipients' time and focus more on clarity and being direct when sending future cultivation emails.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a security callout at bottom of email acquisition form for users on the How to Save a World landing page will achieve an increase in acquisition rate because it relieves the stress and anxiety around how their email will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/03/2023
For Navigators’ How to Save the World landing page, we hypothesized that a simple security callout beneath the form would increase email acquisition rate. This short line of copy reinstates to the user that their information is secure and that they are only providing their email in order to receive the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a security callout at the bottom of an email acquisition form on a landing page may not have a positive impact on the email acquisition rate. The treatment group resulted in a 12.9% decrease in email acquired for all traffic with an 88.3% level of confidence, indicating that the control group was more effective. In addition, desktop visitors had a significant 730% decrease in email acquisition, suggesting that the callout may have had an adverse effect on this device.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct further testing to confirm whether the security callout is effective or not. If it does not work, other possible strategies could include using social proof or highlighting the benefits of subscribing to the newsletter. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the impact of the callout on different devices and user segments to ensure that it is not hurting the acquisition rate. Overall, ongoing experimentation and analysis are vital for improving the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition leading into a survey impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that enhancing the value proposition communicated on the School Choice landing page will increase email acquisition rates because it will provide visitors with more clarity and appeal on the impact of their survey.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2023 - 06/18/2023
IPI’s School Choice Survey is producing a low 14.4% sign up rate. In an effort to acquire more emails and increase this metric, we’ve built a treatment that adds value proposition copy leading into the survey.
The current survey page includes just two short sentences leading into the survey. Our analysis dictates that these sentences feel transactional, lack personability, and due not do a good job of expressing the value to someone for taking and completing the survey.
That’s why we’ve built a treatment that we believe does those things. We added a call-to-action headline, focused the copy on the reader, and communicated the value and purpose of the survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition communicated on the School Choice landing page has a significant impact on email acquisition rates. The treatment with a call-to-action headline, personalized copy, and clear communication of the value and purpose of the survey led to a 24.7% increase in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 82.5% statistical level of confidence.
People are much more likely to complete the action you want them to complete when you express value and the "why" behind the request. If they don't have good reasons to take that action, then they're less likely to take it. By expressing value and focusing on the reader through "you" language, we got more people to complete the survey!
It's also worth noting the the treatment variant led to two donations through the subsequent instant donation page compared to none from the control variant.
We will roll out the treatment variant to the page given the direction lift in email acquisition.

experiments.title: How a slide-out with value prop affected Co-author sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prominently featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author will achieve an increase in Co-Author shares because the slide-out not only educates on the value, it makes it easier to share..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge allows users to share their page access with a friend or family member—making them a Co-Author on their CaringBridge page. We hypothesized that making this a more prominent feature on the user’s homepage, would not only help inform them what a Co-Author is—but would also increase the sign-up/share rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The main finding of the experiment is that featuring a slide-out for CaringBridge users without a Co-author led to a statistically significant increase in the rate of sending invitations to co-authors.
However, the level of confidence suggests that further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of the slide-out feature in promoting co-author shares and to identify the conditions and segments where it works best. Future experiments could focus on refining the design of the slide-out or testing different formats or placements to assess their impact on co-author shares.

experiments.title: How changing the language for existing donors impacts email conversion rates second iteration
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that recognizing a donor’s status for donors will achieve a change in conversion rate because we are showing them that they are more than a email address.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/05/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
One of their best-performing offers is a book titled Perfect Ending; this is an offer they run on multiple channels at least once a year. For this specific email appeal, we wanted to test whether recognizing the impact a donor has had on the ministry by changing the language in an email would impact their conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that by referencing that they were a donor, they would be more likely to make a donation. This test was run for the active donor segment only.
We previously tested adding one line of donor language but did not see much of an impact. For this iteration, we added donor language in 2 locations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that recognizing a donor's status in emails for donors is likely to increase the conversion rate. The treatment group showed a 50.2% increase in donations, but further testing is required to confirm the validity of the results. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing the impact of donor language in email appeals and measure the level of statistical confidence to determine the validity of the results. It is also important to continue testing different variations of language to optimize for the highest conversion rate. The findings from this experiment can guide future email appeal strategies for First Baptist Dallas (Pathway to Victory) to encourage donors to continue supporting the ministry.

 

In regards to revenue, we saw a 47.7% increase with a 87.1% level of confidence for the treatment version

Control average gift, $56.67

Treatment average gift, $61.42

experiments.title: How using images of politicians impacted petition signatures
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images of well-known politicians for petition signers will achieve an increase in signatures because the motivation to sign would be more heavily impacted by emotion by association.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2023 - 01/28/2023
On a petition to repeal alcohol tax, we wondered if instead of using a standard image of the issue itself, how petition signers would respond to images of the politicians in the decision-making seats who were the ones implementing the policies. Our hypothesis was that the treatments that used politician images would invoke more emotion from visitors which might fuel their momentum to complete the petition. We split traffic 3 ways and let the test run for 16 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using images of famous politicians for petition signers did not achieve the intended increase in signatures. The control version of the petition outperformed the treatment with a statistical level of confidence approaching validity. The use of politician images did not motivate visitors to sign the petition, and some categories of visitors, such as social, female, and 25-34 age group, had a significant decrease in signatures with a high level of confidence. Desktop and other visitors had an increase in signatures, but these results were not consistent across all traffic.

In future experimentation, it may be necessary to test the use of images of politicians in a more targeted way, such as using images of politicians who are in favor of repealing the alcohol tax. Also, it may be useful to test different types of emotional appeals, beyond the association with politicians, to motivate visitors to sign the petition. The language used in both versions of the petition was similar, so testing different wording may also be beneficial for future experiments. It is also important to consider the characteristics and preferences of different visitor segments when implementing new designs or strategies.

experiments.title: How Adding Privacy Language on an Acquisition Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement to the email series acquisition pages for visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate and donor conversion rate because there is a perceived sense of added security and communication guidelines.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2023
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They run several different acquisition offers to draw in donors. Several of these people that land on an acquisition page might not know who FOTF is, or if they do they might be worried that they will get their inboxes spammed with unwanted emails. They also could be concerned about data privacy.
We decided to run a series of tests to determine the impact of adding a statement promising to protect their privacy on the landing pages and donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could increase email acquisition and downstream donor conversion by committing to protect their privacy. We also wanted to test if we could further increase the value of the offer by also clarifying what types of emails they would receive.
We created two treatments. One emphasizing our commitment to privacy and the other incorporating a statement of the types of emails they would receive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a privacy statement on landing pages for email series acquisition from visitors can have a positive impact on email acquisition. Specifically, our A/B experiment found there to be a 19.4% increase in email acquisition with a 90.4% statistical level of confidence, though this confidence level was not high enough to be considered valid. This is an encouraging result and indicates that this privacy statement can be helpful to visitors in making their decision to provide their information and sign up for the offer.
Most importantly, we increased donor conversion downstream by 63.3% with a 94.5% level of confidence in the treatment promising "relevant practical resources for your family."  This reveals that committing to provide ongoing value while protecting privacy increases the perceived value of the content offered and increases donor generosity.

experiments.title: How revamping email subscription button copy affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate because it will create a more immediate response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2022 - 03/04/2022
If we change the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency, email conversion will increase because users need to act immediately. We tested four different variants against our control language “join us” for this experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall email conversion was directionally higher for "Sign Up" and "Join Now."
• Mobile email conversion was directionally higher for "Join Now."
• Desktop email conversion was directionally higher for all variants.
• Of the five, "sign up" (13.7%) and "join now" (24.1%) had higher email capture rates.
• Mobile email conversion was higher for "join now" (19%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (64%).
• All four variants' desktop conversion was higher at a non-statistically valid confidence levels.
• Recommendation is not changing the HP email copy due to lack of statistical significance.
• We could potentially test "sign up" vs. "join now" button language in the future, to see if there is a more significant result with fewer variants since both had a more significant email conversion rate.

experiments.title: How placement of a premium link impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by moving the placement of the link higher in the email copy for email subscribers will achieve an increase in conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that generates a majority of its revenue through monthly premium offers. Throughout the month, Pathway to Victory will email its file 4-5 times a month about the premium featured.
For the first email about the November 2022 premium, we decided to look at the placement of the link to get the premium. This particular premium is one that PTV has featured before and has seen great success. The success of this offer presented us with the opportunity to test having the link to the premium higher in the email copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did see an increase in email clicks, 16% with a 92.5% level of confidence, we did not see a significant increase in donations or revenue.

In fact we saw a very slight decrease of donation conversions by 2% with a 6% level of confidence.

 

So while we did see an increase in email engagement this did not translate to an increase in donations or revenue.

 

Further testing ideas for this offer via email include, specific dollar amount ask, other placement tests and images of the premium in the email copy.

experiments.title: How offering a free ebook for email acquisition in the home page footer instead of a general email subscription form impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free ebook as an email acquisition method in the home page footer for page vistiors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2022 - 10/13/2022
In their home page footer, TGC gives users the opportunity to subscribe to email updates. The section says “Email Updates” and users can enter their email below and sign up. We wondered if offering a free ebook to users when they sign up would acquire more emails than the existing “email updates” sign-up opportunity, so we tested the idea. In the footer, we replaced the email updates section with an offer to receive a popular ebook for free. We included value prop telling the user how the ebook could help them and below was a sign up form including first name, last name, and email, and a button that read “Get my free ebook »”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control saw a 0.03% conversion rate compared to a 0.02% conversion rate for the treatment. Ultimately, the treatment had a 26.6% decrease in emails acquired with an 89.1% level of confidence. Users were more likely to sign up for email updates than to receive a free ebook.

Given that TGC offers a ton of content, which users can see as they scroll through the home page and down to the footer of the page, a user's primary intent may be to receive email updates for new content and new articles. So the opportunity to receive only a single free ebook is limiting compared to receiving lots of updates with lots of new content.

We're going to run this same experiment on TGC's Commentaries page to see if/how the user motivation adjusts from the home page to the commentaries page.

It may be a good idea to test adding value prop language to the home page footer in line with receiving email updates. Right now, the sign up opportunity only says "Email Updates" offering no value prop as to why the user might want to sign up for email updates.

experiments.title: How adding Value Prop language to sign up form impacted emails acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding value prop for website visitors will achieve increased emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2022 - 09/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry that has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on emails acquired through the sign up form on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 25.0% decrease in email acquired with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 13.7% decrease in email acquired with an 88% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 22.1% decrease in email acquired with a 93% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 56.3% decrease in email acquired with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an email acquisition form directly to a relevant blog page impacted offer sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an offer sign up opportunity directly below a related and important blog article for page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2022 - 08/04/2022
Reasons to Believe built an offer in which users could welcome the organization’s newly appointed CEO by submitting an “encouraging note” to him. The offer was set up in its traditional way: After clicking on an ad or email link, users would go to a landing page where they would read about this opportunity to submit a personal note. They could then write the note and submit it after providing their first name, last name, and email address.
RTB also posted a blog article on the news of naming the successor to their longstanding president and CEO. We wanted to test how adding the same acquisition form below the blog content would impact email submissions. So instead of reading about the opportunity to submit a personal note, users would read about the news through the blog article and be given the opportunity to submit the personal note afterwards.
For the treatment, we added a bolded call-to-action after the blog article and then added the same acquisition form as we used in the control. We wanted to test which presentation of the offer would lead to more email conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 60% lift in email conversions but only with a 72.5% level of confidence. The experiment did not validate. This offer could only run for a limited amount of time since it was news related. But it might be worth testing again in a similar capacity to see if there is in fact a trend in email conversion for a blog-level acquisition opportunity.
The experiment also revealed that returning visitors were twice as likely to convert than new visitors, which tells us that returning visitors are more familiar with RTB blogs and would perhaps get more out of reading a blog article about a new president taking over for the former president. If a similar offer is run again, it could be tested further for returning visitors.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials in a sidebar impacted email acquisitions on a free eBook offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility via book reviews for a free eBook offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 08/05/2022
We wanted to find out if increasing the appeal by adding social proof through credibility and evidentials would improve email acquisition rates on a free eBook offer. We took testimonial quotes from another author and from one of the organization’s trusted pastors and council members and featured them on the landing page to further reinforce to landing page visitors that this was a valuable book offer that was available for free. We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed as a 2.2% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence. The results were pretty much flat but overall we saw a slight decrease in the percentage of total emails acquired across all traffic for those who experienced the treatment version of the landing page. Our hypothesis is that visitors could have been unfamiliar with those who were quoted in the testimonials. Because the recommendations were coming from another author and from a pastor rather than a layperson, it could have been assumed that this offer was only suitable for those in positions of ministry or Christian writers—rather than a person in the general workforce or average home. This could have led some people to think the offer was not intended for them and so they did not take the next step. Some changes in visitor segments in the experiment that were interesting to note were that while direct visitors had a 16.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence, email visitors had a 14.0% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. It's possible that email visitors could have been more receptive to the treatment since they are likely to be more familiar with the style and nature of TGC book reviews if they have been receiving regular communications via email from the organization.

experiments.title: How does paginating a multi-section survey affect email acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that paginating a multi-section survey for potential donors will achieve higher email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 07/10/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates on a multi-section survey offer, we wanted to test whether or not breaking a long 19-question survey into more manageable, less-intimidating chunks would decrease the perceived “cost” of completing the survey, in terms of time, and decrease perceived friction enough to improve sign-up rates.
We ran this experiment for 28 days, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 28 days, we observed a 31.5% lift in email acquisition rates, with a 97% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis that by reducing the perceived cost of completing the survey, in terms of time, visitors would experience less friction and would be more motivated to complete a multi-section, 19-question survey.

The results of this experiment suggest that presenting all 19 survey questions on one long page may have made completing the survey a long and laborious task, increasing the perceived cost (time) of taking the survey to a point where it outweighed the perceived value in the minds of prospects.

By breaking the survey into more manageable chunks, with each section on its own page, the perceived cost of completing the survey was reduced in the minds of prospects while the persuasion principle of 'behavioral consistency' likely helped to motivate prospects to complete the survey.

For mobile users, the experiment produced a 100.3% lift in acquisition rates, with a 100% level of confidence, likely due to the decreased scroll and potential "gamification" presented by the paginated version.

Based on these results, we recommend that organizations with longer survey offers consider paginating such surveys to reduce the perceived cost and leverage the behavioral principle of 'behavioral consistency' to lift acquisition rates.

experiments.title: The impact on email acquisition of a content offer image placed above the fold for mobile visitors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the eBook image above-the-fold will increase email acquisition for mobile visitors to the landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 06/17/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. 7 Scriptures to Pray for the Child in your Life is one of Buckner’s most important online premium offers on Facebook. It has the highest number of emails acquired, yet its landing page has one of the lowest conversion rates.
When viewing the landing page on mobile devices, the image appeared all the way at the bottom of the page, below the form.
We’ve seen in testing with similar organizations that placing an image above-the-fold for mobile visitors can increase email acquisition. Since the majority of traffic to that page is mobile, we wanted to see if making the eBook image more prominent would likewise increase email for Buckner’s traffic to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: During the experiment, we were not able to validate the test due to lower traffic to the page. However, since we came close to validation (with between a 75% and 84% level of confidence) and didn't see any negative impacts from the placement, we rolled out the top of fold image for all traffic to the page.

experiments.title: How will adding a value proposition to an email sign-up form affect acquisition rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a value proposition to an email sign-up form will increase email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2022 - 07/28/2022
In an effort to increase email acquisition rates for a public policy client, we wanted to test the impact of adding language that illustrates the value of signing up to receive email communications on email acquisition rates.
We tested this concept on a pop-up form that appears after website visitors click a “get updates’ button on the header of their site. The “control” version is a plain form with no headline or body copy above the form fields.
The treatment adds a headline and body copy to reinforce the value subscribers receive by signing up for email communications. We set this experiment up as an A/B test, splitting traffic evenly between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 34 days, we observed a 60.8% lift in email acquisition rates, with an 81.7% level of confidence. And encouragingly, new site visitors experienced a 284% lift in form submission, with a 94% level of confidence.

And while we were unable to collect enough samples to validate our hypothesis, the results of this experiment suggest that conveying the value of signing up for email communications may provide prospects with more motivation to share their email address. As data privacy becomes increasingly important to internet users ... and as inboxes become stuffed with more and more junk and graymail, it becomes increasingly important for organizations to place the value of signing up for your email communications front and center.

While reviewing the data we did realize that more than one concept was tested in the treatment version of the form. Not only was value proposition language added, but the form fields were also reduced in an effort to reduce friction. And while it always feels great to improve performance, testing these two concepts concurrently makes it impossible to know with certainty which of these changes produced the observed lift, and which to what degree. However, due to the encouraging results of this experiment, we decided to roll the treatment version out to 100% of traffic going forward.

experiments.title: How adding an ebook incentive in a survey impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ebook incentive to a survey for survey takers will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2022 - 06/10/2022
RTB launched a new offer: Science and Faith Survey. Surveys have historically produced a lower sign up rate than other offers like ebooks. In order to provide additional incentive to provide contact information as well as provide additional value for the site visitor, we included a checkbox at the end of the survey that, if checked, will send them a free ebook. Our hypothesis is that this extra added incentive and value, will increase email acquisition.
Traffic to this offer was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, we did see a directional 13% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 93%. This is counter to what our initial hypothesis had stated. A possibility is that some users saw this as an extra step in their process instead of an added incentive and therefore did not perceive the ebook as an added value. However, looking at these users at the instant donation page step, the users who downloaded the ebook were more likely to take action on the instant donation page. In fact, we saw a directional 383% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. This tells us that while many did not take advantage of the added incentive, those who understood the value of this ebook were more motivated to give to Reasons to Believe after they submitted their survey responses.
Potential next steps include:
Prechecking the box for the ebook; checking the box may be adding additional friction and more "work" for the end userAdding an image of the eBook to reinforce the added value Add another step after the survey to get the ebook in order to increase the donor conversion rate

experiments.title: Does creating more clarity in the text of a landing page affect email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that broader copy and less imposing text for course prospects will achieve more course sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2021 - 10/02/2021
For RTB’s Listen and Learn course, we wanted to test broader language that may not have been as intimidating for the course prospect. We removed language like “22-part audio series” and “part 1” details. We also limited the amount of bolded text. We thought copy that appeared passive and read more broadly might result in more email sign-ups.
Traffic to this free online course primarily came through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a directional 16% decrease in email acquisition (level of confidence 93%). We had theorized that the extra detail regarding the course were a point of friction for landing page visitors but the data seems to indicate that RTB's audience is actually looking for these details to make their decision.
The course-specific language resonated better with users and resulted in more emails acquired than the broader language in the treatment.
Users like to know the specifics of a course and appreciate bolded details. It gives them greater clarity as they decide to sign up for a new course.

experiments.title: How promoting a subscription type instead of a content offer impacted email signup rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that promoting an eBook in the “dear reader” ads for visitors to the newsroom will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/04/2022 - 01/14/2022
As a part of experimenting with article traffic conversion opportunities, we wanted to experiment with whether promoting a subscription would increase the email signup and donor conversion rates over promoting a downloadable PDF eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not shown here was that email signup rates decreased by 51.5% (LoC: 99.6%). It appears that the downloadable content isn't as motivating to subscribe (and subsequently to give) when it comes to this ad unit type.

Our recommendation is to test a different offer type (a petition) now that we have seen that this content offer type was defeated by the subscription promotion.

Other notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 77.5% decrease with a 90% level of confidence.
Returning visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How framing the message around the donor’s impact affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: A landing page with donor-centric language will have a higher conversion rate than one that focuses on the accomplishments as a result of Heritage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2014 - 12/17/2014
The standard messaging points employed by Heritage have been refined over many years across multiple channels. At the start of the year end campaign, we decided to test how the information is presented to the donor. The control uses standard messaging while the new treatment would take the same information but frame it around how the accomplishments were the result of the donor, not the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The messaging points of Heritage have not changed but we have learned that we need to frame them around the donor instead of around the organization.

experiments.title: Does adding a credibility block to a content offer increases email acquisition?
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding a credibility block highlighting WUNC’s editorial team to the short-copy landing page will increase email sign-ups (primary KPI) and increase donation conversion rate (secondary KPI) by reinforcing trust and perceived value without changing the streamlined structure that previously improved performance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/28/2026 - 02/23/2026
WUNC tested whether adding a credibility block to the winning short-copy version of the Get Out Guide landing page would impact performance. The short-copy version (problem–solution narrative, streamlined body copy, no bullets) had previously won and became the new control. The test version held all layout and copy constant while introducing a credibility block highlighting WUNC’s reporters and editorial expertise. The goal was to isolate the effect of the credibility element alone.
experiments.keyLearnings:
🧠 Overall Takeaway
This test does not provide sufficient evidence to adopt or remove the credibility block based on performance impact. The directional split between lower email acquisition and higher donation behavior suggests the block may influence downstream trust signals differently than initial sign-up intent, but the data is not statistically reliable enough to support a firm decision.
📊 Performance Metrics
Primary KPI: Email Sign-Ups (Acquisition Rate)
Control: 90 / 346 = 26.01%Test: 75 / 324 = 23.15%Absolute Difference: -2.86 percentage pointsRelative Lift: -11.00%Two-proportion z-test (two-sided) p-value: 0.390Statistical Confidence: 61.0%
Secondary KPI (Conditional Donation Rate Among Email Sign-Ups)
Control: 6 / 90 = 6.67%Test: 10 / 75 = 13.33%Absolute Difference: +6.67 percentage pointsRelative Lift: +100.0%Two-proportion z-test (two-sided) p-value: 0.156Statistical Confidence: 84.4%
Supporting Metric (Observed): Average Gift
Control: $17.50Test: $27.00
Required sample sizes shown in reporting interface:
Email acquisition validity threshold: 1,739 total sample (actual: 670)Transaction validity threshold: 979 total sample (actual: 670)
✂️ Segment Breakdown
All TrafficTraffic source: 100% Meta ads
⚠️ Limitations
Total sample (670) below required validity thresholds for both primary and secondary KPIs.Known client issue where the email form sometimes does not render; additional JS required for the test may have exacerbated this issue (not confirmed as affecting experiment).All traffic sourced from Meta ads, limiting channel diversity.Seasonal timing: outdoors guide run during winter months.
🚀 Next Steps
Option 1: Adopt the credibility block as control and test focused optimizations to increase email acquisition rate.Option 2: Remove the credibility block and test an alternative designed to improve both email and donation conversion simultaneously.

experiments.title: Does Making the “Last Name” Field Optional Increase Email Sign-Ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the “Last Name” field optional on content offer sign-up forms will increase email acquisition because reducing required effort lowers friction at the moment of decision and makes the form feel simpler and less intrusive to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/24/2026
Luther Seminary uses its Illustrated Book of Acts and Enter the Bible Exodus content offers as ongoing acquisition tools to introduce new audiences to its ministry and grow its email list. These resources consistently attract individuals interested in biblical scholarship and theological education and serve as an important entry point into long term engagement.
As the team reviewed the landing page experience for these offers, they identified a potential source of friction within the sign up form. The form required three fields to be completed: first name, last name, and email address. While collecting a full name supports personalization and database management, required fields can introduce subtle resistance, especially for individuals who are early in their relationship with the organization.
This raised an important motivational question. When someone is downloading free theological content, are they willing to provide only the minimum required information, and does requiring a last name create enough additional effort or privacy concern to reduce completion rates?
Rather than removing the last name field entirely, Luther Seminary tested making the field optional while still requiring first name and email address. This approach preserved the opportunity to collect full names from those willing to provide them while lowering the minimum amount of required information needed to complete the form.
Two split URL tests were conducted, one for Illustrated Book of Acts and one for Enter the Bible Exodus. In each case, the page experience was identical except for the required fields on the form.
Control
First name required
Last name required
Email required
Treatment
First name required
Last name optional
Email required
Because both offers tested the same structural change with similar acquisition audiences, the results were aggregated to evaluate the overall impact of reducing required form fields across content campaigns.
The primary metric was email acquisition measured by form completion rate. Secondary metrics included overall transactions, one time gifts, recurring gifts, and revenue to ensure that any improvement in top of funnel performance did not negatively affect downstream donor value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test reached 77.2% statistical confidence, which does not meet the 95% level of confidence threshold for a valid learning. Therefore, we cannot conclude that making the “Last Name” field optional had a meaningful impact on email acquisition. The observed differences between control and treatment may be due to normal variation rather than a true behavioral effect.

Email Acquisition Results

Control
119 conversions out of 432 visitors
 	27.5% conversion rate
104 conversions out of 434 visitors
 	24.0% conversion rate
Transaction Results
11 transactions out of 432 visitors
 	2.55% transaction rate
16 transactions out of 434 visitors
 	3.69% transaction rate

experiments.title: Can framing reporter credibility  increase email sign-ups for a content offer?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that emphasizing the credibility of the reporter who created the Education 101 email series for homepage visitors would increase email acquisition because authority and expertise cues can build trust and reduce hesitation at the moment of sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2025 - 11/30/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving North Carolina. This experiment tested homepage pop-up messaging for an Education 101 email series content offer. The standard pop-up was compared against a variant that emphasized the credibility and expertise of the WUNC reporter who created the series. The test was prioritized to explore whether authority-based framing could enhance email acquisition without increasing friction or disrupting the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
Big takeaway:
Performance metrics
Control
 	Samples: 8,874
 	Emails acquired: 34
 	Conversion rate: 0.38%
 	Treatment
 	Samples: 8,949
 	Emails acquired: 36
 	Conversion rate: 0.40%
 	Relative lift: +5.0%
 	Confidence level: 16.1%
Segment notes
No segment-level breakouts were available for this test.
Statistical significance
Result is not statistically valid.
 	Confidence (16.1%) is far below the 95% threshold.
 	The experiment did not reach the required sample size for validation.
 	Timing for this test could have affected results.

experiments.title: Can shortened, problem–solution copy increase Get Out Guide sign-ups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing a shortened version of the Get Out Guide landing page for all visitors would increase email sign-ups because quickly establishing the reader’s problem, clearly presenting WUNC’s solution, and simplifying the layout would improve relevance, readability, and trust, encouraging more visitors to complete the sign-up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2025 - 12/18/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving central North Carolina, producing local journalism and cultural coverage supported by its audience. As part of its 2025 growth strategy, WUNC is focused on expanding its email audience through content-led offers that introduce new users to its editorial value and create long-term engagement opportunities.
This experiment tested a revised landing page for WUNC’s Get Out Guide, a content offer promoted primarily through Meta ads to both new audiences and existing followers. Building on a previous winning approach from the Education 101 series, the team prioritized this test to evaluate whether shorter, more focused copy could improve email acquisition and downstream donations without changing the core offer. The test compared the existing long-form page with bullet points against a streamlined, problem–solution–driven version designed to improve clarity, flow, and perceived relevance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Big takeaway:

The shortened copy outperformed the long version on both email acquisition volume and conversion rate, but results did not reach statistical significance. Performance was directionally positive and supports further iteration rather than immediate rollout.

experiments.title: Does Identity-Based Messaging Outperform Recipient-Based Messaging on a Campaign Landing Page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing appeal through the lens of the donor’s identity for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because identity-based messaging increases personal relevance and motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2025 - 10/29/2025
This test ran on Bible League Canada’s Transforming India landing page, which aimed to send Bibles to waiting believers. We wanted to explore whether donor identity-based messaging could outperform the standard recipient-based messaging.
The control version emphasized the hardship and urgency experienced by Christians in India, featuring a clear call to action. The treatment led with a direct appeal to the donor’s faith and identity. The narrative remained the same, but the message positioned the donor as an active participant in Bible League’s mission.
This A/B test ran for the month of October and was split 50/50 amongst website traffic. Visitors came from a mix of meta ads, email appeals, and organic website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

This test did not produce a statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. Both versions generated nearly identical numbers of donations, with a 0.8% relative decrease in revenue per visitor. The average gift size was slightly higher in the treatment, but still not enough to come to a strong conclusion. The biggest difference was the decrease in recurring gifts (5 vs 1), but this wasn't at a statistically-significant level of confidence.

Framing the landing page message around donor identity did not outperform a more traditional, recipient-focused messaging. This may suggest that:
There was not a clear enough difference between the control and treatment.
 	The visitors didn't have much of a preference between the two versions.
 	The donors' faith identity was already assumed through earlier steps in the journey
Implications for Future Experimentation:
Testing the identity-based language earlier in the donor journey
 	Combining identity with stronger urgency or social proof to create a more layered motivation
 	Identity framing language in a colder traffic segment where shared beliefs aren't assumed

experiments.title: Does More Context Increase Email Sign-Ups for Content Offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that providing more context and credibility through longer copy—including a testimonial, bullet points, and a detailed explanation—would better persuade users to sign up for the email series by building trust and emotional resonance.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/02/2025 - 10/13/2025
This test evaluated the impact of copy length on the Education 101 email series landing page, which offers readers a free series covering public education policy in North Carolina.
The long copy version included:
A quote from the author
A longer headline
Bullet points outlining what users would learn
Contextual background on the reporter and WUNC’s editorial credibility
The short copy version removed these supporting elements and focused directly on the offer. The shorter layout also surfaced the reporter’s bio above the fold on most screen sizes.
Note: While this test was part of a broader effort to improve Meta-driven performance, all traffic in this experiment came from Meta ads. That context is useful when interpreting results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
🔍 Main Takeaway
The shorter copy version generated more email signups, but the difference was not statistically significant (58% confidence). This suggests the difference was likely due to specific copy and layout decisions, not just length alone.
Since the two versions differed in multiple ways—headline, presence of a quote, bullet points, and bio visibility—we cannot isolate what caused the performance lift. This test shows that this specific short version outperformed this specific long version, not that short copy always wins.
Performance Metrics and Insights
Email SignupsControl (long copy): 387 signupsTest (short copy): 414 signupsConfidence Level: 58%Conclusion: Not statistically significantDonations from Landing Page: 14 totalDue to the low volume of gifts, the team is reevaluating the Meta audience to improve lead quality and downstream conversion. Initial indicators suggest the launch targeting may have missed the mark.
Next Steps
Refine Meta Targeting: Adjust audiences to improve quality of leads and conversion potential.Refactor Copy Strategically:
Add back bullet points or a testimonial to the short version to see if trust and clarity can be blended effectively.Highlight the reporter’s identity more clearly—especially above the fold.
Address Visual Distractions:
Test removing or modifying the left-hand sidebar, which may distract users from the form.
Form Optimization:
Explore simplifying the form fields to reduce dropoff and improve completion.

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Moody Radio acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 08/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Moody Radio’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which simplified the opt-in copy and removed the form frame for Moody Radio's acquisition landing page, did not lead to an increase in email opt-ins compared to the control. The treatment actually resulted in a 5.0% decrease in Opt-in checkbox clicked for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 60.2%. Therefore, the hypothesis that reducing friction and decision fatigue would streamline the process for users and increase opt-ins was not supported by the data.

On the positive side, the experiment did show a significant increase in Revenue by 216% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while the simplified messaging did not lead to more opt-ins, it may have had a positive impact on revenue generation.

Moving forward, the insights gained from this experiment suggest that when making changes to acquisition landing pages, it is essential to carefully consider the potential impact on user behavior and to thoroughly test any hypothesis before implementing changes. Additionally, when interpreting results, it is important to focus on statistical significance levels of at least 95% to ensure the validity of the findings. Finally, while an increase in revenue is positive, it should be balanced with other key metrics such as opt-in rates to fully evaluate the effectiveness of a new approach.

experiments.title: How changing the placement of an eBook a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because By presenting the offer right away, we reduce friction and maximize clarity, especially for users in scanning mode.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Dueling Populisms”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Further Results:

Transactions:
Control: 38 donations
 	Treatment: 37 donations
 	A 2.63% decrease
Control: $1,434
 	Treatment: $1,399
 	A 2.44% decrease
The treatment showed a modest but meaningful increase (+4.4%) in email acquisition by moving the ebook offer to the top of the page. However, this result was not statistically significant. More testing needs to be done to confirm that above-the-fold visibility enhances user engagement for lead-generation offers, particularly when screen space is limited.
While email acquisition increased slightly, there was no significant difference in downstream revenue or transactions. This suggests that while placement can improve micro-conversions (like lead capture), it doesn’t automatically translate into macro-conversions (like donations or purchases). Additional nurturing may be needed.
This experiment reinforces that even small changes to content hierarchy — without altering the offer itself — can lead to improved performance. This is especially important for high-traffic, high-value landing pages like this one.
Test the size or prominence of the CTA button
 	Try shorter copy on just the mobile audience
 	Trigger a popup if the user has gone idle for a bit... to see if you can remind them to signup.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page redesign impact email signups for a video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
This organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition for the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.
 	The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the control and treatment pages.
 	Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: How the placement of an ebook image on a landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the ebook image directly under the headline on the landing page for Hoover’s mobile audience will achieve an increase email acquisition and donation rates because eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive. because  eading with a visual representation of the offer will capture attention more quickly, establish trust, and reinforce the value of the content before the user scrolls—ultimately making the landing page more persuasive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2025 - 06/30/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank based at Stanford University. Hoover is committed to advancing ideas that promote economic prosperity, individual liberty, and a constitutional government.
For this test, we focused on the content offer “Three Keys to Unlock the American Economy”, with a specific emphasis on mobile user behavior.
The core question we wanted to answer was: Does the visual placement of the ebook image impact performance for mobile users?
In the control, the ebook image was positioned further down the page, requiring users to scroll to view it. In the treatment, the ebook image was placed directly beneath the headline, making it immediately visible upon landing. Our aim was to determine if making the offer more visually concrete up front would increase conversions—particularly email acquisitions and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings & Takeaways:
Visual Priority May Not Always Drive Action - While it’s often effective to lead with a visual cue—especially for mobile users—the results suggest that immediate exposure to the ebook image did not enhance performance. In fact, conversions were slightly lower in the treatment. It’s possible that the early image displaced other persuasive copy or created a “banner blindness” effect.Perceived Value Comes From Message, Not Just Visual - This experiment suggests that copy structure and headline framing may carry more weight than visuals in persuading users to submit their email. Users might be more compelled by clarity around what they’ll gain from the ebook than the ebook’s image itself.Small Sample Sizes = Direction, Not Decision - With limited traffic and low donation volume, this test doesn’t give us statistically significant answers, but it provides a useful signal that visual-first design changes on mobile should be introduced cautiously and tested against message clarity.
Next Steps & Future Testing:
•	Test Hybrid Layouts: Consider a version where the ebook image is still visible early—but paired more tightly with benefit-driven subhead copy to retain persuasive momentum.
•	Run Similar Tests on Higher-Traffic Offers: This pattern is worth validating at a larger scale, especially on mobile-focused offers with greater reach.

experiments.title: How changing the copy above the comment box affects comments
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy above the comment box for profile visitors will achieve an increase in comments on posts because the copy options encourage users to leave a comment by describing how it would positively affect the user who posted.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/29/2025 - 07/07/2025
CaringBridge has a feature that allows users to leave comments on posts. There is a line of copy that encourages users to leave a comment on a post. For this test, we wanted to test how changing that copy option would affect users leaving comments on posts. This was tested on site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy above the comment box on site profiles resulted in a small increase in comments left for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 46.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that there is a sufficient sample size and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the copy to see if there is a stronger impact on user behavior. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence user engagement, such as the design of the comment box, the visibility of the call-to-action, or the overall user experience.

experiments.title: How adding a quote in email copy impacts email acquisition and donor conversion with a nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a quote from Victor Davis Hanson to the body of the email for Hoover’s nondonor audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donations because Hoover’s non-donor audience may still be familiar with or influenced by VDH’s commentary, and a quote could deepen their interest in downloading the ebook or even making a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank that champions principles of individual, economic, and political freedom. This specific experiment was targeting their nondonor audience with an email promoting Victor Davis Hanson’s latest eBook: Ten Paradoxes of Our Age. The goal was to determine whether adding a VDH quote to the body of the email would increase email acquisitions and donations by leveraging name recognition and the intellectual appeal of Hanson’s thought leadership.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Donations:
Control - 24
Treatment - 21
Resulting in a -12.5 decrease in donations, with a 61.5% level of confidence.
Key Learnings:
Consistent Pattern Across Audiences - As with the donor test, adding the VDH quote did not improve performance. In both audiences, the control yielded slightly higher email acquisitions and donations, suggesting that the quote, in this placement and form, may not serve as a persuasive enhancement.Familiarity Likely Lower for Non-Donors - While donors likely recognize VDH by name, the non-donor audience may be less familiar with Hoover’s fellows, reducing the quote’s impact. In this context, the quote may have felt disconnected or abstract, lacking the built-in credibility it holds for existing supporters.Clarity and Value Remain Key for Non-Donors - The control version’s clear structure, focused entirely on the value of the content and offer, appears to slightly outperform the version that introduced a more reflective tone via the quote. This reinforces the importance of direct, benefit-driven messaging when engaging cold or newer audiences.
Recommendations for Future Strategy:
•	Tailor Thought Leader Content to Audience Awareness - Before introducing name-based authority elements like quotes, assess whether the audience has adequate familiarity or affinity. If not, it may be more effective to frame thought leaders through introductory or contextual narrative, rather than standalone quotes.
•	Reserve Quotes for Warm Audiences or Use Them as Lead-ins - Rather than placing the quote at the end, test it at the top of the email as a hook or curiosity driver, especially for audiences that already show signs of interest in similar topics.
•	Continue Focusing on Clear Offer Framing for Acquisition - The control’s stronger performance across both experiments suggests that clear, benefit-led messaging consistently works—especially in acquisition campaigns. Personal or philosophical flourishes should be carefully tested before inclusion.
•	Future Test Opportunity - Try using a quote in the landing page or post-download email, where it might provide credibility reinforcement after the initial decision to click has been made.

experiments.title: How connecting an offer’s impact to the organizations mission impact email acquisition on a funnel landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “Connecting an offer’s impact to the organization’s mission for Note of Encouragement landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it reinforces the visitor’s sense of purpose and alignment with the organization’s values, increasing the likelihood of engagement and boosting email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/16/2025
To boost email acquisition rates on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested new landing page copy that connected the user’s action to IMB’s mission and the Great Commission. The goal was to present the act as a meaningful partnership in the gospel, rather than just a kind gesture, with the expectation that this would drive higher email acquisition rates.
By clearly linking how a simple note supports missionaries and aligns with IMB’s mission, this version gives users a stronger reason to engage and complete the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that connecting an offer's impact to the organization's mission on the Note of Encouragement landing page did not lead to an increase in email acquisition rates. Despite the hypothesis that reinforcing the visitor's sense of purpose and alignment with the organization's values would boost engagement, the treatment actually saw a 2% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.
 	It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 36.0%, well below the 95% threshold needed for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and messaging to determine what resonates best with users. It may be beneficial to explore other ways of tying the visitor's action to the organization's mission and values to see if a different approach yields more positive results. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that future experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How reframing funnel landing page copy as a gospel partnership impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reframing the funnel landing page copy for “Note of Encouragement” visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition because it shows how a simple note strengthens the mission of IMB therefore giving the visitor a stronger reason to engage.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2025 - 06/16/2025
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s Note of Encouragement funnel, we tested into landing page copy that would frame the act as a way to partner in the gospel versus a transactional experience. We wanted their audience to feel like they were truly playing a part in the mission of IMB.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which reframed the landing page copy for "Note of Encouragement" visitors to emphasize partnering in the gospel, resulted in a 2.2% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. However, the statistical confidence level of 40.0% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and unreliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the messaging and framing of the landing page copy to effectively communicate the value proposition to visitors and encourage email acquisition. A/B testing remains a valuable tool for optimizing conversion rates, but it is crucial to interpret results with caution and rely on statistically significant findings for decision making.

experiments.title: How changing the headline from conversational to more analytical impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the headline to a more formal and analytical headline for the Hoover audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition because more people will perceive the content as authoritative and intellectually valuable, appealing to readers interested in economic analysis..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2025 - 05/06/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of landing page headlines for one of their content offers, “Globalization and Its Discontents eBook. If the landing page headline is changed from a conversational phrasing “American Jobs vs. Affordable Goods: Can We Have Both?” to a more formal, analytical one “The Globalization Dilemma: Protecting Jobs vs. Lower Prices—Is There a Balance?”, then more users will perceive the content as authoritative and intellectually valuable, because the revised headline frames the topic as a balanced, data-driven dilemma—appealing to readers interested in serious economic analysis, which may increase their likelihood of submitting their email to access the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Takeaways:
Conversational Headlines May Drive Higher Engagement
The control's conversational tone ("Can We Have Both?") outperformed the more formal treatment in both email acquisition and donations—suggesting that a more accessible, human-centered approach may resonate better with visitors.
Headline Framing Has Measurable Influence on Email Signups
Although the difference in email acquisition was modest (3% decrease), the 83.9% confidence level indicates a likely negative impact from the more analytical, formal headline. Small language shifts can significantly affect user perception and action.
Donation Behavior May Be Sensitive to Perceived Approachability
The treatment saw a 14.2% drop in donation rate and a lower average gift amount, hinting that a formal or abstract headline may reduce emotional connection or urgency—key drivers of giving behavior.
Tone and Framing Should Align with Audience Expectations
While Hoover’s brand is scholarly, this test suggests users may still prefer direct, clear language that frames the issue as personally relevant rather than academic or complex.
Further Testing Ideas:
Position the eBook as a solution - change the offer description to emphasize a takeaway. Maybe something like: “A Practical Guide to Understanding Global Trade and Its Impact on You.Test the CTA "Download Your Copy Now"Test Value-Oriented vs. Curiosity-Oriented HeadlinesValue: “How Globalization Can Strengthen Both Jobs and Prices”Curiosity: “What Happens When Trade Meets Jobs? Find Out Inside”

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal thermometer impacts email conversion rate on funnel landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the introduction of a goal amount in the copy and a goal thermometer for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it reinforces social proof and urgency on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2025 - 04/17/2025
In an effort to boost email conversions on ACI’s Landing page, we opted to test into a goal thermometer on the page to focus in on urgency and social proof of joining a community of similarly minded individuals.
We have tested this concept on different ACI funnels and seen positive results, so we were hopeful to see the same for the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which introduced a goal amount in the copy and a goal thermometer, resulted in a 0.6% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 21.9% statistical level of confidence. This means that the control version, without the goal amount and thermometer, performed better than the treatment.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results with a high level of confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test variations of the treatment to see if different approaches to presenting the goal amount and thermometer could yield more positive results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to always validate results with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an A/B experiment.

experiments.title: How redesigning content blocks on the homepage impacted clicks.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redesigning content blocks for homepage visitors will achieve more clicks on said blocks.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2024 - 07/24/2024
This test explored the impact of redesigning the content blocks on the Homepage. The goal was to increase clicks (engagement) and conversion by creating a more enticing and easier to navigate look to the content. We changed copy to be in white box overlaying image, with CTAs presented as links and highlight teal when hovering. CTA links always teal on mobile.
Our hypothesis was that redesigning the content blocks on the homepage will make them more engaging for the visitor and will lead to higher clicks and ultimately higher donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the redesigned content blocks on the homepage did not lead to more clicks from visitors. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control version. Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.

This experiment also did not impact donor conversion rates:

Control: 5%

Treatment: 4.9%, which was a 1.3% decrease with a 53% level of confidence.

 

The differences between desktop and mobile were also relatively small:

Desktop

Control: 2.4% click through rate; 5.2% donor conversion rate

Treatment: 2.4% click through rate (1.1% increase with a 21% level of confidence); 5.2% donor conversion rate (0.38% decrease, 11% level of confidence).

 

Mobile

Control: 2.6% click through rate; 4.9% conversion rate.

Treatment: 2.5% click through rate (1.6% decrease with a 34% level of confidence); 4.7% conversion rate (2.3% decrease with a 62% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (standard) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
