An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Standard landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the redesigned landing page did not perform better than the original landing page in terms of email acquisition for all traffic. There was a 0.7% decrease in email signups with a statistical confidence level of 31.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the test are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the results and ensure that the statistical significance meets the minimum threshold before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on the test. Additionally, considering other factors like user feedback, usability testing, and further iterations of the redesigned landing page could help in determining what changes need to be made to improve email signups effectively.

experiments.title: How does a radical landing page (lighthouse) redesign impact email signups for video series?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a more structured and visually cohesive landing page design will improve readability and increase email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2024 - 08/23/2024
An organization frequently creates and promotes free video series as a key strategy in their email acquisition efforts. Each video series is housed on a dedicated landing page, but over time, these landing pages have become visually inconsistent and difficult to scan—especially on mobile.
To improve the user experience and increase email signups, we designed a radical redesign of their landing page. The redesigned page includes several key updates aimed at enhancing readability and structure:
A more centered layout with narrowed text width
Addition of the organization’s logo
A light background behind the email acquisition form
A distinct background color behind the episode list (or impact statement list)
A new footer that includes a link to the Privacy Policy
Because the formatting of their video series pages varies, we divided them into two separate test groups: Standard pages and Lighthouse pages. For each group, we created two versions of the page. This write-up details results from the Lighthouse landing pages:
Control: The original, existing landing page
Treatment: The redesigned landing page with improved readability and structure
Traffic was split 50/50 between the Control and Treatment in both versions of the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The redesigned landing page did not lead to a significant improvement in email signups compared to the original landing page. In fact, there was a slight decrease in email acquisitions when the Treatment page was used.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 47.7%, which is below the 85% threshold needed to consider the results valid. This indicates that the results could be due to random chance rather than a true difference between the Control and Treatment pages.

3. Given the inconclusive results of this experiment, it is important not to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on these findings. It would be advisable to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and potentially different designs to determine the true impact on email signups.

4. In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors such as the layout, design elements, and overall user experience when testing landing page variations. It may also be beneficial to explore different hypotheses and iterate on design changes to find the most effective approach for increasing email signups.

experiments.title: Are we presenting an acquisition offer in the best way on the landing page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the landing page for the Lent offer in MMS will achieve more form submissions because the page will be optimized for a mobile experience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2025
For this test we wanted to see if we could change the landing page copy for the Lent offer in MMS and see more email sign ups.
We made several changes to the design of the page to make it more mobile friendly in terms of scrolling and skimming.
Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the adjusted landing page for the Lent offer in MMS did not result in more form submissions compared to the original page. Despite efforts to make the page more mobile-friendly for easier scrolling and skimming, the treatment actually saw a decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 63.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test and iterate on the changes made to the landing page. It may be worth exploring different design elements, content, or CTAs to determine what truly resonates with the target audience and drives more form submissions. Additionally, ensuring a higher statistical level of confidence in the results will provide more reliable insights for decision making.

experiments.title: How adding more prayer centered language on a landing page impacted sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing emotional appeal, personal transformation, and prayer for the landing page viewer will achieve will see an increase in email acquisition rates because it better resonates with the audience’s desires and challenges in developing a deeper prayer life..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2025 - 03/04/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For this specific test, we tested whether rewriting the copy on Avila’s offer, Into the Deep, to be more prayer-centered would impact email acquisition. Since this offer is about prayer, we wanted to try engaging the viewer with emotional appeal and frame prayer as a deep personal need and struggle, addressing common challenges like distractions, uncertainty, and the desire for true communion with God. Presenting more of a problem and solution.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Takeaways:
The Treatment Showed Promise – The increase in email acquisition (+3.3%) and donations (+12.7%) suggests that the emotionally driven copy may be more persuasive.
Low Confidence Levels – The confidence levels (32.8% for email acquisition and 23.1% for donations) indicate high variability, meaning the test may need more traffic or a longer duration to reach statistical significance.
Next Steps:
Increase sample size – Running the test with more visitors could help clarify whether these trends hold.
Refine the treatment copy – Since the treatment showed promise, small tweaks (e.g., optimizing the CTA, adding social proof, or adjusting the donation appeal) could strengthen its impact.
Test a stronger CTA or incentive – A/B test a variation that further emphasizes urgency or value to see if it boosts conversions more reliably.

experiments.title: How using No-primed questions in a survey impacted engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “No-primed” questions for a survey offer will achieve an increase in survey completions (email submissions) because respondents will feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers, which may lead to a subconscious desire to resolve with a “Yes” when asked to contribute later.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2024 - 11/27/2024
During high-urgency campaigns such as calendar year-end, we often use a survey as a tool to help “prime” Luther Seminary’s audience with knowledge about the problems that Luther Seminary is working to solve through their fundraising campaign. Typically, these survey questions are “yes-primed”, which means that they’re designed so that a Luther Seminary supporter would respond “Yes” to each question.
For Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test this “yes-primed” framing against a survey that was “no-primed”. Our goal was to understand how this “Yes” or “No” priming impacts engagement and downstream donor behavior.
Both survey funnels were identical, with the only change being the phrasing of the 5 survey questions. The Control survey used “Yes”-primed questions, which are designed to elicit affirmative responses from respondents. The Treatment survey, on the other hand, used “No”-primed questions, encouraging respondents to think critically about the topics and more likely to answer negatively.
This experiment aimed to explore the psychological principle of reactance theory, which suggests that when people feel their freedom of choice is being restricted or guided (such as through overly positive framing), they may react by resisting or disengaging. Conversely, by using “No”-primed questions, we hypothesized that respondents would feel a greater sense of autonomy and control over their answers. The idea is that when people are encouraged to think critically and reject easy affirmations, they may feel more invested in their responses, fostering a stronger connection with the survey.
Additionally, answering “No” to these questions may create a cognitive dissonance effect, where respondents feel the need to resolve the negative stance with a positive action, such as donating after completing the survey. This subconscious desire for resolution may lead to higher engagement during the survey and increased donor conversions later in the funnel, especially when presented with an opportunity to act on their beliefs.
We split traffic to the survey pages evenly, and measured the # of survey submissions (emails acquired), as well as downstream donations on the post-survey donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test can be broken down into 3 key learnings:

 

#1 - Increase in Engagement with “No”-Primed Questions

The Treatment survey, which used "No"-primed questions, resulted in a 7.8% increase in form submissions compared to the Control survey. While this increase did not meet the 95% statistical level of confidence (79.1% LOC), it still suggests a promising trend toward higher engagement. This aligns with our hypothesis, which was grounded in reactance theory. By encouraging respondents to critically evaluate the questions and answer negatively, we likely fostered a greater sense of autonomy. Respondents who felt they were not being led to affirmations may have felt more invested in their responses, leading to increased engagement.

 

#2 - Potential Impact of “No” Priming on Conversion Behavior

Despite the increase in form submissions, the Conversion Rate for the Treatment group (2.41%) was slightly lower than the Control group (3.36%). This discrepancy suggests that while “No”-primed questions led to more people completing the survey, there might be a short-term resistance to following through with donations immediately after completing the survey. However, the cognitive dissonance effect mentioned in our hypothesis may still be at play—respondents who answered "No" to the questions may feel a subconscious need to resolve the negativity, and this could influence them to donate in the future as they reconcile their answers with the desire to support Luther Seminary.

 

#3 - Higher Average Gift in the Treatment Group

The Treatment group had a higher average gift of $32, compared to the Control group’s $23. This finding suggests that while the number of donors was slightly lower in the Treatment group, those who did choose to give were more likely to contribute larger amounts. This is potentially indicative of commitment consistency. Respondents who were engaged through the "No"-primed survey, and who felt more autonomous in their responses, may have been more motivated to make a larger, more deliberate contribution to Luther Seminary's cause. By thinking critically about their answers, they may have been more intentional in their giving.

 

The results of this experiment point toward the potential effectiveness of “No”-primed surveys for increasing engagement and stimulating larger gifts, even if immediate conversions didn’t significantly increase. Respondents who answered negatively may have felt more empowered and invested in their decisions, which could lead to higher-value gifts in the long term. Future tests could explore the extended impact of these psychological principles, with a focus on donor behavior over time and refining the optimal balance between engagement and conversion.

experiments.title: Does a targeted lookalike audience convert at a higher rate than a broad lookalike audience on Meta?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using a lookalike audience based on past donors from economic content offers would improve cost efficiency in donor acquisition because the audience would be more aligned with historical donor behavior.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2025 - 02/12/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The goal was to evaluate the impact of audience targeting strategies on Meta by testing a broad lookalike audience against a more targeted lookalike audience based on previous donors from economic content offers. The objective was to determine which approach would yield better cost efficiency for donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How using a question for a landing page headline impacts email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a question headline for potential course takers will achieve a change in email signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2024 - 12/04/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For one of their most popular free courses, we wanted to test whether using a question as a headline made an impact on email sign ups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a question as a headline for a course did not lead to an increase in email signups. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 16.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to detect any meaningful effects and to have a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be helpful to consider other factors such as the wording of the question, the target audience, and the overall messaging strategy when testing different approaches to increasing email signups. Additionally, it is important to continue conducting experiments and gathering data to inform decision making and to be open to testing different hypotheses in order to optimize the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: Does clarifying an offer through a headline on a landing page impact email signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to a headline for prospective content offer downloaders will achieve a change in email conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2024 - 11/07/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
For this experiment we wanted to test whether adding some clarity to a headline about what the content offer would help the prospect learn impacts email conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to a headline for prospective content offer downloaders resulted in a 0.5% increase in email acquisition for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 11.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the treatment to enhance its effectiveness before conducting another experiment. It is essential to rely on statistically significant results when making decisions based on A/B testing to avoid drawing inaccurate conclusions.

experiments.title: How adding credibility to a petition landing page impact email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a landing page like its a letter from an important figure for potential Pro-Life Petition signers will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it adds credibility and trust to the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/25/2024
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on ACI’s Pro-Life Petition landing page, we decided to run an A/B Test to lean into additional credibility through the presences of Dr. Ben Carson.
The treatment landing page copy was written as a letter from Dr. Ben Carson along with his signature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved presenting the landing page as a letter from Dr. Ben Carson, did not result in an increase in email acquisition for the Pro-Life Petition. In fact, there was a slight decrease in emails acquired compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment do not have a statistically significant level of confidence, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further refine the treatment and perhaps test different variables to determine what truly resonates with potential petition signers. It is also important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide more reliable results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, considering the sensitivity of the topic, it may be valuable to approach the messaging and presentation of the petition in a thoughtful and strategic manner to effectively engage the target audience.

experiments.title: How an instructional headline effects email acquisitions
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that the instructional headline will lead to increased email acquisitions because it clearly communicates the benefits prospects will gain from signing up for the series, rather than simply stating the name of the series.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 10/22/2024
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, focusing on a content offer landing page designed to encourage prospects to sign up for an email series aimed at helping them become more informed as the 2024 elections approach in North Carolina. We aimed to test different headlines to determine whether a more instructional would lead to higher email acquisition rates. The control group featured only the title of the email series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment remain inconclusive due to a low statistical significance level of 25.8%. While the treatment group, which featured an instructional headline, experienced a slight decrease in email acquisitions compared to the control group, this difference cannot be considered statistically significant.

We also observed a marginally higher average gift, but this finding is similarly inconclusive. This experiment serves as a useful starting point; however, our focus should shift to optimizing the body copy of the landing page to assess its potential impact on results. Currently, it appears that neither headline significantly influenced whether prospects opted to sign up for the series.

Given these findings, it is premature to draw any definitive conclusions or use these results for future decision-making. For subsequent experiments, it is crucial to ensure an adequate sample size to yield reliable and meaningful results. Additionally, testing different variations of the action-oriented headline may provide insights that could lead to a more significant impact on email acquisitions.

experiments.title: How a slide out on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide out with supportive copy for authors will achieve an increase in co-author invitations sent because it addresses the felt need and highlights it as a call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2024 - 10/24/2024
For CaringBridge authors, a key call to action is inviting a co-author to help manage your page. In order to increase this, we hypothesized that a slideout  with supportive copy would increase co-author invitations (email sign ups).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved a flyout with supportive copy for authors, resulted in an 8.9% increase in Co-Author Invites Sent for All Traffic. This indicates that the treatment had a positive impact on the desired outcome. However, the statistical level of confidence for this increase was 62.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.

It may be beneficial to further iterate on the flyout design and messaging to optimize the impact on Co-Author Invites Sent. Continue to monitor the engagement metrics such as Flyout CTA clicked to understand how users are interacting with the flyout.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is essential to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes to validate the effectiveness of the flyout in increasing co-author invitations.

experiments.title: How an in-line prompt impacts new journal entries (non-clickable)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line Create a Post card for authors will achieve an increase in new journal entires because it prioritizes the CTA above others and makes it more visible..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2024 - 10/04/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
This was the first iteration in a testing series which presented an in-line card with a small clickable button – in the next test, the entire card was made clickable. Primarily, the impact of this change upon the first test was a significant decrease in clicks overall to the treatment, indicating that users did not know where to click or interact with the prompt.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While both experiments within this series resulted in a similar increase in journal entries (3%), the non-clickable card impacted this treatment negatively in clicks where users did not know what step to take next.

It is worth noting that making this clickable change didn't impact our end metric ultimately (journal entries) but it did improve the user experience.

experiments.title: How an in-line, clickable “Create a Post” button impacts new journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable, in-line “Create a Post” prompt for authors will achieve an increase in journal entries because it prioritizes it as a primary call-to-action by making it more visible for the author..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2024 - 09/24/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The in-line "Create a Post" prompt led to a 3.3% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. This indicates that making the journal entry prompt more visible and clickable can encourage authors to post more frequently.

2. However, the statistical confidence level of 79.6% is below the standard threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and not completely reliable for future decision making.

We believe that these soft results indicate that the true point of friction hasn't been addressed by this treatment. A similar level of clicks between the cards and a similar conversion rate of journal entires may indicate that "where" to post or "how" is not what's preventing authors from doing so. Rather, it's likely a question of "what" to post. How can we make it easier for authors to share what's on their mind, even if initially they may believe they don't have anything to say.

experiments.title: How the placement of the call to action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers will achieve more email acquisitions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS runs courses for email acquisition. For these courses, they use a course platform that has a template for what the landing page looks like. The template has the headline underneath the CTA. The user also has to scroll to see it. We wanted to test putting the headline above the CTA to see how it impacted email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers resulted in a 11.2% decrease in Email Acquisitions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any fluctuations in traffic or other external factors that could impact the results. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to consider other factors that could potentially influence email acquisitions, such as the design of the CTA button itself or the messaging around it.

experiments.title: How offering a digital version of Is Christianity the Right Religion impacts email acquisition rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a digital download for a Facebook audience will achieve a better email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 05/11/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Most of their new name acquisition efforts (not donor acquisition) focused around offering up brochures and pamphlets that were then mailed to the recipient. Because this fulfillment process usually took a month, we hypothesized that offering an instant digital download of the same exact premium, in PDF format, would drive a higher email acquisition rate at a much lower cost to acquire.
This is an extension of an experiment we tested with a single premium in April; for this experiment, we were testing the Is Christianity the Right Religion premium resources.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Pathway to Victory was able to 4.8% increase in email conversion rates; however the level of confidence is only 67%. Moreover, the cost to acquire an email address was 6% higher for the digital premium.

We believe this result is due to a combination of factors:
By offering up immediate gratification through a digital download, versus potentially waiting up to a month to receive your resource in the mail, the audience was much more likely to sign up to receive it.
 	By not requiring a full mailing address for the digital version, we removed some friction in the sign-up process.

experiments.title: How adding a video on a landing page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a video to a landing page for Hoover’s audience will achieve in increase in donations because   they will be more likely to engage and feel connected to the offer they are signing up for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/12/2024 - 08/21/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.  For one of their most popular offers, Victory and the Savior Generals, we wanted to test whether adding a video that we were already running as a YouTube ad to the landing page would make an impact on donations. We hypothesized that by adding the video to the page, people would become more engaged and connected with the offer and would be more likely to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a video to a landing page for Hoover's audience resulted in a 5.2% increase in Revenue for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for valid results.
On the other hand, Email Acquisition experienced a significant 18.2% decrease with a high level of confidence at 98%.
These results suggest that while the video may have positively impacted the revenue, the decrease in email acquisitions raises concerns about the overall effectiveness of the video on the landing page.
In future experimentation, it is important to take into consideration both the positive and negative impacts of the changes made to the landing page. It is also crucial to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is higher before drawing any definitive conclusions. Additionally, further testing and analysis are needed to understand the full impact of adding a video to the landing page and to make informed decisions about its effectiveness in driving conversions.

experiments.title: How bypassing the registration page impacted email signups and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction in the course registration process for landing page viewers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because there will be less friction in the process and fewer clicks to achieve the desired action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2023 - 12/18/2023
After moving to a new course platform, we wanted to test reducing friction in the process by not having visitors have to click an additional button to begin the registration process. However, that page that preceded the checkout option is what communicated the value proposition of the course to the reader so we wanted to test before changing the process entirely. We set up an A/B test and split traffic to the registration page URL and the other 50% of traffic went to a treatment page which bypassed the registration page and went straight to the course checkout page. We ran the experiment for 2 months and observed how visitors behaved on the email acquisition page as well as downstream impacts to guide decisions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the registration page and reducing friction in the course registration process resulted in a decrease in email acquisition. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

To determine the impact of reducing friction on the registration process and email acquisition, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and longer durations. This will help obtain more statistically significant results and provide more reliable insights.

Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that might have influenced the decrease in email acquisition. For example, the treatment page may have lacked important information or a clear value proposition, leading to lower engagement and conversions.

Future experiments can also focus on improving the treatment page by incorporating elements of the registration page that effectively communicate the value proposition of the course. This way, the experiment can gauge whether reducing friction while maintaining the value proposition can positively impact email acquisition. It is important to analyze and understand user behavior and preferences to optimize the registration process and maximize email acquisition.

experiments.title: How enhancing the personal value for the user of a free online course impacted email registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the value to the user for potential course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition because the course is then positioned more uniquely and personally as a solution to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/12/2023 - 10/18/2023
When reviewing a course with low sign up rate, we wanted to propose a landing page treatment that would enhance the value of the course to the individual visitor. We put together copy that focuses more on the historical debates and questions about the return of Christ—addressing the questions in the minds of the visitors that would be answered or solved by signing up for the free online course. We attempt to tap into the curiosity of the visitor, positioning the course as a solution to those uncertainties. We also added clarity in the call to action to make it clear what next step the visitor should take before introducing the button to register. The treatment specifically mentions that by signing up, users will receive their first lecture instantly. This adds a sense of immediacy and instant gratification which might entice visitors to take action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the value of the course to potential users can result in an increase in email acquisition. This highlights the importance of positioning the course as a unique and personal solution to the uncertainties and questions that potential users may have.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on highlighting the value and benefits of the courses we offer. This could involve addressing specific pain points or uncertainties that potential users may have and demonstrating how our courses can provide the solutions or answers they are seeking.

Additionally, it is important to remember the significance of clarity in the call to action. Clearly stating the next steps for the visitor and highlighting any immediate benefits, such as receiving the first lecture instantly, can help to create a sense of urgency and incentivize users to take action.

To validate the findings of this experiment and make informed decisions for future experimentation, it is necessary to run additional experiments with a larger sample size and higher statistical level of confidence. This will provide more reliable and valid results that can be used to guide future decision-making.

experiments.title: How condensing a form and using more positive copy for a content offer impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the landing page form and using more positive copy for prayer calendar landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because the form will be perceived as less questions to fill out and the positive tone will resonate more with the visitor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to boost our email acquisition rate on IMB’s main Prayer Calendar funnel, we decided to run a 3-way test. Treatment 1 condensed the landing page form so that it would be perceived as shorter for the user, and treatment 2 also condensed the form as well as used a more positive tone in the copy by changing the first line of the body copy.
Our hypothesis was that both of these changes would increase the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that both condensing the landing page form and using a more positive tone in the copy had little to no impact on the email acquisition rate for the Prayer Calendar landing page.

While these were not the results we were anticipating, it is interesting that the more positive copy did not have more of an impact on the landing page visitor.

Although we did not get any valid metrics from this test, it does tell us that the long form on the landing page does not negatively impact the visitor's motivation to complete the landing page.

We plan to continually run experiments on this funnel and test the motivations of IMB's audience.

experiments.title: How using a shorter more concise landing page for email prospecting traffic impacts the email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using shorter and more concise copy for Pray for a Missionary landing page visitors acquired through email prospecting will achieve an increase in the email acquisition rate because it would reduce friction on the page by getting to the purpose much quicker.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 08/07/2024
In an effort to build a funnel that resonated best with the traffic IMB was receiving from email prospecting efforts, we decided to test using a shorter and more concise landing page solely for individuals who received our Pray for a Missionary prospecting emails.
We used VWO audience segmentation to be sure that this test did not impact normal traffic to the landing page.
Our hypothesis was that using more concise copy would help visitors understand the purpose of the page quicker and therefore reduce friction on the page. We tend to think traffic coming in through prospecting channels want quick and easy information to consume so we wanted to put that concept to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using shorter and more concise copy on the Pray for a Missionary landing page for visitors acquired through email prospecting resulted in a 2.5% increase in emails acquired for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity, meaning that these results were ultimately flat.

Therefore we can not make any decisions based on this test. It would be interesting to test whether additional copy and value proposition for email prospecting traffic has a positive impact on email acquisition since our hypothesis was not proven with this test.

experiments.title: How an outcomes-focused headline affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an outcome-based headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2021
The Navigators were running a landing page for an ebook and wanted to test if outcome-focused headlines have an impact in conversion email acquisition? For this test we decided to create two different headlines and keeping the copy intact. The control group receives a headline that incites a demand, while the experimental group receives a headline that incites an outcome-focused action.
Control group headline: Get the help you need to memorize Scripture!
Treatment group headline: Commit dozens of the Bible’s most helpful Scriptures to memory
We created this experiment in Unbounce.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not see a significant change between these two groups. however, it is worth testing how an outcome-based headline could potentially increase email acquisition conversion rate when focussing on positive gains (the benefit of getting the ebook, knowledge) rather than a negative one (like putting in some work, or perhaps is time consuming).

Moving forward we could try the above hypothesis.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How using short, “concern”, communal language in a pop-up impacts email conversion

rows.name: YOU ARE RIGHT TO BE CONCERNED

experiments.researchQuestion: How will short, “concerned”, communal language impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 03/25/2021
In a continued effort to better understand the motivations of site users and people converting on the forced pop-up on the STC website, we tested communal language again – but this time is was much shorter, used “concern” language, and had a similar tone of the call-to-action. This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for nine days, there was no difference in conversion and no sign of the percentage of validity increasing either. The good news is that we didn't see any negative impact on email conversion. That said, the motivation of people seeing and converting on this pop-up is extremely high, and even when trying to reinforce how a person feels - that they are "concerned" - we aren't able to influence their decision to convert or get more people to convert.

experiments.title: How more relevant copy on a content offer landing page impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using relevant and specific copy for Month specific Prayer Calendar landing page visiotors will achieve our email acqusition rate because there would be more relevancy and an inside look at what is inside the calendar for that month.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/08/2024 - 08/05/2024
IMB has a Prayer Calendar offer that offers monthly prayer prompts. While we have used the entire calendar to market the product, we decided to focus on month-to-month as well to see if the relevancy would increase our email acquisition rate.
On the month-to-month landing page, we tested using a copy change that included the contents of the July prayer prompt versus the general copy used on the main funnel.
We ran this A/B test for about a month with the traffic split evenly between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while using relevant and specific copy for the Month-specific Prayer Calendar landing page seemed like a promising idea, the results did not support the hypothesis. The treatment, which included the contents of the July prayer prompt, actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 17.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and potentially refine the specific copy used for the Month-specific Prayer Calendar landing page. It may be worth exploring different approaches to see if there are ways to leverage the content of the calendar for increased email acquisition without negatively impacting the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as the design of the landing page, call-to-action placement, and overall user experience could also help in optimizing the effectiveness of the campaign.

experiments.title: How using a lighter background on a video series landing page impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a light background for the video series landing page will achieve an increase in clicks to the checkout page because the contrast from the white background will improve readability for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2024 - 04/25/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They created a landing page for a new paid video series and wanted to know if the current, black background of the page was harming the user experience.
We decided to test a light-background version of the page against the current dark background. Our hypothesis was that the white background would lead to more clicks to the checkout page because white backgrounds in web design are shown to enhance readability and reduce visual strain.
We ran this as a split URL test in VWO, with a random 50% of the traffic going to the dark background page. The other 50% were instead shown a light background version of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment was that changing the background color of the video series landing page from black to white did not result in the desired increase in clicks to the checkout page. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 2.4% decrease in visits to the Focus Store Checkout Page compared to the control group.

Additionally, the statistical level of confidence was only 15.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of this experiment were essentially flat and shouldn't be considered a valid test result.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that changing the background color alone may not be enough to drive increased engagement with the checkout page. Further research and experimentation may be necessary to identify the most effective strategies for improving user experience and encouraging clicks to the checkout page.

experiments.title: How a simplified and user-focused value proposition affects sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop about the user’s journey and desired impact for the DDJ landing page  will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because this will better communicate the value and focus on answering why they should be interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2023 - 08/16/2023
After a previous test on the DDJ landing page that tested into layout, The Navigators wanted to turn their attention to testing the elements themselves. In this copy test, we hypothesized framing the language around the user and simplifying the copy to their desired impact rather than the Navigators would increase sign-up rate. Rather than answering why the organization created Digital Discipleship, the treatment is focused on asking questions directly to the user and positioning the resource as the answer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifyign the value prop   did not achieve the desired increase in sign-up rate for the DDJ landing page. The treatment actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control, however this was not at a valid level of confidence. More testing is required.
Some questions to consider are the ways in which this framing shifted the responsibility onto the reader. Although the attempt was to not focus on why the organization created the resource so much as the user's journey and the impact they want to make — it's possible this put too much pressure on the reader. What if they aren't ready to disciple or commit to "come alongside someone?" The original used these terms, but only in the sense of whether the readers themselves has ever had someone do this for them, not whether they want to do this for someone else.
Future experimentation should continue to test different approaches to framing the language and offer on the DDJ landing page to determine the most effective strategy for increasing sign-up rates. It is important to gather more data and achieve a higher statistical level of confidence in order to make valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How using clarity and an offer in a email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate

rows.name: Do you want to grow in your discipleship?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and incentives in the subscription for site visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because prospects will know what they’re getting and are further incentivized by receiving something of value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2023 - 05/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section to add clarity in the first variant. We also wanted to test using an ebook as additional incentive to sign up in the second variant. The use of these two different variants would give us some indicators as to what motivates people to subscribe to the Navigators’ communications.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment showed us that people were more drawn to take action when given clear proposition language over an ebook incentive.

Treatment 1 resulted in a 27% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 64% while treatment 2 resulted in a 5% decrease with a level of confidence of 15%. In order to get valid results, Navigators will ran this test again with a 50/50 split with the first treatment.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition of a sticky bar impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the value proposition for the course sticky bar will achieve an increase in email conversions because it increases the clarity of what someone will receive when they sign up for the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2024 - 02/13/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary regularly runs sticky bars on their voice.dts.edu website to advertise new courses, surveys, and more. They’ve previously tested into language that acknowledges them as a friend of DTS and use this language regularly.
For their new course, we wanted to test different language angles to see if we could get more people clicking into the course landing page.
For the first treatment, we wanted to test language about starting off the new year with a new course as it launched in January.
For the second treatment, we focused on the felt need that the course addresses.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results showed that the treatment version resulted in a 3.1% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.7%, falling below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, while the treatment won, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

The key learning from this experiment is that changing the value proposition in the sticky bar can impact email conversions. However, to draw more concrete conclusions, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be necessary. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence to make reliable decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.

They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How invitational language on a landing page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by having language that is more invitational for potential acquisition individuals will achieve a higher conversion rate because the copy is more invitational and things to do with family and friends .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2023 - 03/30/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio news and radio station.
WUNC is currently running an acquisition offer around Things To Do in North Carolina. In an effort to drive conversion rates, we decided to test a more invitational language approach.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted by WUNC with the hypothesis that an invitational language approach would yield higher conversion rates for email acquisition did not produce a conclusive result as the treatment showed a 4.9% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.6%, below the recommended confidence level of 85%, hence, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Despite this, the experiment identified a significant increase in email acquisition from Desktop visitors, with a 193% increase and a 95% level of confidence. This result suggests that future experimentation should focus on optimizing user experience for Desktop visitors specifically, perhaps by creating a more tailored approach to their specific user requirements.

Overall, the key learning from this experiment is the importance of having a sufficiently high level of statistical confidence to make valid decisions based on the experiment’s results. Moreover, it is crucial to carefully consider the target audience to optimize any experiment, as seen with Desktop users in this experiment. Thus, future experiments with invitational language should be run with a larger audience and a better targeted approach to maximize the benefit of such an approach.

experiments.title: How using different images for a content offer acquisition funnel affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an image of the ebook on the landing page for potential downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate because users will perceive the ebook image as higher value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2023 - 01/16/2024
The Heritage Foundation is offering the “Guide to the Constitution” digital version as a free gift through paid Facebook ads, email prospecting and website traffic.
Heritage originally tested the concept of the laptop image vs the ebook image in a Dear Reader on daily signal. The results showed that click-through rate on the dear reader was 68.6% higher when using the ebook image vs no image at all. The laptop image produced a 30.1% decrease in clickthrough rate compared to the control.
The offer has always had the laptop image on the landing page, so they decided to test changing the image to the ebook to see if it would increase email acquisition rate on the landing page.
They believed it would because the ebook looks to have more value than just a picture of the offer through a laptop or phone screen.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the image on the landing page from a laptop to an ebook did not result in a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment version actually saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic.

Although the results showed a decrease in Transactions, the statistical level of confidence was below 85%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to gather more data and increase the sample size in order to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be worth considering other elements on the landing page, such as the layout, color scheme, or messaging, to determine if any of those factors have an impact on the email acquisition rate.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar on acquisition donation pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a sticky bar for newly acquired names will achieve a higher conversion rate because the donation ask is enforced with year end motivations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/30/2023 - 12/28/2023
Belmont Abbey College is a Catholic university located in North Carolina.
During calendar year-end, we decided to test the presence of a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages encouraging potential donors to make a year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a persuasive message on acquisition offer donation pages can potentially increase the conversion rate. The treatment version, which included the sticky bar with a specific call-to-action and a motivational message, resulted in a 31.5% increase in transactions for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be important to gather a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer duration to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence in the results. A higher level of confidence is essential to ensure that any changes made based on the experiment results are valid and reliable.
Additionally, it would be worth considering testing variations of the sticky bar message, design, or placement to find the most effective combination. Exploring different strategies and optimizing the implementation of sticky bars can potentially lead to improved conversion rates and better engagement with potential donors.
Overall, this experiment provides a valuable insight into the potential impact of adding a sticky bar on acquisition offer donation pages. However, further experimentation is needed to validate and refine the results before implementing any changes on a larger scale.

experiments.title: How using an eBook cover image on a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an eBook cover image for a free content offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition because there is additional clarity enhancing the perceived value of the offer on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2023 - 12/21/2023
When sharing a digital magazine issue, we wondered if adding clarity to the landing page by showing a preview of the magazine cover would motivate more people to convert on the email acquisition page. We developed a treatment that included the eBook image alongside the copy and split traffic 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an eBook cover image to a landing page for a free content offer did not result in an increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment saw a decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and longer duration to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other factors that could potentially increase email acquisition, such as different call-to-action copy or alternative content offers. Continuous testing and experimentation will help to identify what strategies and elements are most effective in attracting and converting users.

experiments.title: How a recurring tab impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a monthly option for web donors will achieve higher conversion rate because there is additional give options and clarity in the type of gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/10/2024 - 01/26/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
PTV’s offers are primarily various books and premiums with donations. Our January emphasis was to increase the number of recurring donors. With this in mind, we decided to try adding a recurring tab to normal offers to see if we could passively acquire recurring donors by just having the option available.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Note: treatment generated 0 recurring gifts
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a monthly option for web donors did not achieve a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, meaning the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
Based on this, it is necessary to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or different variations to determine the effectiveness of adding a recurring tab for web donors. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the specific offer being presented.
Future experimentation should focus on gathering more data and conducting rigorous A/B testing to determine the impact of different variables on conversion rates. It may also be beneficial to analyze other aspects of the donor journey, such as the effectiveness of different messaging or marketing channels, to gain a deeper understanding of donor behavior and preferences. Additionally, seeking feedback from donors and analyzing their motivations and decision-making processes can provide valuable insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How the presentation of a digital resource on a landing page impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the resource in a digital form for landing page viewers will achieve an increased email sign up rate because they have clarity in the way they will be able to use the digital resource. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2023 - 09/21/2023
Dallas Theological Seminary has participated in North Texas Giving Day each year. From previous testing, they know that priming their housefile with a content offer has historically lead to increased donors and revenue. This year, they wanted to test if presenting the eBook in a different method on the landing page would impact email sign ups.
Historically with eBooks, they have created an image that represents the eBook as a 3D eBook preview—more like an actual book. They decided to test this against a mock-up of the eBook on an iPad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting the resource in a digital form on the landing page resulted in a slight increase in email signups compared to the traditional 3D eBook preview. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 40.1%, which is below the acceptable threshold of 85%.
Based on these results, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions regarding the effectiveness of presenting the eBook in a digital form. The results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.
In order to obtain more reliable and valid results, it is recommended to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence. By doing so, the nonprofit can gather more accurate and conclusive data to determine the most effective method of presenting the eBook on the landing page.
DTS will be testing this again during Calendar Year End on their Christmas Devotional, which has historically gotten a significantly higher rate of traffic and should be able to give us a better sample size.

experiments.title: How does referencing a recurring donor’s status in email copy impact additional giving?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing a donors’ status for recurring donors will achieve a change in giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2023 - 07/01/2023
Pathway to Victory is the international ministry for First Baptist Dallas. They have 2 major match campaigns throughout the year-one during December, the other in June.
For this June match campaign, we wanted to see whether we could get additional one-time gifts from Pathway Partners–who are recurring donors–by referencing their status in email copy and writing to them accordingly.
This test ran over 4 separate email sends.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Referencing a donor's status as a recurring donor in email copy has the potential to increase one-time donations.
2. The treatment group, which received emails referencing their status, saw a 20.0% increase in donations compared to the control group.
3. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 23.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for valid results.
Given the very small donation sample size, this experiment requires further refinement and re-testing during future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a ebook acquisition page affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for online course enrollees will achieve a higher email sign up rate because the social proof shows the user that there are others who have downloaded the ebook and it offers them a chance to join a group of others with similar interests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client is offering a free online course on the book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, so we decided to test adding the concept of social proof. We added social proof by adding a floating white box that says “We’ve already had over 3,000 people sign up for this online course! Will you join the group of people who are expanding their knowledge of the Book of Psalms?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding social proof for the online course resulted in a 10.1% increase in email acquisitions with a 75.1% level of confidence. This indicates that social proof has a positive impact on encouraging users to sign up.

2. When analyzing the results based on gender, it was found that female visitors had a significant 681% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the addition of social proof had a strong and positive impact specifically on female visitors.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the impact of social proof on overall email sign-up rates.

experiments.title: How reinforcing security upon course sign up affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing security for course takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because there is reduced anxiety about how their information will be used .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 07/02/2023
When signing up for a course there may be added anxiety as to why we may be asking for their contact information. Adding security language below the submit button can let them know that their information is safe with DTS. Our hypothesis is that the addition of this language will encourage more people to fill out the form at the end and lead more people to the instant donation page (also resulting in an increase in donor conversion rate).
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 22 days, the treatment (security language implemented) produced a 1.6% increase in email acquisition rate with a 40% level of confidence. Given the flat lift and modest level of confidence, we cannot confidently roll out the treatment. It seems the added security language had little impact on a user's motivation and comfort to provide their personal information and sign up for the course. We may want to try a similar test on the instant donation page to see if it provides a more significant impact. This could be a more effective place to run the test given the additional information required from someone in order to complete a donation.

experiments.title: How a value-driven headline on an acquisition offer landing page impacts email conversion, donor conversion, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating reader-focused value proposition language for visitors to the Cherish video series landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life. because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/10/2023
Focus on the Family is a Christian ministry that serves families worldwide. Every year, hundreds of thousands of people sign up for their free video series’ to learn more about strengthening marriages, families, and faith.
One of their most effective series is Cherish Your Spouse. In reviewing the landing page for this offer, we felt that the headline lacked a strong value for the visitor. We hypothesized that if we changed the headline to clarify the impact of the series on subscribers, it would increase the appeal of the offer and signups on the page, ultimately leading to more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for three weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant impact on email signups from the new headline. Downstream donor conversion actually trended down for the treatment page but we were not able to validate that decrease either. Future tests will need to change more than the headline to have a higher likelihood of resulting in a statistically valid result. Our next test will be to change the body copy to emphasize what subscribers will experience as a result of watching these videos.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Sign-Up Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the Course on Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in email acquisitions because it removes anxiety around giving up an email. because  it removes anxiety around giving up an email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/25/2023
The IMB’s Course on Prayer offer was experiencing a low sign-up rate on the landing page. We hypothesized that eliminating anxiety with a simple security callout at the bottom of the form would reassure users by clarifying what their information will be used for. We believe a reminder to the user of why their email is being requested (to receive their course lessons) will increase the number of emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment should not be considered valid due to the statistical level of confidence being below 85%. Ultimately, a very small lift with a low level of confidence shows that this treatment did not cause much of a difference for users arriving at this landing page. More testing is required, but this also prompts us to explore what other factors could be causing anxiety/friction to email acquisition.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to have a larger sample size and a higher level of confidence before making any decisions based on the results. It may also be beneficial to test alternative forms of security callouts or messaging to determine which resonates best with visitors.

experiments.title: How using language around gaining deeper knowledge and enhancing their faith affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language centered around gaining a deeper knowledge of the book of Psalms and enhancing their faith for potential online course signees will achieve a higher email conversion rate because it taps into the inherent desire for personal growth, intellectual curiosity, and a sense of accomplishment.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2023 - 06/15/2023
Luther Seminary is offering a free online course of the Book of Psalms. They wanted to try and increase the email conversion rate on the sign-up page for the course, so we decided to test using language around gaining a deeper knowledge of this book of the Bible while enhancing their faith. We did this in two ways:
Emphasis on Knowledge and Understanding: The treatment page focuses on the idea of gaining deeper knowledge and understanding of the Book of Psalms. By highlighting the informative and insightful nature of the course, it appeals to individuals who are seeking to expand their knowledge base and develop a more profound understanding of the Psalms. This emphasis on learning and personal growth can be compelling for those who value educational opportunities.
Cultivating Personal Qualities: The first page mentions that studying the Psalms can help cultivate qualities such as a heart of worship, hunger for God, authentic prayer life, and a thankful heart. This emphasis on personal transformation and spiritual development appeals to individuals who are looking for practical applications and benefits from studying the Psalms. It positions the course as a means to enhance one’s relationship with God and deepen their faith.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted to test the effectiveness of using language centered around gaining a deeper knowledge of the book of Psalms and enhancing faith resulted in a 6.5% decrease in email acquisition for all traffic with a 73.3% level of confidence. However, this result cannot be considered valid for future decision making as the level of confidence is below 85%.

The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing knowledge and understanding or cultivating personal qualities may not be effective for all visitors. Therefore, it is important to understand the audience and tailor language accordingly. Future experiments should continue to test various messaging approaches, target specific audiences, and gather feedback to determine what messaging is most effective in increasing email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate

rows.name: Do you want to grow in your discipleship?
