experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity  for site visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because there will be reduced anxiety when the provide their contact information .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 06/02/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment copy added to the footer section did not increase the email acquisition rate for the Navigators website. There was a 21% decrease with a level of confidence of 73%.
However, when looking at new vs returning users we saw the language resonate with returning users and new users saw a valid 41% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 95%. Our hypothesis is that the header language might be too high of a commitment for new users. We want to retest this concept and change the copy so that it's a lower barrier to entry for new users on their site.

experiments.title: How adding appeal and exclusivity to course sign up value proposition copy impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity for landing page visitors will increase sign up rate because visitors will gain an improved understanding of what they’ll get out of the course and how the course will improve their relationship with Christ.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/29/2023
In an effort to increase sign up rate to DTS’ Intro to Prophets online course, we built a sign up page treatment focusing on enhancing the appeal and exclusivity with the value proposition copy. We feel these elements are lacking in the existing copy.
For exclusivity, we added language including “Don’t miss this rare opportunity”, “This one-of-a-kind course”, and “become part of an exclusive community of like-minded learners.”
For appeal, we transitioned the copy to focus more on the potential course participant and the course itself instead of the organization and the organization’s mission, and added more action sentences to hopefully invoke the action of signing up.
Let’s see how the adjusted value prop performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing elements of appeal and exclusivity can potentially increase the sign-up rate for a course. However, the statistical level of confidence of 32.6% is well below the threshold of 85% and the results cannot be considered valid.
The treatment produced a minimal 0.98% lift in course sign ups.
Downstream metrics were minimal as well, with the treatment producing a 5% increase in donations with just a 22.5% level of confidence, and revenue decreasing by 2.7% with a 55.7% level of confidence.
The added appeal and exclusivity did not enhance user motivation and increase course sign up like we hypothesized it would. We will need to revisit the value proposition copy to brainstorm alternative motives to increase sign ups. Or consider testing opportunities elsewhere on the sign up page.
Further experiments need to be conducted with a larger sample size and more significant changes to the treatment. Future experiments should focus on creating a strong value proposition that directly addresses the needs of the potential course participants and highlights the unique features of the course. Additionally, the experiment should explore other factors that could potentially impact the sign-up rate, such as the design of the landing page and the placement of the call-to-action button. Overall, the experiment provides a starting point for further exploration of how to increase sign-up rates for online courses, but a larger sample size and more significant changes are needed to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a newsletter subscription opportunity impacted email opt-ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to a newsletter subscription for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will gain clarity in what they’ll get from signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2023 - 05/21/2023
In an effort to get more people to sign up for IPI’s newsletter, we built a treatment that added value proposition copy above the newsletter sign up form. We highlighted what type of news coverage subscribers could expect, added more call-to-action language, and expresses the importance of being an engaged citizen.
The control version lacks each of these elements, so we’re interested to see if the treatment will result in an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for over a month, the experiment resulted in only one email opt-in per variant. Thus, the experiment had no winner and the lack of email conversions prevented any valid result. Value proposition does not see to be the issue. We should test different elements of the page to isolate the problem. Perhaps there was too much friction for visitors given the various check boxes they're asked to consider. A new test could be around simplifying the page to reduce decision friction.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and exclusivity to a newsletter signup footer impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including value prop about why to sign up for communications from IMB for for users on the homepage will achieve an increased email conversion rate because people need to see value in signing up if they are going to provide their personal email because  people need to see value in signing up if they are going to provide their personal email..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2023 - 05/06/2023
At the bottom of IMB’s homepage is a call to sign up for communications and a form for users to submit their email.  We hypothesized that adding more value prop copy to the Sign-Up—verbiage about why should someone sign up, what will you receive, etc—would increase email acquisition. The control copy was shorter, but more general, leveraging with a grab about a newsletter with interesting stories. Alternatively, the treatment tested more copy, but conveyed more details about what you’d receive and why to sign up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The statistical level of confidence is not high enough to consider these results valid. Future experimentation should be conducted with a larger sample size given that the differences observed were under 1%. The experiment resulted in a 6% decrease in email conversion rate with a level of confidence of 33%.
One idea to execute a test of this hypothesis with a validating result is a more prominent treatment. This Sign Up form is located at the very bottom of IMB's homepage which limits the pool size only to those who scroll to the bottom. This same copy could be tested with a slideout or popup that appears on the first portion of the page.

experiments.title: How addressing the felt need of the ebook recipient impacts account creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language that addressed the felt need for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in site creation rate because the “why” of a site creation would be established before being asked to create one, thus reducing friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 04/09/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added language that addressed the felt need of the ebook recipient.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment invited the individual to “start a CaringBridge site” and was an explicit as to why someone would need a CaringBridge site. We believed that changing the language to address the felt need would decrease friction the person would experience in being asked to create a site right away.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version of the call to action saw a 7.6% increase in website creations for all traffic with a 21.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making. The results indicate that changing the language to address the felt need had little to no impact on the site creation rate. It is likely that the change in copy was not significant enough to sway the recipient to take action.
For future experiments, it is recommended to continue testing language that addresses the felt need of the audience for increased website creations. Perhaps a complete rewrite may be more impactful.

experiments.title: How addressing a felt need in landing page copy impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the felt need for prospective ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate  because relevancy to the individual’s needs strengthens the value proposition for the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/13/2023
IMB was experiencing a relatively low sign up rate on an offer landing page. To address this, we developed a treatment for this landing page that would better address a person’s felt need. The control for the experiment starts the page with a declarative statement to take action. The treatment invites the person to think about why they would want to sign up for this online course and provides ways that this offer will fulfill those needs. Our hypothesis is that the language that unpacks the “why” would result in more emails acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of prospective customers can lead to a higher email acquisition rate. The treatment that focused on the "why" behind signing up for the online course instead of just encouraging action saw a 4.4% increase in email acquisitions compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.3%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid for decision making.
Additionally, downstream we saw that donor conversion rate was negatively affected by the copy (though also not a valid result).
It's likely that this low impact is because the felt need is already addressed at the ad level (where most traffic to this page is coming from). Therefore, people weren't more motivated by this language on this landing page.

experiments.title: How changing the language for existing donors impacts email conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that referencing a donor’s status for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates because we are showing them they are more than just an email address.. because we are showing them they are more than just an email address..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 04/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
One of their best-performing offers is a book titled Perfect Ending; this is an offer they run on multiple channels at least once a year. For this specific email appeal, we wanted to test whether recognizing the impact a donor has had on the ministry by changing the language in an email would impact their conversion rates. Our hypothesis was that by referencing that they were a donor, they would be more likely to make a donation. This test was run for the active donor segment only.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Changing the language in the donation ask slightly, using terms such as 'continue your support' and ' reinforce that you stand behind bold....' resulted in a slight decrease in conversion rates of 8.5% with a 49% level of confidence.

There was a bigger change when looking at the impact on revenue; a 10.9% decrease in revenue, with a 68% level of confidence.

We believe that this was due to the fact that this experiment only changed a couple of words; the call to donate was buried at the bottom of the email, so potential donors to this campaign were already convinced to donate and get a copy of the book before they were even influenced by the donor-specific copy.

Future variants of this test will look at the placement of donor-centric language, as well as bigger copy tests.

experiments.title: How including student testimonials on a course landing page impacted signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page will increase course sign ups because it establishes more credibility for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/06/2023 - 04/01/2023
In an effort to increase the number of course sign ups on the our Genesis course sign up landing page, we wanted to know how including testimonials of previous students on the course sign up page would impact the number of individuals who enrolled in our Genesis course.
For this experiment, we ran an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment variants. The control version modeled the standard landing page format for all courses, while the treatment version included a text box of two different quotes regarding this course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment aimed to determine the impact of including student testimonials on the Genesis course signup landing page to increase course signups. Results showed that the treatment resulted in a 4.9% decrease in course signups with a statistical level of confidence of 63.7%. Although the control variant won, the results cannot be considered valid since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
Key learnings from this experiment include the importance of setting a higher statistical level of confidence for more valid results, considering the impact on specific audience segments, and the need for further experimentation on the use of testimonials on course signup landing pages. Future experimentation could focus on testing different variations of testimonials, targeting specific audience segments, and testing other elements on the landing page that could impact course signups.
We chose to end this experiment before the 30 day mark due to results consistently being in the negatives.
The key learning from this experiment is that including student testimonials on the course sign up landing page may not always be effective in increasing course sign ups, and further testing will be necessary.

experiments.title: How an alternate motive conveyed in the course landing page headline impacted course sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing the headline on personal application and self-improvement for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because visitors will be more interested in how the course can improve their life in faith rather than deepening their knowledge of a book of the bible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2023 - 03/26/2023
The Dallas Theological Seminary’s James course landing page is producing a 19.1% sign-up rate and $7.57 gross cost per name – both metrics are missing their benchmark. In an attempt to improve these results, we’ve developed a landing page test that tests what the user hopes to get out of the course.
Looking at the current headline, we wonder if it effectively speaks to the user’s desire. Do they want to deepen their knowledge of a book of the bible or do they want to take the overall message of the course and apply it to their life in faith? Are they looking for knowledge or self-improvement?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment version that emphasized action and how the course could improve the visitor's life in faith resulted in a 3.5% increase in course signups, albeit with a statistical level of confidence of only 61.9%. This means that the findings cannot be deemed significant or conclusive for future decision-making.
Moving further down the funnel, the treatment lifted donation conversion by 6.2% but with a 16.6% level of confidence. And revenue experienced a 16% decrease through the treatment with a 70% level of confidence. None of the metrics validated through this test.
The key learning from this experiment is that a headline that emphasizes personal application and self-improvement could increase course signup rates, but the data overall is not consistent or significant enough to determine an optimal approach through this headline test. The collective audience in the test did not lean favorably in either direction when it comes to desirability or motivation to sign up for the course. The next test we run on this page should have more significant changes to trigger more conversions and a more significant level of confidence.

experiments.title: How reducing friction by shortening survey length impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the length of a survey will increase email acquisition rates by reducing friction and capitalizing on the cognitive momentum created by the ad that sends traffic to the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2023 - 03/17/2023
When reviewing results of a survey offer funnel, we observed a solid click-through rate, a low cost per click, and a fair instant conversion rate indicating that this is an offer and an issue people care to engage with. However, we identified lower-than-average email acquisition rates as an opportunity to improve overall results. After reviewing this campaign, we believe that the length of the survey was introducing significant friction and causing visitors to abandon. In all, the survey contains 20 questions. In a previous experiment, we were able to produce a sizable lift in survey completions by paginating the survey, rather than presenting all 20 questions on one page. However, over time, email acquisition was still struggling for this offer and we believe that its length was causing the cognitive momentum in landing page visitors to fizzle out. To test this theory, we built a treatment that truncated the survey to ~5 questions all presented on one page to reduce friction/cost of completing survey in effort to improve email acquisition. We split traffic 50/50 between the two versions and ran the experiment for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month we saw a 0.86% increase in conversion rate with 12.4% level of confidence. However, we learned that not all friction is bad friction because we saw a significant decrease in Donations and Revenue, with a 100% and 98% level of confidence, respectively. This suggests that reducing the length of the survey may have a negative impact on the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. When reviewing downstream effects on donations, the treatment produced a 90% decrease in gifts with 99% level of confidence. We believe this happened because the more time that was invested in the survey, the more willing the visitor was to convert on the instant donation page. This means the control version with a lengthier survey was actually producing more qualified traffic to the donation page. Because the results were overall flat at the landing page level, we will need to do additional testing to improve email acquisition rate while still maintaining the quality of the visitor at the donation page level. In future testing, we could add additional elements to the process to let visitors know how many steps there are to complete the survey and display their progress along the way so they have more clarity around how long it will take them to finish the survey and submit their responses. It is important to note that we saw a significant increase in email acquisition rates for Housefile Email visitors, suggesting that the treatment may have a positive impact on visitors who are already familiar with our organization. Future experimentation should focus on finding a balance between reducing friction and maintaining the willingness of visitors to donate or contribute financially. We can also consider targeting Housefile Email visitors with a truncated survey to capitalize on their familiarity with our organization and potentially increase email acquisition rates.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to an online education program sign up page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a program sign up page for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will have a better understanding of the program and the value it provides.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 03/17/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute has a program called Little Patriots that offers lessons, activities, videos, cartoons, workbooks and more tailored toward children to teach them the story of America. Parents and teachers can sign up on behalf of their children to gain access to this content, and they would do so through the Little Patriots sign up page.
Currently, this page contains just a standard sign up form – it contains no value proposition copy and the headline simply says “REGISTRATION”. We wondered if the implementation of value proposition copy coupled with a descriptive call to action headline would lead to an increase in Little Patriots program sign ups. We’ve run countless tests that support this approach. Audiences typically respond well to value proposition and descriptive call to actions because the elements present value/benefit to the reader/prospect and gives them a clear path to the intended action. Let’s see how the approach works in this scenario…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 1.7% lift in email conversion rate with just a 22.6% level of confidence. The results do not dictate that the treatment should be rolled out or applied to the page moving forward. As the vast majority of page visitors come from the Little Patriots home page, there is a good chance they already have a strong understanding of the program and its benefits and do not need the help of value proposition copy on the registration page to convince them to register. In other words, the value is already clear in their minds and registering is just a formality.
The evidence is in the results. The treatment produced a 2.1% decrease in sign ups among returning visitors while new visitors produced a 1.9% lift. Still, the 1.9% lift only had a 22.5% level of confidence.
Paid social traffic produced a 9.6% decrease in email sign ups from the treatment, which could be explained by value being communicated to the user at both the Facebook ad level and the Little Patriots home page before they reach the sign up page.
Ultimately, value proposition may not be the most urgent focus for improving sing up rate. There are other approaches/elements we can try along the lines of social proof or credibility.

experiments.title: How including the word “active” in an email acquisition form headline affects sign-up rates

rows.name: "Join us to learn how you can help make change for children."
"Be an active supporter. Join below to lean more."

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language to “active” text above the homepage email capture module  for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate from users on the site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2022 - 09/06/2022
Changing the language above the homepage email capture module to see if adding active text increases email conversion from users on the site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• No significant positive differences in email conversion (by channel, new/return visitor, or location).
• Variant directionally underperformed (-11%) compared to the control (69% LoC).
• Recommendation is not to change the language above the homepage email module.
• Overall email conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at anon-statistically validLoC (69%).
• Mobile email conversion was lower for the variant (-20%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (91%).
• Desktop email conversion was higher for the variant (23%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (65%).
• U.S. email conversion was slightly higher for the variant (7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (38%).
• International email conversion was lower for the variant (-51%) at a statistically valid LoC (99.7%).
• This test aimed to determine whether adding “Active” language above the homepage email module would increase email conversion. There were directional declines in email conversion across mobile, international, and new/ returning visitors. The only conversion lifts were seen for desktop (23%) and U.S.(7%) visitors, both of which were directional.
• We recommend not changing copy above the homepage email module

experiments.title: How modeling ad targeting after just your most qualified contacts impacts qualified lead generation results
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that modeling after our most highly qualified contacts for our target audience will achieve an increase in qualified leads generated (and/or decrease our cost per new qualified lead generated) because it will be based upon a better source list.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 01/31/2023
As a part of our recent experimentation with our own acquisition efforts, we decided to try specifically modeling a lookalike audience in Facebook (Meta) Advertising based upon our most engaged/best qualified contacts in the database, rather than our control approach (which is a mixture of interest-based targeting, lookalike models from our overall email database, and directly uploaded prospecting lists).
Would this improve the overall volume of the new qualified leads (and decrease the cost per qualified lead)?
experiments.keyLearnings:
After a month of experimenting with this hypothesis, we were unable to fully validate this concept, although we have some directional data that suggests that it is not necessary to model your prospecting audience within Facebook (Meta) Advertising solely based upon your most qualified contacts.
It's worth documenting some other movement on KPI's as a part of this study, although it did not validate.
Those are:
The control list had a 41% lower cost per qualified lead generated when compared to the treatment list.The control list generated 8% less "generic email domain" leads when compared to the treatment list.The control list did have a slightly higher cost per lead generated (+5.3%) when compared to the treatment list.
Conclusion:
Our hypothesis as to "why" this is that it could simply be due to pure audience size — whereas the "control" list was 41.9% larger than that of the "Qualified Contact Lookalike" list used within the control. This manifests itself in the CPM (or "Cost per Thousand Impressions Served") metric — which was 11.9% less expensive for the larger control list when compared to the treatment.
With all of that said, this evidence suggests that it's not worth the effort to model just off of your most qualified contacts within Facebook (Meta) Advertising, at least as it pertains to our own B2B marketing campaigns. It may differ within a donor program, and is worth further experimentation in that regard.

experiments.title: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed through our welcome series

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed (from an engagement status) once they made it through the welcome series of emails to onboard them as a new subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/20/2022
Recently, we experimented with using Facebook Lead Ads for our own acquisition campaign efforts, which we discovered that we could increase email signup rates by +131.5% (LoC: 100%), but the real question was “How would these leads perform downstream.”
We marked two check-in points, which were:
To review unsubscribe, bounce, disengagement, and never-opener rates after all leads generated in the experiment have “completed the welcome series.” And,
60-days downstream to see if either lead segment produced a more engaged subscriber well after the welcome series had ended, and they were introduced to 2-months of “live messaging.”
This study is focused on the first of these two check-in points, so we wanted to review how the leads performed now that all leads in the study had enough time to make it through the welcome series of emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that we're through the "welcome series" of messaging, we could not fully validate an increase in email engagement rates, but do see a slight improvement on subscriber engagement from the treatment segment.

This is actually very encouraging news, as it indicates that there is no discernible difference between using the control lead generation experience when compared to using the Facebook Lead Ad Form treatment approach.

To put it another way: The treatment experience has increased email signup rates by +131.5% (with 100% level of confidence), but HAS NOT produced a lower quality of email subscriber (at least through the point where all leads have successfully made it through the "welcome series" for new subscribers).

Perhaps this means that the hypothesis that Facebook Lead Ad Forms generate "bad emails" is debunked — at least in our own case.

We'll continue to monitor performance and look forward to checking back in at the 60-day mark from the point of when these emails were acquired to see if there is any noticeable difference between the quality of the two approaches to generating leads.

experiments.title: How adding an image of an incentivized item to an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing an image of the special CATIS coin in the welcome series email for potential CATIS members will achieve an increase in clicks because they’ll be impressed with the coin and gain a true feeling of possessing the coin.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2022 - 12/02/2022
As part of TPPF’s welcome series, new names are sent an invitation email to join the Come and Take It Society (CATIS) by making a monthly gift. When joining, members receive exclusive perks. One perk is receiving a special CATIS coin. This specific perk is mentioned in the email, however, the email does not include an image of the coin to give potential new members an accurate, tangible impression of the coin.
We hypothesized that adding an image of the coin would further pique the interest of email recipients, giving them a preview of the special coin they would receive, and therefore encouraging them to click through the email to the CATIS donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment resulted in an 89.9% lift in clicks - a 6.3% click rate compared to 3.3% for the control. Our hypothesis proved correct: Those who saw the coin were more likely to click within the email. The coin image gave these people a preview of the coin and provoked visions of what it would be like to possess the coin. The mere mention of it can only go so far. By just mentioning it, email recipients don't know what the coin looks like and if it'd be worth essentially paying for as part of a monthly donation commitment. They could envision the coin as appearing tacky and poorly made. Showing an image of the coin promotes something tangible and takes some of the guesswork out of the person's mind.

experiments.title: How adding an offer to subscription form impacts email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free eBook for signups on the homepage will achieve an increase in email acquisition..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/30/2022 - 10/13/2022
IMB’s homepage has an email signup form at the bottom of the page. It encourages users to Sign Up Today to receive emails with transformative stories and highlights of the work the organization is doing. We hypothesized that offering a free eBook in place of this would lead to an increase in email acquisition. This was for two reasons. First, the original signup copy can at first seem a little organization-focused. Is the user just signing up for a standard weekly update? Second, we believe the free offer communicates greater value immediately by providing them with a free ebook; it also makes the implication that signing up would ensure you receive future offers like this. Value is provided immediately, but there is an emphasis on the value of becoming a subscriber.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment ran 44 days, but despite a strong number of conversions for the data, ultimately the results did not validate with confidence — at only 38.7%
Regardless, the increase in conversion rate was very slight at 1.4% for all traffic. Notably, mobile visitors did see a valid 45.5% increase at a 97% level of confidence. Ultimately, more testing is required in order to determine results.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 45.5% increase in email acquisitions with a 97% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 65.2% increase in email acquisitions with an 89% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 103.1% increase in email acquisitions with an 86% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 75.6% decrease in email acquisitions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the ‘I’m new’ button for new website visitors impacted email acquisition rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlight a specific section for new visitors will achieve a change in click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
As one of the largest Baptist churches in the country, First Baptist Dallas drives a lot of new website traffic to it’s site every day. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether highlighting the ‘I’m New’ navigation menu in the header menu for new visitors only made an impact on email sign-ups on that specific page.
Our hypothesis was that by highlighting this section, any new visitor to the site would easily find information that was more relevant to them, instead of spending time searching through the site. New visitors were also asked to fill out a form on that page to receive more information; by reducing the amount of friction to find that form, our hypothesis was that email acquisition rates would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Highlight the 'I'm New' header navigation menu for new visitors to the site led to a small, 1.7%, increase in email acquisition rates, with only a 48.8% level of confidence.

While this shows a slight directional lift in email rates, this test warrants further refinement. For example, the form where people are asked to fill in their information is actually a plan your visit (for Sunday service) form; not all of the new visitors to the homepage went there with the intention of attending a Sunday service. The form itself is also at the bottom of the 'I'm New' page; further testing would place this form higher, potentially above the fold, and make it more appealing to new visitors by offering more than just Sunday service information in exchange for an email sign-up.

experiments.title: How adding social proof affects ebook offer conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof by calling out the amount of people who have downloaded the ebook within the last month will increase conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their critical race theory ebook content offer. We wanted to test whether adding a visual element of social proof—that the ebook was downloaded more than 2,500 times in the last month—on the offer landing page would show that the resource was popular and inspire more people to download the ebook.
We added a grey box under the image of the book with the copy “This e-book was downloaded over 2,500 times last month!”
The experiment was set up as a 50/50 split test where 50% of traffic would see the control and 50% would see the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, the social proof treatment decreased conversion rate by 2.2%, but these results did not validate. This experiment is also affected by the change in headline. A more clear learning could be achieved by isolating the two treatment edits, but we can still hypothesize a learning from the result.

Why didn't the treatment produce an increase in conversion rate?
The social proof box of text might have been distracting from the value proposition copy to the left or from the form itself.
 	Social proof might have dissuaded some from wanting to "follow the herd" and join in on what lots of other people have done.
New visitors had a 5.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Internal Links visitors had a 5.5% decrease in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 17.9% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 39.4% decrease in email acquisition with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Housefile Email visitors had a 16.2% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had an 11.8% decrease in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 10.7% decrease in email acquisition with an 88% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 42.6% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 17.2% increase in email acquisition with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the “fear of loss” positioning impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling someone they’ll lose something instead of telling them what they’ll get for prospective customers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2022
As a part of the marketing campaign to promote the launch of a new online course for NextAfter, we were promoting email signups to participate in our online “training cohort” program (for a guided tour through the course to maximize course consumption and learning retention).
The day before the course was launched, we decided to write a “last chance” (to signup) email and send it the day before the course went live.
In reviewing the copy for the course promotion, we decided to test using the “fear of loss” concept to see if it would drive a higher email signup rate for the training cohort/course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we did not generate enough results to scientifically validate the lift found in the treatment. With only a 54.1% level of confidence, these results are sadly no more reliable than a "coin flip chance" of producing the same lift percentages shown.
This was due to a much smaller audience size on the email resend, which ultimately just didn't produce enough samples.
This concept should be retested in the future to see if pairing "exclusive access" and "the loss of that exclusive access" if the reader doesn't take action can actually substantially lift response rates in the future.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity into a headline affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline of a content offer will result in a higher number of email conversions
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
I Am Second is committed to helping people be authentic and genuine—both with God and with others. For this experiment, they wanted to see if they could increase the number of emails they collect on a content offer landing page. They hypothesized that a headline that gave more clarity into what the subscriber would receive would yield a higher number of emails than the control that was good, but lacked full clarity into what the reader would actually gain by downloading the resource.
experiments.keyLearnings: As hypothesized, the headline clarity resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisitions. Although greater than the control, due to the experiment not getting at least a 95% LoC or greater, we cannot say with full certainty that the treatment had a true measurable effect. In addition, we normally only like to make one change to an experiment to isolate results...and this particular experiment changed both the headline and added an additional supporting sub-headine. While one could argue that the sub-headline further aligned with the headline, providing even more clarity...it cannot be proven as this test had too many variable changed. For future tests, it would be better only to change one element, or if time/urgency is a factor, do a three-way test that has a control, and two variants to see if both of these changes actually add to the increase. It is also important to note that this organization rarely gets enough traffic actually to validate tests with true statistical significance, but even still there are learnings to be had...as shown above.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity and recency copy to a headline on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into a landing page headline, and aligning the message with recent events will result in higher email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 08/27/2022
e3 Partners equips believers to take ownership of the Great Commission. They send believers into local and global communities to evangelize the lost, and they partner with believers to establish new churches across the globe. For this experiment, e3 wanted to test adding clarity and recency copy to their headline on an email acquisition landing page. The control headline had been used for a while and had good results, but after seeing other experiments that tested tried and true content offer copy, they decided to try it on their previously good performing content offer – prayers for the overwhelmed. Since this test ran during a period of societal uncertainty with recent tragedies in the news, inflation fears, and world unrest, we felt it was a prime opportunity to adjust the headline copy to reflect these worldwide events, while still remaining generic enough to stay evergreen.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not have enough traffic to validate the experiment, we did see a 206% increase in conversions for the treatment with a 69% level of confidence. This was a very small sample size, but we did not see a negative effect on any of the channels. Due to this, we decided to role this out oas the new control. The next steps in the experiment might be to test some tactical elements, like testimonials or social proof that aligns with the recency copy.

experiments.title: How adding more value proposition to the headline and body copy affect conversions for new users
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition to the headline and body copy of a landing page will yield better conversion metrics.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/27/2022 - 08/27/2022
I Am Second is a ministry that uses film to capture the true and authentic story of individuals whose lives have been changed when they put Jesus first in their lives. For this experiment, they wanted to test if adding more value proposition into the headline and body copy of the email acquisition landing page for a popular content offer would result in a greater number of email conversions. While the control was very clear, the treatment alluded that the resource would provide a “blueprint for success”…thus potentially eliminating complexity. This came from comments from new readers that prayer life can sometimes be complicated, so we wanted to see how the treatment affected new visitors.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment had a very slight lift of just 3% with a 40% level of confidence. The results were inconclusive as the experiment did not have enough samples or LoC to validate. Since we did not see a directional lift, we cannot say if this experiment had any positive impact. As the experiment did have an adverse effect on returning visitors, future iterations of this experiment might be limited to just new visitors.

experiments.title: How the “fear of loss” positioning impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that telling someone they’ll lose something instead of telling them what they’ll get for prospective customers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/09/2022
As a part of the marketing campaign to promote the launch of a new online course for NextAfter, we were promoting email signups to participate in our online “training cohort” program (for a guided tour through the course to maximize course consumption and learning retention).
The day before the course was launched, we decided to write a “last chance” (to signup) email and send it the day before the course went live.
In reviewing the copy for the course promotion, we decided to test using the “fear of loss” concept to see if it would drive a higher email signup rate for the training cohort/course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Unfortunately, we did not generate enough results to scientifically validate the lift found in the treatment. With only a 54.1% level of confidence, these results are sadly no more reliable than a "coin flip chance" of producing the same lift percentages shown.

This was due to a much smaller audience size on the email resend, which ultimately just didn't produce enough samples.

This concept should be retested in the future to see if pairing "exclusive access" and "the loss of that exclusive access" if the reader doesn't take action can actually substantially lift response rates in the future.

experiments.title: How does removing intro language affect pledge participation for mobile visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing intro language for mobile users will achieve higher sign-up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2022 - 07/24/2022
In an effort to increase sign-up rates among mobile users who have been sent a pledge by The Heritage Foundation, we decided to test removing the intro language above the pledge to reduce friction by eliminating scroll and hopefully prompt more mobile users to complete the pledge and provide their email address.
We wondered if the introduction language was causing users to bounce because they had to scroll to see the pledge
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 90 days, we observed a slight 3.2% lift in sign-up rates, with a 74.3 level of confidence.

Despite being unable to validate our hypothesis, we were able to glean some interesting learnings from this experiment.

First, it seems that removing the intro language did not have a significant effect on sign-up rates, even though the intro language requires a user to scroll before seeing the pledge. This may be because the reduced scroll in the treatment is offset by a motivation to act caused by the problem-agitating into copy found in the control.

Second, as we drill down into the data we see that while the treatment (with intro language removed) did produce a modest lift in sign-up rates, it also produced a significant 27.9% decrease in donations (94% level of confidence) ... indicating that the inclusion of intro language that creates urgency around the issue at hand provides significant motivation for people to act downstream by making a donation.

One final metric of note is that removing the intro language did produce a 58.2% increase in email acquisition (100% level of confidence) for housefile email visitors, indicating that people already familiar with Heritage are more aware of the problem and thus require less motivation to complete the pledge.

Based on these learnings, it does not appear that there was a significant benefit to removing the intro language for mobile users in this offer. And because it produced a sizable decrease in instant donation rates, with only a slight lift in email acquisition, we can deduce that the benefits of including intro language outweigh the impact of reducing scroll to reach the pledge sooner on mobile devices.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity to a landing page headline affects sign up conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline for readers that land on this page will achieve a higher number of email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw a decrease of around a 27% for the treatment, with a 27% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and increased emphasis on the call to action text - not shown in the screenshot).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 27%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the increased emphasis on the CTA were the cause of the drop in conversions. We can say that we saw a decrease in conversions and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and headline clarity affect email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and headline clarity  for readers of a landing page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof under a landing page image and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around 2% for the treatment with a 20% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 20%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How removing an image and adding a reader-centric headline affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image and providing a reader-centric headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2022 - 06/23/2022
The Navigators were running acquisition ads to promote their 7 Tips to Help Others Grow in Christ offer. As they tried to optimize ad creative, they also looked at the landing page to see if there was anything that might be hindering conversion.
They hypothesized that the size of the image might be pushing the form and value proposition too far down the page (especially on mobile), and that removing the image might increase conversion. At the same time, they also noticed that the headline simply declared the title of the offer, and didn’t offer any value to the reader. They decided to test this as well in a “C” variant, which compounded on top of the image removal.
They split the traffic three ways to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither experiment had a material impact on conversion, regardless of device type. This showed them that the image was not hindering conversion (and removal of it didn't help), and that the new headline did not increase motivation or clarity to accept the offer.
They wanted to try a radical redesign next that dramatically altered the layout of the page to see if that would move the needle.

experiments.title: How adding a ebook offer to the homepage affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a free ebook offer for site visitors will achieve a higher email acquisition rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/20/2022
Reasons to Believe has had a generic newsletter sign up at the bottom of their homepage. In a previous experiment, we added more clarity in the language to increase sign up to their newsletter. When that did not result in a lift in email acquisition, we decided to try a more radical approach. In order to inspire more email subscriptions, we replaced the newsletter subscription with an ebook offer. Wanting to reduce friction, we embedded the actual sign up form in this content block so that the site visitor did not have to visit another page to get this offer. Once people filled out the form, they were directed to an instant donation page.
Traffic to this site was primarily organic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for nearly a month, we saw no real impact in email conversion rate. This experiment resulted in a 1% decrease in email conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 6%. This means that the user was not motivated to give more based off of an offer. Given that the form as at the very bottom of the homepage could indicate that only those who are more invested in their site (enough to scroll all the way down) were already looking to take a next step--whether that was signing up for their newsletter or getting an ebook. We saw no difference in new visitors vs returning visitors. Nor did we see a difference in donation activity.
It's suggested that we optimize the newsletter sign up form by focusing on a different aspect of the value proposition, like exclusivity. In addition to that, we can test the offer once more but making it a slide out pop up so that the user does not have to scroll all the way to the bottom of the page to take advantage of this offer.

experiments.title: How the location of the email capture impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for name and email prior to starting the survey for email subscribers will achieve increased survey completion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/18/2021
American Cornerstone Institute was launching a survey on the state of education in America. This was part of a larger campaign to promote their Little Patriots educational curriculum. The first phase included sending this survey to their email house file.
We had a theory that the position of the name and email capture may influence the likelihood of someone to complete the survey. This is something that is normally reserved to the end of the survey but, we believed that this might actually cause greater frustration and be viewed as a bait and switch. We decided to test this hypothesis with this initial launch.
experiments.keyLearnings: After collecting over 800 submissions, we found that there was no statistically significant difference when asking for the email address prior to starting a survey or at the end. This unlocks the possibility of capturing the email address prior to form submission so that we can send "abandon cart" emails for people who begin the survey but don't finish.

experiments.title: How using value focused language impacts email conversion on a pop-up
experiments.researchQuestion: Will value focused copy increase conversion on the forced pop-up?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/12/2021
Save the Children uses a forced pop-up on their website to acquire email addresses. The pop-up copy and functionality has remained the same for awhile as continued testing hasn’t been successful in improving conversion. Through this continued testing we have seen that the motivation of people converting is pretty high and that expanded value proposition does not help increase conversion, nor does a different trigger for the pop-up. Knowing this, we hypothesized that simple, value focused language might help increase conversion. To test this, we developed a treatment that said, “Get updates and stay connected”.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we weren't able to validate the results, there was no positive lift and we saw more of a negative impact than improvement. These results were consistent on mobile and desktop devices. Value focused language does not increase motivation and conversion. These results reinforce what we have learned about this audience in that they are very transactional. Continued testing is needed to press into this insight and see what levers can be pulled to improve conversion.

experiments.title: How aligning the image and value proposition for a content offer affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the image with the offer and increasing the clarity of the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2021 - 06/12/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey resource through Facebook ads. Their cost-per-name was above what they projected, and they wanted to see how they could optimize their results. A previous test had shown that an image that was more specific to the offer greatly increased conversion rate and CPA, so they wanted to apply that to the landing page as well. Additionally, they wanted to test the content on the landing page to see if increasing clarity of the offer and removing vertical space could increase conversion.
They created two treatments and ran an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither landing page made a significant difference in conversion rate. There was directional evidence that the new image provided a slight bump to conversion rate, but it wasn't near significance.
The 29% conversion rate on the landing page is still low compared to other Navigators offers, but this test suggests that the issue is upstream from the landing page—perhaps in the targeting or in the advertising.

experiments.title: Will a more clear and direct subject line lead to more course signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/21/2021
We wanted to let people know about an updated online course that was now available for free. We’ve seen how more mysterious subject lines can evoke a sense of curiosity and get more opens but we’ve also seen that a clear direct subject line can sometimes lead to more action. So we put it to the test. The control was a more mysterious subject line that said there was ‘exciting news for you’ but didn’t say anything about what it was while the treatment was clear with the fact that there was a ‘new, free course for you’. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the final result on conversion wasn't statistically significant, there was a directional lift towards the Treatment increasing conversion. If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:
Mysterious subject lines are better at generating opens
 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: How the order of a signup flow affects conversion on desktop devices
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reversing the order of login options for mobile visitors will achieve an increase in user signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2021 - 04/03/2021
CaringBridge had two options to create an account: using your email, or using single-sign-on (SSO) options like “Login with Google”, “Login with AppleID”, or “Login with Facebook”. Traditionally, these had been vertically ordered email-first, then SSO options.
The team wondered if the rising popularity of SSO options (primarily for reasons of security) would imply that these options should be listed first. They created a simple test to flip the options to see if the SSO-first approach would result in more signups and created CaringBridge sites.
The desktop version of this test showed a decrease in signups—however, the mobile showed promise. So they decided to isolate the mobile test to keep it running longer and cut their losses on desktop signups.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, this experiment failed to produce a winning treatment with any difference. While there was a small directional lift, it was not close to statistical validity. The team moved on to higher priority tests to drive more new users.

experiments.title: How testimonials from previous series viewers affect email conversion rate on an acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing the value of the offer through the words of users will lend credibility to the series, increase its perceived value, and so increase the email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2021 - 03/14/2021
Focus’s video series Raising Your Kids to Defend Their Faith had a low conversion rate. So we tested placing testimonials from parents who used the series on the page. This ran for over a month. Traffic was primarily from acquisition ads on social media.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment did not affect conversion rate with any valid level of confidence either way. However, we do not think that means testimonials are not helpful to convincing the reader to sign up. We do think that this shows that this placement and these testimonials on this offer did not move the needle for readers. Further testing of placement and copy is needed.

experiments.title: How emotional impact language on the tribute widget affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding emotional impact language for personal home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2021 - 05/04/2021
CaringBridge’s tribute widget was a primary source of donations for people who visited their journals. Over the years, they had optimized the copy to maximize conversion.
However, as they reviewed the latest control, they wondered if they could better convey the emotional impact that a journal provides for someone who is going through a health journey, and if that would translate into more gifts or more revenue.
They created a treatment that made two tweaks to the copy. First, they changed the headline to include the impact, as well as the result. Instead of just “Help [FirstName] Stay Connected To Family and Friends”, they added the effect: “Help [FirstName] Experience the Comfort of Staying Connected To Family and Friends”. Then, in the body copy, they inserted language that referred to the potential feeling of loss the author might feel without Caringbridge: “A $30 donation to CaringBridge means that [FirstName] won’t lose this special connection to family and friends.
Then, they split traffic to see the impact of this new emotional impact language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, there was no impact on conversion rate. This suggests a few potential reasons why:
The reader might have already understood the emotional impact, and the specific callout only brought that to the surface—it wasn't missing. Any impact the emotional language might have had could have been blunted by the additional copy. The copy was ultimately not that different from the original.
Either way, this experiment failed to move the needle, and more radical approaches must be taken to improve conversion.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that being more clear with the offer in the email in the subject line for email subscribers will achieve more email course signups.
experiments.summary:

So we put it to the test. Both subject lines used personalization in the first name in the subject line.

If we go 'up the funnel' or 'down the mountain' and look at clicks, we actually observed 15.8% increase in clicks with a 91% LoC which does indicate more intent to sign up from the clean subject line compared to the mysterious subject line. And when we also see that open rate for the clear subject line was 11% lower with 100% LoC, it does seem to validate both the hypothesis that:

 	Clear subject lines are better at generating actions

experiments.title: How a form on the right side of the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: A right side form could harm conversion rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2021 - 06/04/2021
Focus’s web team wanted to roll out a new landing page format with the form on the right side of the page instead of the bottom. We wanted to assure that this did no harm to email acquisition. So we tested this on a new offer they were launching called Preparing Your Kids for the Teen Years.
experiments.keyLearnings: The majority of the traffic and conversions were on a mobile device. This test showed no results because on a mobile device it is almost exactly the same user experience. So we are going to retest this format on a high traffic page to only desktop users.
